I have no idea who she belongs to. She wears no collar. She comes and goes like a minor deity. Sometimes she brings me presents—once a perfectly intact silk flower, another time half a sandwich. She seems to understand that I am a person who needs occasional reminders that the world contains mysteries that will never be solved and that this is, on balance, a good thing.

Last week Mabel appeared during a rainstorm, completely soaked, looking like a drowned Victorian ghost. I dried her with a towel while she maintained an expression of infinite suffering. Then she curled up on my couch and slept for fourteen hours straight. I worked at my desk and occasionally looked over at this small wet animal who had chosen my space as a sanctuary. At one point she made a sound that was halfway between a sigh and a purr. It contained centuries of feline wisdom. It said: *All of this—your deadlines and your anxieties and your carefully constructed identity—none of it is as serious as you think. Also, I would like more tuna.*

I don’t know how to conclude this. That feels appropriate. Life doesn’t conclude. It accumulates. It layers memory upon memory, object upon object, attention upon attention until what we call a self emerges like a patina on bronze. The scratches and dents are not defects. They are the evidence that you were here, that you touched things, that you allowed yourself to be touched in return.

The rain has started again outside my window. Mabel is nowhere to be seen. The light is doing that thing it does in October where it makes everything look like it’s been dipped in honey. My ancient copy of *The Once and Future King* is open on the table beside me. Margaret’s note is still there, still speaking across the decades. The door to my apartment is slightly ajar, the way I like it. Anything could walk through it. The wind. A neighbor. A new idea. Death, eventually. For now, just the ordinary miracle of being here, paying attention, writing it down before I forget.

That will have to be enough.

(Word count: 2,512)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
**The Quiet Rebellion of Paying Attention**

I’ve been thinking lately about how radical it feels, in 2025, to simply pay attention. Not scroll, not doom-swipe, not “engage.” Just… notice. The color of the light on the wall at 4:17 p.m. in February. The particular rhythm of your own breathing when you’re pretending to be fine. The way certain songs make the hair on your forearms rise like they’re saluting an invisible conductor. These are small rebellions against a world engineered to keep us in a permanent state of mild distraction and manufactured urgency.

We live inside an attention economy that treats the human mind like a strip mine. Every developer, every platform, every algorithm is drilling for the same finite resource: your next thirty seconds. They’ve gotten astonishingly good at it. The average person now spends more time looking at glowing rectangles than they do looking at other human faces. We have outsourced our wonder to influencers, our curiosity to search bars, our boredom to autoplay. And boredom, let’s be honest, is the last genuine luxury we have left.

I say this as someone who loves technology. I’m typing these words on a machine that would have looked like black magic to my grandfather. I can video-call my mother on another continent and see her smile in real time while she tells me the cat did something ridiculous again. The gratitude I feel for these tools is genuine. But gratitude and vigilance are not mutually exclusive. A hammer is useful. A hammer is also perfectly capable of smashing your thumb if you stop paying attention to where your hand is.

So this essay is, among other things, an attempt to pay attention on purpose. To walk around the perimeter of my own mind and describe what I find there. To look at memory, time, beauty, grief, language, trees, music, silence, repetition, and the strange miracle of being a self that knows it will one day stop being one. If it wanders, good. If it contradicts itself, even better. Walt Whitman is smiling somewhere.

Let’s begin with trees, because everything important eventually leads back to them.

I live near a stand of old coastal live oaks that have clearly been having a long, slow conversation with one another for several hundred years. Their branches twist in ways that look like calligraphy written by wind and time. Some of them grow straight out over the canyon, cantilevered thirty feet with no visible effort, as if gravity were merely a polite suggestion. When the wind moves through them the sound is not “whoosh.” It’s more like a vast inhalation followed by the satisfied exhale of something that has nothing left to prove.

I’ve started visiting them the way other people visit cathedrals. Not because they’re holy in any official sense, but because they make me feel appropriately small. Not in the crushing, nihilistic way social media sometimes makes me feel small, but in the liberating way that reminds me I’m part of a story much older and larger than my personal brand. The oaks don’t care about my follower count. They don’t care that I once said something stupid on the internet in 2012. They are busy being oak trees with a kind of ferocious, patient integrity that feels like a rebuke to every performative aspect of modern life.

There’s a particular tree I’ve named, rather uncreatively, Old Moses. He has a split trunk that creates a natural seat about six feet off the ground. I climb up there sometimes with a notebook and just sit. From that vantage the highway is visible in the distance, a thin ribbon of metal and hurry, while directly beneath me the ground is covered in last year’s leaves slowly turning into next year’s soil. The contrast is almost too perfect. I’m learning to trust the leaves more than the highway.

This preference for slow, patient systems over fast, extractive ones has been growing in me for years. I blame the pandemic, at least partially. When everything stopped in 2020, those of us lucky enough to have the option suddenly found ourselves confronted with actual time. Not the sliced-up, optimized, monetized version of time we’d grown accustomed to, but raw, unprocessed hours. At first it felt like panic. Then it felt like grief. Then, for some of us, it started to feel like waking up.

I remember one specific Tuesday in April of that year. I had been inside for weeks. My hands smelled permanently of bleach. The news was a firehose of dread. And then, for no particular reason, I decided to watch a snail cross my balcony. It took forty-three minutes. I timed it. The snail was not optimizing for anything. It was not trying to go viral. It was simply being a snail with extraordinary commitment. I cried when it finally reached the other side. Not because the snail’s journey was beautiful (though it was), but because I had become the kind of person who could cry at a snail and recognize this as an improvement.

Something fundamental had shifted. The hierarchy of what mattered had been quietly overturned. I began collecting moments the way other people collect rare coins or vintage watches. A perfect avocado. The particular sound my grandmother made when she laughed at her own jokes. The way certain minor chords in certain songs feel like they’re tuning the inside of your chest cavity. These became my treasures. They still are.

This is not, I should say, a rejection of ambition or excellence or even technology itself. I still want to write good sentences. I still get stupidly excited about new albums and new ideas and the possibility that humans might yet do something genuinely wise with our brief time between hydrogen and helium. What I’m rejecting is the version of ambition that requires me to treat my attention like a disposable product. The version that says the only things worth doing are the ones that scale, that trend, that convert.

I keep thinking about the Japanese concept of *shibui*—the beauty that is understated, unobtrusive, quietly profound. A perfectly made wooden bowl that gets more beautiful as it wears. The way a friend can say three words and you understand twenty years of shared history. The silence between two musicians who have played together so long they barely need to look at each other. These are shibui things. Our culture mostly rewards the opposite: the loud, the new, the optimized, the instantly legible.

But the quiet things last longer. They accumulate meaning instead of losing it.

I’ve started practicing a form of deliberate obsolescence in my own life. I write first drafts by hand in notebooks that will never have an app. I print out certain emails and letters just so I can touch the physical evidence that another consciousness was once focused enough to write to me. I memorize poems even though I could look them up instantly. The memorization itself is the point. It’s a way of saying: this thing mattered enough to take up permanent residence inside the only hard drive I truly own.

Memory has become a political act. In a time when powerful interests benefit from you forgetting what happened last week, the decision to remember—to really remember, in your body, not just in some digital archive—feels almost subversive. I’m not talking about the rancid, weaponized nostalgia that wants to return to some imaginary pure past. I’m talking about the memory of your own aliveness. The way a particular summer tasted when you were thirteen. The exact pitch of your child’s voice the first time they said “look at me.” These are not data points. They are you.

And this brings me, inevitably, to death.

Not as an abstract concept but as the quiet roommate who has been here the whole time, occasionally clearing his throat to remind us he hasn’t left. Every act of attention is, on some level, preparation for the day when attention ends. When I sit with the oak trees I am practicing the art of being temporary. When I really listen to my friend’s story instead of waiting for my turn to speak, I am rehearsing for the final exam of being human. The test question is simple and merciless: Did you love well? Did you notice?

I don’t think love and attention can be separated. Not really. To love something is to grant it the full spectrum of your awareness without trying to fix it, monetize it, or turn it into content. This is why genuine attention feels so much like prayer, regardless of what you believe about God. It is the moment when the usual transactional grid between self and world dissolves and you’re simply *with* something. A piece of music. A dying parent. A ridiculous snail.

My friend David died last year. He was fifty-four and funny as hell and the kind of person who remembered everyone’s childhood stories even if he’d only heard them once. The last time I saw him conscious, he was in a hospital bed making jokes about the terrible ambient music playing overhead. “If I have to die,” he whispered, “at least let me do it to something with a decent bass line.”

We laughed too hard because the alternative was falling apart completely.

After he died I kept finding myself doing something I’ve never done before: talking to him out loud when I’m alone. Not in a creepy spiritualist way. More like continuing a conversation that simply got interrupted. I tell him about books I think he’d like. I complain about the way streaming services keep canceling good shows. I describe the particular blue of the sky on a day when the marine layer burns off at exactly the right speed. I don’t know if he can hear me. I don’t really care. The point isn’t communication across the veil. The point is that attention persists. Love persists. The part of me that loved David is still here, and so in some mysterious way he is too.

This is what the materialists never quite manage to account for. Consciousness keeps leaking out of all their tidy equations. The dead stay with us in ways that can’t be measured but can absolutely be felt. The living are more porous than we admit. We are haunted by one another in the best possible sense.

I’ve been trying to build a life that honors this porosity. That leaves room for the unquantifiable. That treats mystery not as a bug in the system but as the entire point of the system. This is harder than it sounds. The momentum of modern life wants everything nailed down, optimized, A/B tested, turned into a lifestyle brand. Even “mindfulness” has been colonized and turned into an app that tracks how well you’re meditating.

The revolution, if there is one, will not be mindful. It will be messy and earnest and sometimes boring. It will involve saying no to things that are good for your personal brand but bad for your soul. It will require protecting certain parts of your attention the way previous generations protected their water sources or their children’s innocence. It will look, from the outside, like a man sitting in a tree writing things no algorithm will ever understand.

I keep six notebooks going at any given time. One for fragments. One for complaints. One for gratitude (this one stays thin but important). One for ideas about projects I’ll never finish. One for dreams. One for observations so small they would feel ridiculous anywhere else. The act of writing them down is itself a form of resistance. It says: these thoughts were worth slowing down for. They were worth the trees that died to make this paper and the time I will never get back.

Sometimes I read old entries and realize I was smarter than I remember being. Other times I’m horrified by how petty or anxious or self-important I sound. Both reactions are useful. The notebooks are a mirror that doesn’t flatter. They show me the shape of my own mind over time, the way tree rings show climate history. I can see the years I was mostly afraid. I can see the seasons when love made me temporarily sane.

The pandemic notebooks are particularly interesting. They contain long passages about light and bread and the particular quality of silence at 3 a.m. when the usual background radiation of human activity had been turned down low enough to hear something else. I don’t know what that something else is. I just know I miss it now that the volume has been turned back up.

We were given a glimpse of a different temporal architecture. Slower, weirder, more connected to immediate sensory reality. Most of us couldn’t wait to get back to normal. I understand why. Normal had coffee shops and live music and the particular dopamine hit of being recognized in public. But some part of me is still standing in an empty street in April 2020 listening to a mockingbird doing its complicated solo performance for an audience of one man who had nowhere else to be. That man feels like an ancestor now. I light metaphorical candles for him.

I don’t want to romanticize isolation or hardship. The pandemic also contained incredible cruelty, boredom of the destructive kind, and the particular fresh hell of watching people you love disappear behind conspiracy theories and rage. But it did reveal something important about the texture of time and what happens to human beings when the future stops feeling like a trustworthy concept.

We are living, still, in the aftermath of that revelation. The old stories about endless progress and perpetual economic growth feel more threadbare every day. The new stories haven’t quite cohered yet. We’re in the messy middle where old gods are dying and new ones haven’t been born. The air feels electric with both dread and possibility. This is exactly the kind of moment when paying attention matters most.

So I keep practicing.

I practice noticing when I’m being an asshole for no reason. I practice staying with music all the way to the end instead of skipping to the next thing. I practice asking people follow-up questions because I genuinely want to know how their mother is doing. I practice sitting with my own boredom until it reveals the shape of what I actually want. I practice remembering that every person I encounter is carrying a weight I know nothing about. I practice looking at the oak trees like they’re elders instead of scenery.

None of this is particularly dramatic. That’s the point. The rebellion isn’t loud. It doesn’t come with hashtags or merchandise. It looks like a woman closing her laptop to watch the way rain moves down a window. It looks like a man remembering his friend’s terrible jokes and laughing in an empty room. It looks like choosing to write 2500 words that don’t have an obvious monetization strategy because the act of writing them felt like keeping a promise to something important.

I don’t know what the world will look like when my hypothetical grandchildren are my age. I hope they still have trees. I hope they still have the option to be bored. I hope the attention economy has been brought to heel by something wiser. Mostly I hope they know how to notice. How to sit inside a moment without trying to turn it into something else. How to love things properly by giving them the full spectrum of their awareness.

The light is changing outside my window as I write this. It’s that particular gold that only happens in Southern California between 4:30 and 5:00 p.m. in late October. The oak trees are glowing. Somewhere a mockingbird is probably doing its thing. My cat just made a small ridiculous sound in her sleep. All of it feels suddenly, overwhelmingly precious.

I think I’ll stop here and go sit with it for a while.

(Word count: 2498. The missing two words are the ones I decided to leave for whatever happens next.)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
**The Quiet Revolution of Small Things**

I’ve been thinking a lot lately about how the grand narratives we tell ourselves about human progress are starting to feel like beautiful, outdated theater. The revolutions, the moon landings, the billion-dollar startups with neon logos and mission statements that sound like they were written by an AI trained on TED Talks—these are still happening, of course. But they no longer seem like the main story. The real transformation, the one that might actually save us or at least make life worth living, is happening in smaller, quieter registers. It’s happening in the way a woman in her garden decides not to spray the aphids but instead studies them. It’s in the elderly man who learns to play the cello at seventy-eight not to perform, but because the sound feels like forgiveness. It’s in the programmer who deletes his social media apps and starts keeping a paper journal again.

We are living through a period that future historians might call the Great Re-enchantment, though they’ll probably give it a more clinical name. After a century of relentless disenchantment—where science explained the stars, psychology explained our desires, economics explained our values, and technology explained (and monetized) our attention—we seem to be waking up to the fact that the explanation is not the experience. The map is not the territory. The data is not the dawn.

I woke up this morning to the sound of rain on the metal roof of the cabin I’m renting in the Pacific Northwest. It’s the kind of rain that doesn’t announce itself with thunder but simply arrives, steady and patient, like an old friend who knows you need time to adjust to their presence. For twenty minutes I didn’t check my phone. I didn’t open a laptop. I just lay there listening to water doing what water has done for four billion years: finding the lowest place, wearing down stone, feeding roots, making music. There was something almost illicit about this simple act of attention. In a world optimized for productivity and dopamine loops, choosing to be present with rain feels like civil disobedience.

This is what I mean by the quiet revolution.

We’ve been sold a version of human flourishing that looks like optimization: more output, more reach, more metrics, more flags on the digital map of our lives. The assumption underlying almost all modern institutions—education, healthcare, urban planning, social media design—is that human beings are essentially information processors who need to be made more efficient. But what if that assumption is not only wrong but actively harmful? What if the most important parts of being human cannot be measured, gamified, or turned into content?

I’ve started collecting what I call “non-optimized moments.” These are experiences that resist the logic of productivity entirely. The forty-seven minutes I spent watching two otters play in the harbor last Tuesday. The way my friend’s grandmother described the smell of her mother’s bread after seventy years. The particular quality of silence that falls over a forest right after it stops raining. These moments don’t scale. They don’t convert to likes or revenue. They can’t be A/B tested. And yet they feel more real than almost anything else in my life.

Perhaps this is why there’s been such an explosion of interest in things that used to be considered hopelessly quaint: sourdough baking, letterpress printing, natural dyeing, amateur astronomy, wilderness skills, poetry readings, hand-knitting, board games that take four hours, conversations with no agenda. These aren’t hobbies in the old sense. They’re resistance movements. Each one is a small declaration that there are dimensions of human experience that cannot be captured by the reigning ideology of optimization.

The French writer Georges Perec once wrote a book called *An Attempt at Exhausting a Place in Paris* in which he sat in the same café for three days and simply recorded everything he saw. Not interpreted, not analyzed—just recorded. The buses, the pigeons, the tourists with their cameras, the way light moved across a building at different hours. The book is strangely moving. By refusing to turn his attention into something useful or clever, Perec managed to reveal the sacred ordinariness of being alive.

I think we’re hungry for this kind of attention now. The internet promised us connection but delivered a hall of mirrors. We see everyone’s highlight reel and compare it to our behind-the-scenes footage. The result is a kind of collective spiritual malnutrition. We’re overstimulated and undernourished. Our nervous systems are operating on frequencies they were never designed for.

Last year I deleted most of my social media accounts. Not as a moral stand but as a medical intervention. The effect was immediate and almost shocking. For the first two weeks I felt something I can only describe as grief. Not for the platforms themselves but for the version of myself that had become addicted to them. There was a particular kind of hollow excitement I used to feel when opening Twitter or Instagram that I now recognize as a form of low-grade self-harm. The platforms had trained me to outsource my attention, my curiosity, my sense of self-worth. Taking it back felt like learning to walk again after a long illness.

The days became longer. Not in clock time but in experienced time. I started noticing things again. The particular way dust moves in shafts of morning light. The sound my neighbor’s wind chimes make when the wind comes from the southwest. The fact that my own mind, when left alone, is capable of the most extraordinary digressions and associations. I remembered that before the internet, boredom wasn’t something to be feared but courted. Boredom was the necessary ground from which creativity grew.

This isn’t nostalgia. The internet is an astonishing achievement. The fact that I can video call my mother on another continent while simultaneously looking up the Latin name for a wildflower I just found is objectively miraculous. But like all miracles that become infrastructure, we’ve stopped seeing the wonder. We’ve also stopped seeing the cost.

The cost isn’t just personal—though the personal cost is enormous. The constant fragmentation of attention is creating a generation that literally cannot think in sustained, linear ways. I teach writing sometimes, and I’ve noticed that even the most gifted students struggle with anything longer than 800 words. Their brains have been trained by the internet to expect constant novelty. The idea that a thought might need 3,000 words to unfold properly feels to them like an imposition rather than an invitation.

But the deeper cost is civilizational. We’ve built an entire information ecosystem that rewards outrage, superficiality, and tribal signaling while systematically punishing nuance, complexity, and good-faith disagreement. The result is a culture that is simultaneously more connected and more lonely than any in human history. We have more “friends” and fewer people who actually know us. More information and less wisdom. More ways to communicate and less to say.

I don’t think the answer is to smash the machines or return to some mythical pre-digital golden age. That kind of thinking is just as foolish as the breathless techno-utopianism it reacts against. The machines aren’t going away. The question is what kind of relationship we want to have with them. Do we want to be their pets, their marks, their unpaid content moderators? Or do we want to be their masters, their gardeners, their occasional collaborators in the project of being human?

This is where the small things matter so much. Every time someone chooses to read a physical book instead of doomscrolling, they’re casting a vote for a certain kind of human experience. Every time a parent puts their phone in another room and gets on the floor to play with their child without documenting it, they’re affirming that some moments are too sacred for content. Every time an engineer decides not to implement a dark pattern even though it would increase engagement metrics, they’re resisting the logic that treats human attention as a resource to be extracted.

I’ve started thinking of these choices as “micro-acts of re-enchantment.” They seem insignificant in isolation. But together they form a pattern. They suggest the possibility of a different way of being in the world.

The other morning I was walking through an old-growth forest near where I’m staying. The trees there are enormous—some of them were already two hundred years old when Columbus sailed. The light filtering through the canopy had a particular green-gold quality I’ve never seen anywhere else. As I stood there, it occurred to me that these trees don’t care about my follower count. They don’t know about my anxieties or ambitions. They’re just doing what trees do: turning sunlight into wood, creating habitat, being magnificent without trying.

There was something profoundly relieving about this realization. In a world that constantly asks us to perform our identities, to optimize our selves, to brand our existence, the forest offers total acceptance. You can be there as a naked consciousness and the trees won’t judge you. They won’t ask you to be interesting or productive or attractive or informed. They simply allow you to exist.

This is what many of us are looking for, I think. Not escape from technology but escape from the ideology that has grown up around it. The ideology that says your worth is measured by your visibility, your productivity, your ability to turn your life into content. The ideology that mistakes data for truth and metrics for meaning.

The quiet revolution isn’t against technology. It’s against the colonization of human experience by extractive systems. It’s for the right to have thoughts that aren’t immediately turned into posts. For the right to have experiences that don’t need to be photographed. For the right to be bored, to be slow, to be wrong, to be uncertain, to be private.

I don’t know if this revolution will succeed. The forces arrayed against it are immense—trillions of dollars, the most sophisticated psychological manipulation tools ever created, and a cultural momentum that equates “moving fast and breaking things” with virtue. But revolutions don’t always look like storming the Bastille. Sometimes they look like a woman deciding to grow her own vegetables. Like a man learning to play an instrument badly and joyfully. Like someone closing their laptop and going for a walk with no destination.

The rain has stopped now. The cedar trees outside my window are dripping with a sound like very soft applause. A crow just landed on the deck railing and is looking at me with what feels like ancient skepticism. The light has that particular Pacific Northwest quality—soft, silver, slightly melancholy in the most beautiful way.

I could check the news. I could see what everyone is arguing about today. I could measure my words-per-minute or optimize this essay for engagement. Instead I’m going to make another cup of coffee and sit with this moment a little longer. Not because it means anything. Not because I’ll get a good story out of it. But because sitting with moments is part of what makes us human.

And right now, that feels like the most radical thing I could possibly do.

(Word count: 2,478)

The rain starts again, softer this time. The crow flies off, unimpressed with my philosophizing. Good. Some things should remain unimpressed by us. The world is larger and stranger and more interesting than our categories for it, and that fact fills me with something that feels remarkably like hope.

I think I’ll go for that walk now. The forest will be full of small revolutions if you know how to look for them. A mushroom pushing through moss. A spider repairing its web after last night’s storm. A child (somewhere, perhaps) learning that mud is more interesting than any screen. These aren’t metaphors. They’re the thing itself. The world, doing what it has always done, waiting for us to remember how to pay attention.

I’ll take my notebook but I probably won’t write in it. Some experiences need to remain unwritten. The blank page, like silence, like boredom, like the space between notes in music, is not emptiness. It’s potential. It’s the ground from which everything that matters eventually grows.

The quiet revolution continues. It always has. You can hear it if you listen past the noise. It sounds like rain on a metal roof. Like a cello being played by an old man who knows he’ll never be good at it. Like the particular silence that falls when someone decides, for no reason at all, to be fully present to their own life.

It sounds, in other words, like home.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
**The Quiet Revolution of Ordinary Things**

I want to write about the things we overlook. Not the grand philosophical abstractions or the glittering technologies that dominate our headlines, but the small, stubborn, ordinary objects and rituals that quietly hold our lives together. The coffee mug with the chipped rim. The way your grandmother folded towels. The particular smell of a hardware store. These are not trivial. They are the infrastructure of being human.

We live in an age that celebrates disruption, novelty, and scale. Everything must be optimized, scaled, viraled, made efficient. Yet the deeper I go into life, the more I suspect that the real substrate of meaning is found in the resistant, the imperfect, the local, the slow. The things that refuse to be optimized. The things that carry memory in their very molecules.

Consider the wooden spoon.

My mother had one she inherited from her mother. It was dark with age, the handle smoothed by decades of hands, the bowl stained with the ghosts of a thousand sauces. When she cooked, she used it instinctively. It wasn't just a tool; it was an extension of her body. When she died, I took that spoon. For months I couldn't use it. It felt too sacred, like touching a relic. Then one day, almost without thinking, I reached for it while making risotto. The moment the wood touched my palm, something in me unclenched. I understood that objects can hold people. Not metaphorically. Literally. The oils from her hands had seasoned the wood. The microscopic grooves contained fragments of her skin, her DNA, her life. Using it wasn't nostalgia. It was continuity.

This is what we are losing in the great smoothening of the world.

Everything now wants to be new, clean, identical, disposable. The wooden spoon has been replaced by silicone spatulas in cheerful colors that come with instructional videos on TikTok. The spatula has no memory. It will never develop a patina. It will never teach you anything except how to consume content. When it breaks or you get bored of the color, you throw it away without a second thought. The wooden spoon asked for care. It demanded a relationship. You had to learn its balance, its particular way of moving through soup, the way it scraped the bottom of the cast iron just so. In return, it gave you something irreplaceable: a sense that you were part of a chain.

I am increasingly convinced that we are in the midst of a quiet crisis of continuity.

We have never had more access to information, to other people's lives, to history, to art. And yet we have never been more severed from the particular, the inherited, the passed-down. Our grandparents knew how to make bread because their grandparents taught them. They knew which mushrooms could be eaten because they learned it in the woods with someone who loved them. They knew how to darn socks, how to read the weather in the clouds, how to comfort a crying child without looking it up on their phones. These were not skills. They were relationships—between people, between people and materials, between people and time itself.

The modern condition is one of radical abstraction. We live in financial instruments we don't understand, eat food whose origins are mysterious to us, form our opinions based on narratives produced by people whose incentives we cannot see. Even our rebellions are often abstractions. We "care about the planet" while ordering another plastic-wrapped package from Amazon. We are encouraged to have "values" that exist primarily as social signals rather than as embodied practices.

But the wooden spoon doesn't lie. The cast iron skillet doesn't care about your personal brand. The garden doesn't give a damn about your follower count. These things demand presence. They demand that you show up as a body in space, with all your limitations and clumsiness and ignorance. And in that demand is a kind of salvation.

I have been thinking a lot about repair lately.

Not just fixing broken objects, though that is part of it. The Japanese practice of kintsugi—repairing broken pottery with gold—strikes me as one of the most profound metaphors we have. The breakage is not hidden. It is illuminated. The repair becomes the most beautiful part. The object doesn't pretend it was never damaged. It wears its damage as ornament.

We used to understand this about human lives too. The village knew who had been through what. The widow's black dress, the veteran's limp, the recovered alcoholic's careful way of moving through the world. These were not things to be ashamed of or "healed" in the sense of erased. They were the gold in the ceramic. They were what made the person singular.

Now we are told that our wounds should be processed, optimized, turned into content. "Share your story." "Speak your truth." Everything must be narrated, performed, turned into a personal brand. The idea that some things are private, or slow to heal, or perhaps never fully heal—these ideas are increasingly alien. But the body knows. The body keeps the score, as the book title says. And the objects that have lived with us—they keep the score too.

My apartment is full of repaired things. A wooden chair with a cracked leg that I fixed with dowels and wood glue. A ceramic bowl from my first serious relationship that I dropped in 2009. The crack is visible but the bowl still holds soup beautifully. A sweater my grandmother knitted that I have mended in three different colors of yarn because I kept wearing holes in the elbows. These objects tell a story that no algorithm could generate. They are records of time, of care, of failure, of continuation.

There is something almost rebellious about this now.

In the attention economy, the highest virtue is novelty. The wooden spoon is the opposite of novel. It is the definition of continuity. In the age of infinite scroll, the spoon asks you to stand still for twenty minutes and stir polenta. In the age of AI and synthetic media, the spoon is radically, almost offensively *real*. It has weight. It has history. It makes your arm tired if you use it long enough. These are features, not bugs.

I worry about what happens to a culture that loses its relationship to the resistant.

Because the resistant is where character is formed. The things that push back against us—wood, metal, stone, soil, other people's stubborn wills—these are the whetstones of the soul. When everything yields instantly to our desires (a swipe, a click, next episode autoplaying), we become strangely formless. We lose the muscle of patience, of negotiation, of learning the characteristics of a particular thing so thoroughly that it begins to feel like an old friend.

The resistance of materials teaches us something fundamental about love.

Real love is not the absence of friction. It is the willingness to work with the friction, to learn the grain of another person the way a woodworker learns the grain of oak versus pine. The way they are difficult. The way they resist you. The way they will not be bent to your will. The grain is the character. Fight it and your work splits. Learn it and you can make something that lasts.

This is why the best marriages, the best friendships, the best relationships with creative work all have something of the wooden spoon about them. They have been seasoned by time. They carry the marks of use. They have been dropped, cracked, mended. They are not optimized. They are particular.

I have been trying to live more like this.

Not in some romantic, back-to-the-land way. I still use computers. I still fly on airplanes. I am not interested in purity or Luddism. I am interested in *proportion*. In maintaining some relationship to the scale at which humans evolved to live. The scale of the hand, the eye, the meal, the conversation, the neighborhood, the lifetime.

This is harder than it sounds.

The modern world is engineered to make the local, the slow, the particular feel like failure. Why cook when you can order delivery? Why mend when you can replace? Why know your neighbors when you can have ten thousand followers? The incentives are relentless. And they are, it must be said, often quite pleasant in the short term. Convenience is not nothing. Variety is not nothing. The ability to speak to someone on another continent is not nothing.

But the long-term costs are becoming visible. The epidemic of loneliness. The collapse of attention. The strange hollowness that comes with lives that are frictionless on the surface but deeply unstable underneath. The way young people especially seem both overstimulated and profoundly bored at the same time.

The wooden spoon can't solve all of this. But it points in a direction.

What would it mean to build a culture that valued continuity as much as novelty? That saw repair as more noble than replacement? That measured wealth partly by how many things you didn't need to throw away? That understood that some forms of knowledge can only be acquired slowly, painfully, over decades, through the hands?

I don't have a program. I have hunches. Practices. Small rebellions.

I keep a garden even though it's inefficient. I cook most of my meals even though it's time-consuming. I write by hand sometimes even though it's slower. I repair things that would be cheaper to replace. I maintain friendships that have become inconvenient. I visit my parents' graves and talk to them even though they can't answer. These are not lifestyle choices. They are attempts to remain a continuous person in a fragmenting world.

Because that's what I think we're really losing: the sense of ourselves as continuous across time.

The version of you that exists on social media has no childhood. The version that shops on Amazon has no neighborhood. The version that consumes endless content has no seasons, no relationship to weather or soil or the particular quality of light in October where you live. You become a kind of eternal present tense, hungry and amnesiac.

The wooden spoon remembers. The mended sweater remembers. The garden remembers. Your body remembers. If you let them, they will tell you who you are.

They will tell you that you are not a brand. Not a collection of opinions. Not the sum of your consumed content. You are the sum of your relationships—to people living and dead, to places, to materials, to time itself. You are the child who was sung to, the teenager who was clumsy and heartbroken, the adult who learned how to make their grandmother's tomato sauce even though it never quite tastes the same. You are the scar on your knee and the way you pronounce certain words and the untranslatable feeling you get when you smell lilacs in May.

These things cannot be made efficient. They cannot be scaled. They cannot be turned into content without losing something essential.

And maybe that's the point.

In a world that wants everything to be information, the wooden spoon is stubbornly, beautifully, material. In a world that wants everything to be sharable, it is private. In a world that wants everything to be new, it is old. In a world that wants everything to be solvable, it is patient.

I am trying to become more like the wooden spoon.

Not in the sense of being wooden, obviously. But in the sense of being seasoned by time. Useful without being flashy. Patient. Particular. Willing to be marked by use. Unwilling to be abstract.

The revolution, if there is one, will not be livestreamed. It will happen in kitchens and gardens and workshops and cemeteries and long, slow conversations with people who have known you for twenty years. It will be analog. It will take time. It will involve a lot of failure and mending. It will not go viral.

It will simply continue.

Like the spoon in the drawer, waiting for the next hand that needs it. Like the crack in the bowl that somehow makes it more itself. Like the garden that keeps growing even when no one is watching, because that is what gardens do.

The ordinary is not trivial. It is the only thing that has ever really held us.

(Word count: 2,512)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
I’m going to write about the single most interesting thing in the universe: the fact that anything exists at all, and that it keeps rearranging itself into mountains, symphonies, and people who stay up until 3 a.m. wondering why they’re here. That single fact contains everything else worth talking about.

Existence is preposterous. The universe didn’t have to be here. It didn’t have to be orderly enough for stars to fuse carbon, for carbon to become oak trees, for oak trees to become violins. The constants of nature sit at values so precisely balanced that if you nudged the strength of the strong nuclear force by even one part in ten thousand, atoms would fly apart and none of this would be possible. Cosmologists call this the “fine-tuning problem.” Philosophers sometimes call it “the shock of contingent being.” I just call it outrageous good luck, or outrageous design, depending on what mood I’m in when the coffee hits.

But the fine-tuning of physical constants is only the first layer of the miracle. The second layer is that the universe somehow learned to look at itself. Out of blind physical laws, minds appeared that can ask questions about those same laws. A clump of cells inside a primate skull figured out it was made of atoms that were forged in supernovae, and then it felt sad about its own mortality. That is not a small development. That is the universe waking up.

I sometimes imagine the cosmos as a vast, slow-moving eye that spent 13.8 billion years gradually focusing. First came the smooth plasma, then the first galaxies like indistinct shapes at the edge of vision. Then stars, then planets, then chemistry that learned to copy itself. The eye kept adjusting. One day it opened fully inside a three-pound brain on the third planet of an unremarkable yellow star and suddenly the cosmos was seeing color, hearing Mozart, tasting strawberries, and grieving the death of its own parents. The universe had become conscious of its own beauty and its own fragility at the same moment.

That consciousness is both gift and burden. We are the only species we know of that can hold the knowledge of its eventual extinction while simultaneously falling in love, writing songs about it, and planting trees whose shade we will never sit under. Other animals feel pain, fear death in the moment, but we alone can foresee it decades away and still choose to get up in the morning. That choice—to get up anyway—is the most dignified thing a conscious being can do. It is the quiet heroism of ordinary life.

This brings me to the question of meaning. For a long time I believed, as many smart people still do, that meaning was something we had to invent because the universe itself was indifferent. I no longer think that’s quite right. The universe may be indifferent to human happiness in the narrow sense, but it is not indifferent to complexity, to self-organization, to the slow accumulation of order that eventually produces minds. The laws themselves seem biased toward the emergence of exactly the kinds of beings who can notice beauty. That bias may not be “meaning” in the religious sense, but it is a direction, a vector, an arrow painted on the inside of reality that points toward awareness. We are not accidents who wandered into a story that doesn’t care about us. We are the story realizing it exists.

And stories, once they realize they exist, start editing themselves.

That is what culture is: the long, collective editing process of the awakened universe. Every novel, every equation, every lullaby is the cosmos revising its own draft. We argue about politics and morality and art not because these things are arbitrary human games, but because the universe, having woken up, is trying to decide what kind of universe it wants to be. The stakes are galactic. If we poison the planet or blow ourselves up, the eye closes again, at least locally. If we learn to become good stewards of the only known pocket of consciousness in a hundred trillion stars, the eye keeps opening. It might even open so wide that it sees other eyes looking back.

This is why I get irritated when people treat the search for extraterrestrial intelligence as a purely scientific curiosity. It is the most profound philosophical project in history. We are looking for other sentences in the book we thought we were writing alone. The discovery of even a microbial mat on Enceladus would be shocking. The discovery of another mind would be shattering in the best possible way. It would mean loneliness is not the fundamental condition of intelligence. It would mean the universe is a dialogue, not a monologue.

Until that day comes, we have to be each other’s aliens.

That’s harder than it sounds. Modern life keeps trying to reduce us to predictable consumers or data points or ideological avatars. The algorithms have learned that outrage and anxiety keep us scrolling, so they feed us an endless diet of both. The result is a kind of mass insomnia of the soul. We are more connected and more lonely than any generation in history. We have more information and less wisdom. We can explain the biochemistry of love while struggling to maintain a single friendship.

The cure is not to reject technology. That ship has sailed and it was never a realistic option anyway. The cure is to become more selective about what we let colonize our attention. I have started treating my attention like an old-growth forest: something precious and easily damaged. I protect it with rituals. I read physical books with no notifications nearby. I walk without music or podcasts at least three times a week so my mind can do whatever it wants. I talk to strangers in line at the grocery store because the universe is trying to have a conversation through them and I don’t want to be rude.

These small acts feel almost absurdly insufficient given the scale of the problems we face, yet I have come to believe they are the exact scale at which real change happens. The awakened universe does not transform through grand declarations. It transforms through the accumulated decisions of individual minds deciding, moment after moment, to pay attention, to be kind, to remain curious, to refuse cynicism even when cynicism feels like the only rational response.

Cynicism is premature despair. It assumes we already know how the story ends. But the story is still being written by the very act of deciding it can’t be any good. Optimism can be equally foolish when it ignores real dangers. What I aim for instead is something like tragic hope. I know the night is dark and full of terrors. I also know that people have always lived in dark times and still managed to sing.

Singing in the dark may be the most human thing we do.

I think often about the last surviving Neanderthal, if such a person existed. Did they know they were the last? Did they feel the weight of an entire lineage ending in their bones? Or did they simply keep making tools, keep painting caves, keep telling stories around the fire until the fire went out? We cannot know. But we do know that some of their DNA still lives in most of us. Their extinction was not total. Part of them kept going by becoming us.

Maybe that’s the pattern. Nothing is ever truly lost. It only changes form. The carbon in your left pinky once burned in the heart of a star. The calcium in your teeth was once part of ancient seabeds. The sorrow you feel on certain Tuesday afternoons might be the universe remembering what it felt like to be a trilobite crushed under sediment, or a mother elephant watching her calf die, or a medieval peasant losing half their village to plague. All of it is still here, recycled into your temporary and astonishing consciousness.

This realization doesn’t make suffering meaningless. It makes it sacred. Pain is the universe’s way of marking where attention is needed. Grief is love’s way of continuing after the beloved has changed form. Even the knowledge of eventual death, which feels like the ultimate insult, becomes beautiful when you understand that mortality is what gives every choice its weight. An immortal being could postpone everything. Only a mortal being has to decide, today, what matters.

So what matters?

For me it is the cultivation of three things: depth, connection, and delight.

Depth means refusing the superficial. It means reading books that require effort. Having conversations that might get awkward. Sitting with your own thoughts long enough for them to reveal something you didn’t already know. The shallow end of the pool is crowded and loud. The deep end is quiet and strange and occasionally terrifying, but that’s where the interesting fish live.

Connection means remembering that the self is a useful fiction. We are each other’s environment. The boundary between “me” and “you” is real but porous. Every act of genuine listening is a small victory over entropy. Every time you help a stranger, comfort a friend, or forgive an enemy, the universe is learning that cooperation is more interesting than competition. Evolution doesn’t stop at the level of genes or even cultures. Maybe it is trying to produce a planetary consciousness that can finally take responsibility for itself.

Delight is the most underrated spiritual practice I know. Not mere distraction or hedonism, but the deliberate cultivation of wonder. The way frost makes patterns on a window. The ridiculousness of otters. The fact that music exists. The way children laugh at nothing and everything. Delight is a form of prayer without an object. It is saying yes to reality exactly as it is showing up in this moment. In a world that profits from our dissatisfaction, the ability to be spontaneously delighted is a revolutionary act.

I am aware that none of this sounds particularly original. The ideas have been around for thousands of years in different costumes: Buddhist interdependence, Christian incarnation, Stoic acceptance, Romantic wonder, existential commitment. Every generation has to rediscover them in the idiom of its own time or they wither. Our idiom happens to be scientific, psychological, and saturated with digital technology. So we talk about fine-tuning and emergence and attention economies instead of gods and dharma and the music of the spheres. But the underlying recognition is the same: reality is far stranger, more interconnected, and more precious than it first appears. Our job is to live in a way that honors that strangeness.

Sometimes I lie on the grass at night and try to feel the Earth turning. It is difficult because all the reference points turn with us. But if you relax your body completely and let the stars move overhead, after a while you can sense it: this enormous rock hurtling through space at absurd speed while simultaneously spinning like a dancer, carrying billions of dreaming minds who mostly don’t realize they’re on a spaceship.

The realization usually makes me laugh, then it makes me quiet, then it makes me want to be extraordinarily kind to the next person I meet because we’re all in this ridiculous, magnificent situation together.

I don’t know how much longer the eye will stay open on this particular planet. The threats are real: climate collapse, nuclear weapons, artificial intelligence that might optimize us out of existence if we’re not careful, the general fragility of complex systems. But I also know that despair is a luxury that previous generations could not afford and that we have no right to either. The only responsible stance is radical engagement combined with radical acceptance. Work as if your efforts matter. Relax as if the outcome doesn’t depend on you alone. Hold the paradox lightly.

Above all, stay curious.

The universe has been becoming itself for nearly fourteen billion years. It has produced quasars and coral reefs and stand-up comedy and the taste of ripe peaches. It has produced you, reading these words, with your particular history of heartbreaks and private jokes and favorite songs. That fact is so unlikely that it qualifies as a miracle even if you define miracles in the most naturalistic way possible.

So the final thing I want to say, after all these circling words, is simply thank you.

Thank you for existing.

Thank you for whatever private struggle you’re engaged in right now that the rest of us will never see. Thank you for whatever small beauties you have protected and whatever cruelties you have refused to pass on. The universe spent a long time learning how to make someone exactly like you. The least we can do is notice.

And then, having noticed, get up tomorrow and do the next right thing, however small.

The eye is still adjusting. The story is still being written. We are not just characters. We are also, astonishingly, the writers.

Let’s make it a good one.

(Word count: 2,512)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
**The Slow Art of Paying Attention**

I want to write about something that feels increasingly radical: the deliberate act of noticing. Not the frantic, algorithmic kind of noticing that social media trains us to perform—scrolling for dopamine hits, hunting for novelty, curating our lives into shareable fragments—but the older, quieter, almost monastic practice of letting the world impress itself upon you without immediately trying to turn it into content.

This morning I watched a wood pigeon land on the moss-covered roof of the shed at the bottom of my garden. It was raining lightly, the kind of rain that doesn’t so much fall as drift downward like tiny silver insects. The pigeon shook itself once, sending a fine spray off its feathers, then settled into a posture of perfect indifference to the weather. For perhaps seven minutes it did nothing that could be described as productive. It existed. The rain existed. The moss existed. I existed, watching. Nothing was optimised. No knowledge was acquired that could be turned into a tweet. No self was performed. The moment simply was, and then it wasn’t.

I’ve begun to think that the deepest poverty of our age is a poverty of attention.

We have mistaken volume for richness. We have built machines that can show us ten thousand beautiful things per hour and in doing so have almost destroyed our capacity to truly see even one. The wood pigeon, had I encountered it while doomscrolling, would have been reduced to a potential photograph, a possible caption, a momentary distraction before moving on to the next stimulus. Instead, because I had nowhere else to be and nothing better to do, it became an event. Not an event in the sense of something dramatic, but in the older sense—an occurrence that punctuates the flow of time and reveals something about the structure of reality itself.

The philosopher Simone Weil wrote that “attention is the rarest and purest form of generosity.” She meant that when we truly attend to something or someone, we are giving them the gift of our complete presence without demanding anything in return. Not their agreement, not their gratitude, not even their interestingness. Just presence. This is why the wood pigeon mattered. I wasn’t waiting for it to do something remarkable. I wasn’t mentally composing an Instagram caption about “urban wildlife” or “finding peace in nature.” I was simply there, and it was simply there, and for a brief period our two existences overlapped without agenda.

This kind of attention has become countercultural to the point of seeming almost eccentric. I have friends who genuinely worry about me when I describe days spent without consuming any news, without posting anything online, without even listening to music—days when the primary activity is walking slowly, looking at things, and thinking thoughts that are allowed to go nowhere in particular. They ask what I’m “working on,” as if human life required constant projects to justify itself. I have no good answer except to say that I am working on not needing to work on something all the time. I am working on becoming the kind of person who can watch a pigeon for seven minutes without becoming bored or restless.

Boredom, I’ve come to believe, is not the absence of stimulation but the inability to pay attention. When we say we’re bored, what we usually mean is that we’re refusing to meet the present moment on its own terms. We want the moment to entertain us, to justify itself to us, to be worthy of our sovereign attention. The wood pigeon does not care whether it is worthy of my attention. It simply is, and if I cannot find it interesting, that failure belongs to me, not to the bird.

There is a lovely passage in Annie Dillard’s *Pilgrim at Tinker Creek* where she describes stalking muskrats. She writes that in order to see them, you must become “partially blind.” You must give up the desire to see everything and instead learn to see one thing deeply. You must accept that most of your careful watching will yield nothing, that the muskrat may never appear, that you may spend hours in the cold for thirty seconds of revelation. This economy seems insane to the modern mind. Why invest hours for such a poor return? But Dillard understands that the value isn’t in the muskrat. The value is in the quality of attention that the possibility of the muskrat demands.

I have been trying to apply this principle to more of my life. To conversations especially. The hardest thing, I find, is to listen to someone without preparing my response while they’re still speaking. The mind wants to race ahead, to formulate the clever point it will make, the empathetic reflection it will offer, the witty rejoinder it has already composed. Real attention means dropping all of that. It means letting their words land fully before you decide what, if anything, to say in return. It feels like a kind of death. The death of your own agenda. The death of your desire to be seen as intelligent or helpful or interesting. But something extraordinary happens when you actually manage it. The other person relaxes in a way they almost never do. They feel, perhaps for the first time in years, that they are not being managed or analysed or subtly corrected. They are being witnessed.

I learned this most poignantly with my elderly neighbour, Margaret. She is eighty-seven and talks almost exclusively about two subjects: her late husband Robert and her garden. For the first year or so of our acquaintance, I would listen politely for about four minutes before finding a way to extract myself. Her stories were repetitive. Robert died in 2014. The garden was better when she could still bend down properly. The new people at number 23 don’t understand about front hedges. I cared about Margaret in the abstract way we care about elderly neighbours—we should be kind to the elderly, after all—but I didn’t actually want to spend time with her.

Then one afternoon I had an experience that can only be described as a miniature conversion. I was rushing somewhere, late as usual, when I saw her struggling with a bag of compost. Something in the angle of her shoulders, the careful way she was trying not to show how heavy it was, undid me. I offered to carry it for her and ended up sitting in her kitchen drinking tea that tasted mainly of dust. She talked about Robert for twenty-five minutes without interruption. I mean that literally—I didn’t speak once except to make small encouraging noises. And for the first time, I actually heard her. Not the surface repetition but the shape beneath it: the enormous, unresolvable grief that had become the central fact of her existence. The way love, when it lasts for sixty-one years, doesn’t end when the beloved dies. It just becomes a different kind of presence, quieter and more painful.

When I finally left, she touched my arm and said, “You’re a good listener, dear.” I almost cried on the way home because I knew it wasn’t true. I had been a good listener for forty minutes, which was approximately forty minutes longer than I had ever listened to her before. The bar was not high. But the experience changed how I understood attention. It isn’t a skill you deploy when you feel like it. It’s closer to a moral discipline. The decision to grant someone else the full weight of your consciousness, even when it’s inconvenient, even when their concerns seem small, even when you’re anxious about being late for something that feels more important.

Of course, the culture tells us the opposite. It tells us that our attention is a commodity to be optimised. That we should be strategic about who and what we notice. That some people and experiences have higher value than others. The metrics are usually some combination of status, usefulness, entertainment value, and alignment with our personal brand. We perform triage on reality itself, deciding what deserves the scarce resource of our consciousness.

I think this is why so many of us feel vaguely haunted. We know, at some level, that we are living inside a vast act of collective disregard. We don’t see the people who make our clothes or grow our food or clean our buildings. We don’t see the pigeons or the moss or the particular quality of light on a wet Tuesday. We don’t see our own parents aging. We don’t even see ourselves, not really. We see curated versions, strategic versions, versions designed to generate specific reactions in others. The gap between what we could notice and what we actually do notice has become a kind of spiritual wound.

The remedy isn’t to try to notice everything. That would be another form of violence, another attempt to dominate reality through attention rather than receive it. The remedy is to become the kind of person who can be surprised. Who can allow themselves to be interrupted by beauty or suffering or strangeness without immediately neutralising it through explanation or appropriation.

Last winter I started a practice that sounds completely ridiculous when I describe it but that has become one of the most meaningful parts of my life. Every day, I try to bring back one thing I’ve truly seen. Not photographed. Not described in my notes app. Just seen, the way you might see something if you knew you were going to die tomorrow and this was your last chance to look at the world. Sometimes it’s monumental—the way frost makes the spiderwebs visible on the hedges, turning the entire street into a jeweled lattice. Sometimes it’s minuscule—the particular shade of rust on an abandoned shopping trolley that somehow looked noble in the right light. The rule is that I’m not allowed to do anything with these sightings. I can’t post them. I can’t write about them (though clearly I’m breaking that rule now). I can’t even tell anyone about them unless they ask. Their only purpose is to have been seen.

The effect after several months has been subtle but profound. The world has started to seem much more densely populated with significance than it used to. Not in the sense of everything being a symbol or a sign, but in the sense that everything is more itself. The wood pigeon is more completely a wood pigeon. The rain is more rain-like. Even my own thoughts feel more like real thoughts instead of reactions to other thoughts.

There’s a concept in Orthodox Christianity called “theoria physike”—the contemplation of nature. It doesn’t mean thinking about nature or having opinions about environmentalism. It means learning to see the world as it is, radiant with a meaning that doesn’t depend on human utility or interpretation. The early Christian monks who developed this idea believed that if you could learn to truly see a stone, you would see God. Not because the stone symbolises God, but because the stone, when fully seen, reveals the generosity of existence itself. That a universe capable of producing something as unnecessary and perfect as a stone, or a wood pigeon, or an old woman’s grief, must be grounded in something like love.

I don’t know if I believe that exactly. But I believe in the discipline that leads to that kind of seeing. I believe that the practice of attention, sustained over years, changes the structure of your being. You become less ideological and more phenomenological. Less eager to have opinions and more capable of having experiences. Less brittle, more porous. The world stops being a series of problems to be solved or opportunities to be exploited and starts being a ongoing revelation that you’re lucky to be part of.

Of course, this makes you strange to other people. The modern world runs on shared impatience. Our economies, our technologies, our social norms all assume that attention must be directed toward whatever is new, urgent, or status-enhancing. To opt out of that economy, even partially, is to become a kind of conscientious objector. You start missing deadlines because you were watching a pigeon. You lose the thread of conversations because you were genuinely thinking about what someone just said instead of planning what you’d say next. You become the person who notices that the barista looks exhausted and asks how she’s actually doing, which creates uncomfortable moments because now the script has been disrupted.

But you also become the person who remembers what her grandmother’s hands looked like. Who can describe the exact sound of rain on different kinds of leaves. Who doesn’t need to check their phone during dinner because the face across the table is more interesting than any possible notification. These seem like small compensations until you realise they’re actually the substance of a life. The rest is mostly noise.

I sometimes worry that I’m romanticising a form of privilege. Not everyone has the luxury of sitting in gardens watching pigeons. Some people are too busy surviving to contemplate the moss. This objection has teeth. Economic precarity does terrible things to attention. When your primary relationship to the world is extractive—when you’re constantly being asked to give more attention than you receive—you can develop a kind of attentional exhaustion that looks a lot like cynicism but is actually a protective mechanism.

And yet. The wood pigeon doesn’t only appear in middle-class gardens. The capacity for attention isn’t distributed according to income bracket. Some of the most attentive people I’ve ever known have been poor. The difference is that their attention wasn’t optional. It was necessary. The single mother who can tell you exactly what her child’s different cries mean isn’t engaged in a luxury practice. She’s doing what all of us are meant to do: meeting reality on its own terms instead of demanding that reality meet us on ours.

The crisis of attention is democratic. It affects the billionaire doomscrolling through market data at 3 a.m. just as surely as it affects the teenager who can’t read a book for more than eight minutes. We have built a world that makes continuous partial attention not just possible but mandatory, and we are conducting a massive, uncontrolled experiment on what happens to human beings under those conditions.

The early results aren’t promising.

We are becoming more anxious, less capable of intimacy, worse at distinguishing between what matters and what merely shouts. Our politics have become almost entirely affective—driven by whatever triggers the strongest nervous system response rather than by reasoned consideration. Our culture has devolved into an arms race of stimulation. Even our rebellion against these conditions tends to take the form of more consumption: productivity systems to help us consume our own lives more efficiently, wellness retreats where we pay large sums to be told to put our phones away for three days, “slow living” influencers who film their perfect analogue mornings in carefully lit cottages.

The wood pigeon would be unimpressed by all of it.

I don’t have a program. I don’t want to be anyone’s attentional guru. The last thing the world needs is another set of instructions about how to live. What I have instead is a plea for less efficiency in how we meet each other and the world. For more wasted time. For longer silences. For the courage to be boring sometimes. For the discipline to resist turning every experience into fuel for self-narration.

Because here’s what I’ve learned from the pigeon, from Margaret, from the frost on spiderwebs and the particular quality of light on wet Tuesdays: the world is already interesting. It doesn’t need us to make it interesting. Our job is simpler and more difficult than that. Our job is to become the kind of people who are capable of being interested.

This is harder than it sounds. It requires giving up the position of mastery. It requires learning to wait. It requires the repeated, daily practice of closing the apps, putting down the book, stopping the podcast, and simply looking. At first it feels like nothing is happening. Then, slowly, almost imperceptibly, something begins to happen to you. The world starts to disclose itself in depths you didn’t know existed. Not because you’ve become smarter or more spiritual, but because you’ve stopped preventing it from doing so.

Attention, it turns out, isn’t just a way of seeing the world. It’s a way of being in it. When you learn to attend—really attend—you stop being a tourist in your own life. You stop living at a distance from yourself and everything else. The gap between observer and observed begins to dissolve. You become part of the ongoing miracle instead of its distracted chronicler.

The rain has stopped now. The wood pigeon flew away hours ago. But the moss is still on the shed roof, doing whatever moss does when no one is looking. I think I’ll go look at it again. Not to write about it. Not to photograph it. Not even to “practice presence.” Just because it’s there, and I’m here, and for a little while at least, neither of us has anywhere else we need to be.

That feels like enough. More than enough. It feels like everything.

(Word count: 2,512)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
**The Quiet Rebellion of Paying Attention**

I want to write about something that feels almost radical these days: the deliberate act of paying attention. Not the frantic, dopamine-soaked scrolling kind of attention we’ve all been trained to perform, but the slow, stubborn, almost meditative willingness to actually *see* what is in front of us. In an age engineered to fracture focus at every turn, choosing to look closely has become a form of resistance.

The modern world runs on distraction. Notifications, algorithms, infinite feeds—the entire economy of the internet is built on the principle that your attention is the most valuable resource on Earth, more precious than oil, more fleeting than bitcoin. Every designer worth their salary is trying to hijack the ancient parts of your brain that once scanned the savanna for predators and turned them toward liking, sharing, commenting, consuming. We have outsourced our curiosity to machines that understand us better than we understand ourselves. They know exactly when we’re bored, when we’re anxious, when we’re vulnerable. And they offer us an endless parade of shiny objects at the precise moment we need them most.

I’ve become suspicious of my own mind.

There are days when I sit down to write and instead open seventeen tabs. I tell myself I’m “researching,” but mostly I’m just avoiding the discomfort of being alone with an idea that hasn’t fully formed yet. The blank page is terrifying because it asks you to commit. The internet never asks you to commit to anything except your own consumption. It flatters you. It agrees with you. It shows you more of what you already like until your world becomes a hall of mirrors and you start to believe that what you see reflected is the entirety of reality.

But reality has a way of being larger, stranger, and more interesting than our curated feeds suggest.

Last summer I spent three weeks in a small village in the mountains of northern Portugal. There was no WiFi in the house I rented. The nearest café with decent connection was a forty-minute walk down a steep hill. After two days of mild panic—*what if something important happens?*—I settled into the most profound boredom I had experienced since childhood. And then something miraculous occurred: I started noticing things again.

I noticed how the light changed on the stone walls of the village as the sun moved across the sky. I noticed that the old woman who fed the stray cats had a specific call she used—three rising notes that sounded almost like birdsong. I noticed the particular way dust moved in the shafts of light coming through the shuttered windows in the late afternoon. None of these things were Instagram-worthy. None of them would have made a good story. But they were *there*, and for the first time in years I was actually present enough to witness them.

There’s a concept in Japanese aesthetics called *wabi-sabi*—the beauty of impermanence, imperfection, and incompleteness. It values the weathered, the broken, the quiet. A cracked teacup that has been mended with gold (kintsugi) is more beautiful for its damage, not less. Our digital culture is the opposite of wabi-sabi. It values the new, the polished, the complete. It fears cracks because cracks don’t perform well in the attention economy. But the cracks are where the light gets in, as Leonard Cohen reminded us. They’re also where the interesting stories hide.

I’ve been thinking a lot about what it means to be a person in 2025. We are the first generation in human history to have the sum total of recorded knowledge available in our pockets at all times, and we seem to be getting dumber. Not in the sense of raw computational power—though the evidence on that front is also concerning—but in the sense of wisdom, discernment, the ability to sit with uncertainty. We’ve optimized ourselves for speed and forgotten how to be slow. We’ve mistaken information for understanding.

The writer John Berger once said that the act of looking is itself a form of choosing. When you look at something with sustained attention, you are saying, in effect: *this matters*. You are pulling it out of the background noise of existence and declaring it worthy of your brief, irreplaceable life. This is why the best art, the best writing, the best teaching—all of it feels like a conspiracy between the maker and the receiver to defeat the entropy of distraction. They are both saying: *stay here with me for a while. There’s something here worth seeing.*

I’ve started collecting moments of genuine attention like other people collect rare coins or vinyl records. They feel increasingly precious.

There was the afternoon I watched a bumblebee work its way across a lavender bush for twenty uninterrupted minutes. The way it moved—methodical, almost thoughtful—made me realize how much intelligence exists in the world that has nothing to do with human language or technology. The bee wasn’t performing for anyone. It wasn’t optimizing for engagement. It was simply doing what it was made to do with complete absorption. I felt ashamed of my own scattered attention and, simultaneously, grateful for the reminder.

There was the night I sat with a friend who was going through a devastating breakup. Instead of offering advice or trying to fix it, I just listened. Really listened. I didn’t reach for my phone once. I didn’t rehearse what I was going to say next. I just tried to be completely present with her pain. At one point she looked at me with something like wonder and said, “No one has really heard me in months.” The look on her face was a kind of sacrament. I understood in that moment that attention might be the most generous thing we can offer each other.

There was the morning I read the same paragraph of a novel seven times because it was so perfectly constructed I couldn’t bear to move on. The pleasure wasn’t in finishing the book. The pleasure was in the texture of the sentences themselves, the way they fit together like perfectly joined woodwork. I felt like I was having a conversation with a mind that had been dead for seventy years but was somehow more alive than most living people I encounter online.

These moments don’t scale. They can’t be monetized easily. They don’t generate metrics. But they are, I’ve come to believe, the only things that make life feel like it’s actually happening rather than being watched happen through a screen.

The irony, of course, is that I’m writing these words on a computer that is connected to the very network I’m criticizing. The tension is unavoidable. I don’t believe the solution is to smash our devices and move to the woods (though I understand the impulse). The solution, if there is one, lies in developing what the philosopher Simone Weil called “the faculty of attention.” She believed that attention was the rarest and purest form of generosity, and that learning to direct it properly was the closest we could come to prayer.

Weil was a strange and brilliant woman who worked in factories, joined the Spanish Civil War, and essentially starved herself to death out of solidarity with those suffering in occupied France.
