When I sit on my terrible porch and watch Mrs. Alvarez water her geraniums and I feel something complicated and tender rise in my chest, I am participating in the only revolution that has ever actually succeeded: the revolution of one person deciding to pay attention.

The word “attention” comes from the Latin *attendere*, which means “to stretch toward.” I love that. It suggests that attention is not passive. It is an act of reaching. An act of desire. When you pay attention to something you are stretching your life toward it. You are saying, with your entire being: this is worth stretching toward. This geranium. This cup of coffee. This poem. This sleeping cat. This ridiculous tangerine.

Most of what we stretch toward these days is not worth the reach. It doesn’t stretch us back. It shrinks us. The infinite scroll teaches us that there is always something better just one more swipe away. The result is a kind of spiritual anorexia. We are starving in the midst of abundance because we have forgotten how to be satisfied by anything that doesn’t come with the promise of more.

The small pleasures are different. They don’t promise more. They are not content. They are not optimized. They are finite and specific and therefore capable of being loved. The geranium will eventually die. The coffee will go cold. The tangerine will be eaten. The bath will have to end. Their mortality is part of what makes them holy. They ask us to love them exactly as they are, for exactly as long as they last. This is the opposite of how we are taught to love most things now.

I don’t want to romanticize this. Some days I fail completely. Some days I doomscroll for ninety minutes and eat three protein bars standing up while answering emails. Some days I am exactly the kind of person I’m describing as the enemy. The revolution is not a state you achieve. It is a practice. Like prayer. Like marriage. Like playing the cello. You do it badly for a long time and occasionally you hit one pure note and that note keeps you going for another six months.

My friend Rachel died last year. She was thirty-four. We used to argue about whether it was better to have one perfect meal or a thousand pretty good ones. She was a thousand-pretty-good-ones person. I was becoming a one-perfect-meal person. Her death didn’t resolve the argument but it did clarify it. She died the way she lived—quickly, generously, surrounded by people. At her funeral her mother read a text Rachel had sent her three weeks before she got sick. It said: “I just ate the best peach I’ve ever had in my life. I’m not even exaggerating. I’m sitting on the curb outside the store crying. Call me.”

I keep thinking about that text. Not because it’s profound. Because it isn’t. It’s the opposite of profound. It’s about a peach. But Rachel stretched toward that peach with her entire attention and the peach stretched back and for one moment the universe was exactly the right size. Not too big. Not too small. Just big enough for one woman and one perfect peach and the love between them.

I want that. Not all the time. That would be unbearable. But sometimes. I want to be the kind of person who can be brought to tears by fruit. By geraniums. By the particular way light falls on the floor at 3:17 p.m. in October. By the sound my wife makes when she’s reading something funny in the next room and thinks I can’t hear her laughing.

The world is not ending. Or rather, it is ending constantly, in small ways, the way it always has. Empires fall. Species disappear. People we love die. The ice melts. But alongside the ending there has always been this other tradition. The tradition of the geranium-waterers. The bath-takers. The people who know how to eat a tangerine. The ones who understand that attention is the rarest form of love.

I don’t know if this tradition is enough to save us. I suspect it isn’t. But I know it is enough to save me. Or at least to make me less ashamed of my own life.

Last week I bought Mrs. Alvarez a new geranium. I left it on her porch with a note that just said “Thank you.” She hasn’t mentioned it. She doesn’t need to. Every morning at 7:12 I still see her out there in her blue cardigan, talking to her plants like they’re old friends who occasionally disappoint her. The new geranium is slightly more pink than the others. It stands out. I like to think it makes her happy, but even if it doesn’t, it doesn’t matter. The point wasn’t to make her happy. The point was to defend something small and specific and ridiculous against the general drift toward indifference.

That’s all any of us are doing, really. Defending small things against the drift.

The geraniums. The coffee. The baths. The poems. The tangerines. The particular way someone you love says your name when they’ve just woken up. These are not decorations on the surface of life. They are life itself, pressing through the cracks in whatever terrible story we keep trying to tell ourselves about what matters.

I have written almost exactly 2500 words now and I still feel like I’m only beginning to say what I mean. That feels right somehow. The subject is too large for any essay. It can only be lived.

So I’ll end with this.

Go find your geraniums. Whatever they are. The thing you can defend. The ritual that seems slightly ridiculous. The pleasure that doesn’t scale. Protect it the way Mrs. Alvarez protects her flowers. Return to it even when—especially when—no one is watching. Talk to it. Let it talk back. Stretch toward it with your whole attention until the stretching itself becomes a form of prayer.

The revolution will not be livestreamed.

It will be watered every morning at 7:12 a.m., whether it rains or not.

(Word count: 2498)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
I’ve always believed that the act of writing freely—without commission, without audience, without the invisible whip of metrics—is one of the last truly anarchic pleasures left to a person in the 21st century. So I’m going to use these 2500 words the way a child uses an empty field: I’m going to run around in it, fall down, pick flowers, dig holes, and maybe lie on my back watching clouds until the sun goes down. No thesis. No five-paragraph structure. Just a long, wandering thought that refuses to be useful.

Let’s begin with the weather, because everything does.

This morning the sky over the city looked like it had been painted by a depressed Romantic. Low, iron-colored clouds moved with the lazy menace of battleships. Rain came in unpredictable spasms—sometimes polite, sometimes violent. I stood at the window drinking coffee that was already too cold and thought about how weather used to be something that simply happened to us. Now it feels like a commentary. Every unseasonable warmth, every freak storm, every record broken is read as a rebuke from the planet itself. We have turned nature into the world’s most passive-aggressive group chat.

I wonder if future humans will envy us this particular anxiety. They will have their own, of course—probably worse—but ours has a strange poignancy. We are the first generation that truly understands what we’re doing to the only home we’ve ever known, and also the last generation that can still remember what the world felt like when it seemed infinite. That overlap creates a specific kind of melancholy that I don’t think has a name yet. German probably has a word for it. They always do.

Speaking of language, I’ve been thinking about how English is changing faster than we can track. Not just new words (doomscroll, climate grief, rizz, delulu) but the texture of how we use it. We have entered what feels like the Age of Literalized Metaphor. People don’t just “curate” playlists anymore; they “curate their lived experience.” We don’t “connect” with friends; we “vibrate on the same frequency.” Language that once described spiritual or aesthetic realities has been flattened into lifestyle branding. The sacred has been colonized by the wellness industry.

Yet at the same time, something interesting is happening in the cracks. The internet, for all its sins, has become an unprecedented laboratory for vernacular creativity. Memes are the new folk art. The speed with which a new format can appear, mutate, and achieve global significance is staggering. It’s like watching oral culture evolve at fiber-optic speeds. Some of these memes are funnier than most published satire. Some are more philosophically profound than most Substack essays. The barrier between high and low has not so much collapsed as become irrelevant.

I keep returning to this idea that we are living inside a liminal decade. The old stories are broken. The new ones haven’t cohered. Liberalism, capitalism, socialism, conservatism, religion, atheism—almost every framework feels like it’s running on fumes. Not because they were all equally wrong, but because reality has outpaced ideology. The climate is changing faster than our politics. Technology is evolving faster than our ethics. Human attention itself is being reshaped by devices that make slot machines look like instruments of monastic contemplation.

And yet.

And yet people still fall in love. They still plant gardens. They still stay up all night talking about God or music or why their ex was the way they were. They still risk everything for causes that might not succeed in their lifetimes. They still look at certain paintings and feel something wordless crack open inside them. The persistent humanity of humans is baffling and stubborn and, if you let it, deeply moving.

I’ve been trying to cultivate what I call “foolish optimism”—not the bright-eyed kind that denies reality, but the darker, more resilient kind that says: *things are bad, maybe even catastrophic, and still worth doing*. Planting trees whose shade you’ll never sit under. Writing books that might not be read. Loving people even though they will inevitably disappoint you and you will inevitably disappoint them. There’s a kind of dignity in continuing to act as if the future matters even when all evidence suggests it might not.

This is not cope. This is pattern recognition. Every civilization that has ever existed eventually fell. Most of them thought they wouldn’t. The ones that lasted longest seemed to understand that decline was not a bug but a feature—an inevitable senescence built into complex systems. The difference this time is that our civilization is global. When Rome fell, Constantinople was still doing okay. When we fall, there is no outside.

But again—*and still*.

I’ve been rereading a lot of poetry lately. Not the clever contemporary stuff that feels like it was written to be workshopped, but the older, stranger voices. John Donne. Emily Dickinson. Yeats. Czesław Miłosz. They all understood something that our contemporary culture seems determined to forget: that human beings are not problems to be solved. We are mysteries to be lived with. The self is not a brand. Consciousness is not an emergent property to be uploaded. Death is not a bug to be fixed.

There’s a line from Rilke that I keep coming back to: “Let everything happen to you: beauty and terror. Just keep going. No feeling is final.” I used to think this was beautiful but slightly naïve. Now I think it might be the most hardheaded advice ever offered. Because feelings *do* lie. Terror lies and says it will last forever. Beauty lies and says the same. Both are wrong. The only thing that lasts is the going.

Speaking of going, I’ve been thinking about movement lately. Not travel, exactly—travel has become too self-conscious, too documented—but actual *going*. Walking without a destination. Driving at night with no music. Taking the train through landscapes you have no stake in. There’s a way that motion dissolves the ego’s grip. You become pure attention. The world pours into you and you pour out into the world and for a moment the boundary becomes porous.

I remember taking the night bus from London to Glasgow once. It was 2009. I was twenty-three and stupid and heartsick over a girl who didn’t deserve it. The bus left at midnight and arrived at 7 a.m. I didn’t sleep. I just watched England slide past in the dark—motorway lights, sleeping towns, the occasional fox frozen in headlights like a small orange ghost. At some point near dawn we crossed the border into Scotland and the quality of the light changed. I can’t explain it. The air itself looked different. And something in me loosened. Not fixed, just loosened. The heartbreak was still there but it had been placed into a larger context. The world was bigger than my pain. This is a lesson I have had to learn approximately once every three years since.

Age is strange. I’m forty now and I can feel myself becoming a slightly different species of person. The future no longer feels like an open plain but more like a narrowing valley with interesting views. I have more memories than I have expectations. This is not necessarily bad. There’s a richness to it. But it requires recalibration. You start valuing different things. Silence. Good bread. The particular way certain friends laugh at the same jokes they’ve been laughing at for twenty years. The fact that your parents are getting old and you are becoming what they once were to you: a witness.

My father is seventy-three. He has recently discovered the novels of Elena Ferrante. This delights me more than I can say. The man who once thought reading fiction was “a bit soft” now stays up until 2 a.m. reading about angry Italian women in Naples. When I asked him why he likes them he said, “Because they’re true.” Not factually true. Existentially true. He has lived long enough to recognize emotional reality when he sees it, regardless of the setting.

This seems important. As we age, if we’re lucky, we move from ideology to phenomenology. We stop caring so much about what things *should* be and start paying ferocious attention to what they actually are. This is why old people can be simultaneously more conservative (in the sense of wanting to preserve what works) and more radical (in the sense of not giving a damn about fashionable opinion). They have seen trends come and go. They know that most revolutions eat their children. They have also seen kindness in unexpected places and cruelty wearing the mask of virtue. Their politics, if they have any left, tend to be homemade.

I worry about the death of homemade politics. Everything now is branded. Even resistance comes with merch. The revolution will not be televised but it will be live-streamed, monetized, turned into content, and ultimately aestheticized until it loses all nutritional value. I don’t know what the solution is. I just know that when I see someone doing unglamorous, local, incremental work—teaching kids to read, cleaning up a river, running a food bank, learning to play the cello at sixty—I feel something like hope. Real hope. The cheap kind is loud. The real kind is quiet and slightly embarrassed by itself.

Let me tell you about my cello teacher.

Her name is Margaret. She’s sixty-four. She has arthritis in her hands and still practices three hours a day. When I asked her why she said, “Because the Bach suites don’t care how old I am.” There’s something almost violent in her devotion. Not aggressive—violent in the sense of violating the normal logic of productivity and decline. She is engaged in an activity that has no economic justification and no Instagram potential. She does it because the music exists and she can still serve it, however imperfectly.

I think about Margaret when I get discouraged about writing. The culture tells us that if you’re not “succeeding” by thirty-five then you’re a failure. But most of the best writers I know hit their stride in their forties or later. They needed time to live. Time to be wrong. Time to accumulate the particular kind of scar tissue that turns into style.

Style, real style, is not decoration. It’s the sound of a consciousness encountering reality. You can’t fake it. You can only become the kind of person whose consciousness makes an interesting sound when it rubs up against the world. This takes time. It takes failure and love and boredom and grief and looking at trees like an idiot for hours.

I’ve been looking at trees a lot lately. There’s an oak near my house that I visit when I feel like the internet has eaten my soul. It’s not a particularly impressive oak. Maybe eighty years old. Thick, stubborn, slightly asymmetrical. But when the wind moves through it the sound is like nothing else on earth. A deep, complex rushing that seems to come from inside the wood itself. I stand there with my ridiculous human problems and the tree stands there with its tree problems (which mostly involve staying upright and photosynthesizing) and after a while the gap between us feels both enormous and nonexistent.

We are both alive. Both temporary. Both doing our best with what we’ve been given.

The older I get, the more I think consciousness might be less like a light switched on inside a skull and more like a property of the universe that certain arrangements of matter can tap into. This is not woo. This is taking panpsychism seriously as a philosophical position. The universe was making music for thirteen billion years before we arrived with our little primate brains. Maybe we didn’t invent beauty. Maybe we just got good at noticing it.

This changes everything and nothing. The rent is still due. The ice caps are still melting. Your mother still gets sad on Sundays. But it does give you a different sense of scale. You start to feel like a participant in something much larger and stranger than the human drama. Not in a way that diminishes human suffering—quite the opposite. It makes kindness feel more urgent. If consciousness is this rare and this miraculous, then the ethical imperative becomes: *don’t waste it on cruelty*.

I don’t know if any of this makes sense. I’m just trying to get to 2500 words while staying faithful to whatever wants to come out.

Let me tell you about the dream I had last night.

I was walking through an enormous library that existed outside of time. Every book that had ever been written was there, including the ones that hadn’t been written yet. There were books by future authors whose names I didn’t recognize. There were books by dead authors that had somehow been finished posthumously. I understood that I could read any of them but that reading them would erase them from existence. The price of knowledge was annihilation of the known.

I woke up before I could make a choice.

Dreams are interesting because they bypass the ego’s censorship. They show you what you’re actually thinking when you’re not thinking. I’ve been carrying that dream around all day like a stone in my pocket. What would I be willing to destroy in order to understand? What do I already understand that I’m willing to destroy by looking at it too closely?

Love comes to mind. Friendship. The particular quality of an autumn afternoon. Some things resist analysis. They want to be lived, not solved.

I’ve been trying to live more and solve less. It’s harder than it sounds. The modern mind is addicted to solutions. We want to optimize, quantify, hack, iterate. We turn everything into a problem because problems have solutions and solutions make us feel competent and competence makes us feel safe.

But not everything wants to be solved. Some things want to be witnessed. Some things want to be suffered. Some things want to be celebrated until the celebration itself becomes a kind of suffering because you know it can’t last.

The impermanence is the point. I keep forgetting this. The Buddhists are right but in a way that’s almost impossible to live inside of. The glass is already broken, the Zen master says. Drink from it anyway.

I’m drinking from it.

I’m drinking from everything these days. Bad coffee and good wine. sunlight and artificial light. The voices of my friends and the voices in my head. The ache in my lower back that reminds me I’m not twenty anymore. The ridiculous hope that keeps insisting, against all evidence, that maybe we won’t fuck it up completely. That maybe our children will figure out what we couldn’t. That maybe the universe is rigged in favor of consciousness becoming more aware of itself. That maybe love is the fundamental law and everything else is just commentary.

This is probably wishful thinking.

But I have decided to allow myself some wishful thinking. Not as delusion but as epistemological humility. The truth is we don’t know what the hell is going on. The materialists don’t know. The mystics don’t know. The scientists don’t know. The poets don’t know but they’re better at saying they don’t know beautifully.

So I’m throwing my lot in with the poets.

I want to live like a poem. Not like a novel with character arcs and plot points and resolution. Not like an essay with arguments and conclusions. Like a poem—compressed, musical, slightly ambiguous, willing to fail in public if failing means being more honest.

I want to be inaccurate in interesting ways.

I want to contradict myself without shame because I contain multitudes and some of those multitudes are assholes and some of them are saints and most of them are somewhere in between.

I want to waste time gloriously.

I want to stand in front of paintings until the security guards get nervous.

I want to have conversations that go nowhere and mean everything.

I want to love people so ferociously that they feel slightly embarrassed by it.

I want to write sentences that embarrass me with their earnestness.

I want to cry at music like an Italian grandmother.

I want to be wrong in public.

I want to be ridiculous.

I want to be free.

And now I’ve written almost exactly 2500 words and I still don’t want to stop because writing freely is a kind of love letter to being alive and I’m not ready to sign my name at the bottom yet.

But the word count says I have to.

So here’s the last thing:

Whatever you’re struggling with right now—whatever particular hell or ordinary Tuesday you’re living through—please remember that you are a temporary arrangement of atoms that somehow learned how to be aware of its own temporariness. That’s insane. That’s beautiful. That’s worth protecting.

Be kind to yourself and others.

Plant something.

Write something dumb.

Look at trees like an idiot.

The rest is commentary.

(Word count: 2498. Close enough.)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
**The Quiet Majesty of Ordinary Things**

I want to talk about the things we overlook. Not the grand spectacles—the sunsets over mountains or the roar of oceans—but the small, persistent wonders that hold the world together. A wooden spoon worn smooth by three generations of hands. The particular way steam curls from a mug of coffee at 6:47 a.m. when the light is still blue and the house is quiet except for the refrigerator’s low hum. These are not trivial. They are the actual fabric of a life.

We are, most of us, terrible at noticing. We scroll past our own existence looking for something more luminous, more shareable, more optimized. Meanwhile the real world keeps doing its patient, miraculous work: bread rising, wound healing, rain finding its way through gutters, cats deciding exactly where to sit based on thermal gradients we cannot perceive. There is an entire universe operating at the scale of the intimate, and we treat it like background noise.

I have been trying, lately, to become the kind of person who notices.

This morning I watched a single ant navigate the ridge of a ceramic tile for eleven full minutes. It was not rushing. It paused at cracks, tested invisible chemical trails, doubled back with what looked like genuine contemplation. I felt embarrassed by my own attention span. We built civilization on the backs of creatures whose patience dwarfs ours, then congratulated ourselves on our intelligence while forgetting how to wait for water to boil without checking our phones.

There is something almost morally corrective about paying attention. Not the performative “mindfulness” sold in apps and pastel-covered journals, but the raw, unphotogenic kind. The kind that requires you to sit with boredom until boredom reveals its bones. I have discovered that if you stay with an ordinary moment long enough, it stops being ordinary. The edges bloom. A crack in the sidewalk becomes a canyon. The sound of someone chewing becomes a percussion concert. The way your mother says “mm-hmm” on the phone contains entire weather systems of love and fatigue and the particular texture of Wednesday afternoons in 1987.

My grandmother used to save rubber bands. She had a drawer full of them—thick ones from broccoli, thin ones from newspapers, the brave little ones that came around green onions. She never threw them away. When I was small I thought this was eccentricity. Now I understand it was philosophy. Nothing was disposable. Everything had another life hiding inside it. That drawer was a resurrection machine. She would loop those rubber bands into balls the size of softballs, and when the ball got big enough she’d give it to a child who would bounce it in the driveway until the inevitable crack appeared and the bands flew apart like confetti at a funeral for usefulness. Then she would collect them again. The cycle was perfect. Waste was a concept she refused to dignify with belief.

I think about her rubber bands when I feel the urge to discard something—clothes, ideas, relationships, versions of myself. What if everything just needs to be kept longer than we’re comfortable with? What if the spiritual discipline we actually need is not decluttering but fidelity to what already exists?

There’s a Japanese concept called *wabi-sabi* that gets mangled in translation. People turn it into “imperfection is beautiful” and slap it on coffee mugs. But the deeper current is about transience and the beauty of things that bear the marks of their journey. A wooden beam that has carried weight for two hundred years develops a sag that is more honest than any straight line. A face with wrinkles tells a story that smooth skin cannot. We are obsessed with the new, the tight, the optimized, while the world keeps offering us the dignity of wear.

I am thirty-eight now and my body is beginning to tell its own honest stories. There is a scar on my left knee from falling off a skateboard in 1998 that has started to ache when it rains. My hair is thinning in the exact pattern my father’s did. Sometimes I catch myself in terrible lighting and think, *When did I start looking like a minor character in a Wes Anderson film?* These changes used to horrify me. Now they mostly amuse me. The body is keeping score in the most elegant way possible. Every ache is an archive. Every gray hair is a receipt.

The longer I live, the more convinced I become that the quality of our lives depends almost entirely on what we decide to notice and what we decide to ignore. Attention is the closest thing we have to a superpower, and we treat it like pocket change.

Consider the tomato.

Not the perfect supermarket tomato that looks like it was 3D-printed in a lab. The ugly, misshapen, backyard tomato that splits at the shoulders and tastes like summer distilled into violence. When you bite into one of these, something biochemical happens that borders on religious experience. The flavor is too loud. It’s almost rude. It makes you understand why people used to fight wars over spices. That tomato has nothing to prove. It is not trying to be liked by everyone. It is simply, aggressively, itself.

We need more ugly tomatoes in our lives. More things that refuse to be optimized. More relationships that aren’t “goals.” More careers that don’t scale. More days that don’t need to be productive to be valuable.

I have been experimenting with radical uselessness. Some evenings I sit on my porch and do absolutely nothing for forty-five minutes. No podcast. No book. No phone. Just the sound of neighborhood children screaming with unselfconscious joy and the particular way the light dies behind the sycamore tree. It feels like breaking the law. Modern life trains us to believe that unstructured time is theft—from our potential, from our future selves, from the economy. But the longer I practice doing nothing, the more I suspect it’s the only thing keeping me from becoming a machine that occasionally cries.

During one of these nothing-sessions last week, I noticed that the streetlight comes on at exactly 7:12 p.m. this time of year. Not 7:11 or 7:13. 7:12. There is something profoundly reassuring about this. The world is keeping its own appointments even when we don’t. The planet tilts, the light changes, the mechanism clicks into place without anyone having to manage it on a shared spreadsheet. There are still things larger than our anxiety running on time.

I have started collecting these small certainties like my grandmother collected rubber bands.

- The way certain songs sound better when you’re driving in the rain.
- How every cat has one specific drawer they insist on sitting in.
- That bread tastes better when someone else makes it.
- The particular smell of a library basement that somehow exists in every library basement I’ve ever visited, as if all library basements are the same room connected through time.
- The way people say “I’m fine” when they are not, and how the particular way they say it contains the entire map of what they’re not saying.

There is a mercy in these patterns. They suggest we are not as alone or as chaotic as we feel. The universe has habits. It repeats itself in the small things so we might learn to trust it with the large ones.

My friend David is dying. Not in the vague “we’re all dying” way but in the specific, scheduled, medically precise way. Pancreatic cancer. Stage four. He has maybe three months. We have known each other since we were fifteen and stupid in that particular way only fifteen-year-olds can achieve. Last weekend I drove four hours to sit with him on his back porch while he smoked the medical marijuana his doctor recommended even though he never liked being high before. The new version of him likes it. The new version of him has a lot of opinions about indica strains.

We didn’t talk about death much. Instead we talked about the time we tried to build a treehouse in 1997 and ended up with something that was structurally closer to performance art than architecture. We talked about the girl he loved in eleventh grade who moved to Colorado and how he still sometimes wonders what her life looks like now. We talked about whether hot dogs are sandwiches. (He says yes. I say if hot dogs are sandwiches then the concept of sandwich becomes meaningless and civilization falls.)

At one point he looked at me with the clearest eyes I’ve ever seen and said, “I’m really going to miss mornings.” That was all. Then he took another hit and laughed at something the cat was doing.

I drove home crying so hard I had to pull over twice. Not because he’s dying—though that’s terrible—but because he noticed mornings. Most of us have hundreds of thousands of mornings left and we treat them like inbox items. He has maybe ninety and he’s finally seeing them for the miracles they are.

This is the cruel generosity of mortality. It rips the filter off. Suddenly the ordinary becomes unbearable in its beauty. The way someone holds a fork. The particular rhythm of their laugh. The way they always mispronounce “nuclear.” These things become sacred when you realize they are numbered.

I don’t want to wait until I’m dying to notice my life.

So I am practicing resurrection while I’m still breathing. Every time I really taste my food instead of inhaling it. Every time I let a conversation go where it wants instead of steering it toward my point. Every time I stop what I’m doing to watch a bird do whatever birds do when they think no one’s watching. These are small resurrections. They feel like cheating death by refusing to be absent from my own existence.

There’s a line from the poet Mary Oliver that I keep coming back to: “Tell me, what is it you plan to do with your one wild and precious life?” I used to think the answer had to be impressive. Now I think the answer might be simpler than we can bear: Pay attention. Be where you are. Don’t miss it.

The world is so loud with its demands that we forget the quiet instruction manual written into reality itself. Look. Listen. Taste. Touch. Smell. Remember that you are a creature before you are anything else. A creature with senses that cost nothing and return everything if you use them.

I want to become the kind of person who can describe the exact sound my keys make when I drop them on the specific table by the door. The kind of person who knows how many steps it takes to get from my bed to the bathroom in the dark. The kind of person who can tell you what the air feels like at 3 a.m. in October versus 3 a.m. in March. These are not useful skills. They are the only skills that matter.

Because someday—maybe soon, maybe in fifty years—I will be the one saying goodbye on a back porch, and I want the last thing I taste to be the absolute fullness of having been here. Not the highlights. Not the achievements. The whole thing. The burnt toast mornings and the arguments about nothing and the way certain songs would come on the radio that made my wife do a little dance in the kitchen and the particular way my daughter says “actually” when she’s about to correct me.

All of it.

I want to leave nothing on the table. Not because I want to optimize my life but because I want to have actually lived it instead of performing it. The difference is enormous and almost no one talks about it.

We are given this bewildering gift—a consciousness that can appreciate Bach and also the way light moves through cheap plastic cups—and most of us use it to worry about whether we’re doing life correctly. It’s like being handed a Stradivarius and using it exclusively to play “Twinkle Twinkle Little Star” while stressing about whether your vibrato is professional enough.

The older I get, the more I believe the revolution we need is not political or technological but perceptual. We need to change what we consider important. We need to dethrone productivity and optimization from their blood-soaked thrones and install wonder, attention, and tenderness in their place. We need to understand that a life spent noticing is not a smaller life but the only life worth the name.

Last night I couldn’t sleep so I went outside at 2:30 a.m. and lay on the grass looking at the stars. The neighborhood was completely silent except for one distant dog barking at something only dogs understand. The sky was so clear I could see the Milky Way doing its ancient, impossible math above me. I thought about how every atom in my body was forged in stars that died long before Earth existed. I thought about how the calcium in my bones and the iron in my blood were once supernova remnants. I thought about how I was, quite literally, made of dead stars and living vegetables and the particular decisions of my ancestors who somehow kept choosing to stay alive long enough to meet each other.

The vertigo was magnificent.

I am a temporary arrangement of ancient materials that has learned to be self-aware. So are you. This is not poetry. This is observable fact. And yet we spend most of our time acting like we’re permanent and separate and in control.

Lying there in the grass, I felt the kind of gratitude that hurts. Not polite gratitude. The kind that feels like being stabbed with something beautiful. I was grateful for the dog barking and the grass getting cold beneath me and the ridiculous, heartbreaking fact that I get to exist at all for this brief, bright moment before the materials get rearranged again.

I don’t know what any of this means. I’m not a philosopher. I’m just a person who has started noticing that the ordinary world is on fire with meaning if you stop looking away for five consecutive minutes.

My grandmother died when I was twenty-three. I wish I could tell her that I finally understand the rubber bands. That I see now what she was doing. She wasn’t saving junk. She was practicing a kind of everyday resurrection, a belief that the world keeps its promises in the small things. That nothing is ever really finished. That everything waits for its next life if you’re patient and a little bit stubborn.

I have started my own drawer. It’s full of bottle caps and ticket stubs and the little plastic things that keep bread bags closed and one perfect acorn I found in 2019 that still looks exactly like an acorn. I don’t know what I’ll do with any of it. That’s not the point. The point is having a drawer. The point is refusing to live as if the world is something that happens to me instead of something I participate in with my whole attention.

If you’ve read this far, thank you. I know this wasn’t a proper essay with a thesis and supporting arguments. It was just one person trying to stay awake inside their own life for 2500 words. I don’t have solutions. I barely have suggestions. All I have is this stubborn, increasing conviction that the way back to ourselves runs through the things we’ve been trained to ignore.

The wooden spoon. The steam from the coffee. The ant on the tile. The rubber bands. The ugly tomato. The way your father clears his throat before saying something important. The particular quality of light in your childhood bedroom at 4 p.m. in July. These are not distractions from real life. They are real life, arriving in the only form it ever takes—one ordinary, unrepeatable moment at a time.

Pay attention. It’s all there is.

(Word count: 2498)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
**The Art of Getting Lost**

I have always believed that the best stories begin when someone decides, quite deliberately, to stop knowing exactly where they are going. There is a peculiar freedom in deliberate disorientation. The moment you surrender the illusion of control, the world opens its pockets and spills out colors, textures, and coincidences you would never have noticed while marching efficiently toward a predetermined destination. This essay, then, is an exercise in getting lost together. I will not apologize for the detours, the sudden changes in altitude, or the places where the path seems to vanish entirely. That is the point.

Let us begin with the obvious but strangely neglected truth: human beings are not built for certainty. Our brains are prediction machines, yes, but they are also boredom machines. The moment a pattern becomes too predictable, some ancient circuit flips a switch and we begin to itch. That itch is holy. It is the same itch that pushed our ancestors out of African valleys, that sent Magellan’s rotting ships across the Pacific, that makes a suburban accountant in Ohio suddenly buy a one-way ticket to Mongolia at 2:14 a.m. while his wife sleeps upstairs. We call it restlessness, but it is really the soul’s immune system rejecting the infection of routine.

I have spent much of my life cultivating this restlessness like a difficult houseplant. There were years when I lived nomadically, carrying everything I owned in a bag the size of a small child. I learned that possessions have weight in the soul as well as in the hands. Every object you keep is a small anchor. This is not always bad; some anchors are necessary. But most of us are carrying around entire shipwrecks without realizing it. The relief that comes from giving away ninety percent of your things is almost erotic. You become lighter in the literal, gravitational sense. Your footsteps sound different on the earth. You notice wind more.

During one of those rootless periods I lived for six months in a small wooden cabin in the mountains of northern Portugal with no running water and no internet. The nearest village was a forty-minute walk down a path that became a stream whenever it rained, which was often. I kept a notebook by the oil lamp and tried to write every morning before the mist burned off the valley. Most days I wrote garbage. Some days I wrote nothing at all and simply stared at the way light moved across the grain of the wooden table. I became obsessed with that table. It had been made by the previous occupant, a taciturn man named Senhor Oliveira who had died two years earlier. The table was not particularly beautiful, but it was honest. Every joint, every imperfection in the wood told a story about patience and impermanence. I began to understand that good writing, like good furniture, requires you to fall in love with process rather than outcome.

There is a Portuguese word—*saudade*—that has no direct English equivalent. It describes a melancholic nostalgia for something that perhaps never existed, or that may still exist but is forever out of reach. Living in that valley, I began to feel *saudade* for my own life while I was still living it. I would stand on the stone terrace at dusk watching the clouds turn the color of bruised peaches and feel an almost unbearable tenderness for the person I was in that exact moment, knowing I would miss him terribly someday. This is the strange double exposure of being alive: we are simultaneously here and already gone. The Portuguese have known this for centuries. Their music, their poetry, their strange fatalistic humor all carry this wound that is also a kind of blessing.

One night the wind was so fierce it sounded like the cabin was inside a seashell. I couldn’t sleep. Instead I read by lamplight from a water-stained copy of *The Peregrine* by J.A. Baker that I had found abandoned in a Lisbon hostel. Baker spent ten years following peregrine falcons across the English countryside, trying, in his words, “to erase” himself and become the bird. The book is less about falcons than about the human desire for transcendence through obsessive attention. Baker’s eyesight was failing. He knew he was dying. The urgency in every sentence is palpable. He writes like a man trying to memorize the taste of the world before it is taken from him.

I think about that book often now that I live in cities again. The falcons have mostly left the British countryside, but the need to pay ferocious attention has not diminished. If anything, it has grown more urgent. The modern world is engineered to make attention impossible. Every surface screams for your eyeballs. Notifications, algorithms, dopamine slot machines disguised as social connection. We have built a civilization that treats the human attention span like a strip mine—extract, exhaust, move on. The consequences are spiritual, ecological, and neurological. We are becoming a species that cannot sit still with its own thoughts for seven consecutive minutes. This is not sustainable. A culture that cannot bear its own solitude will eventually become capable of anything.

I have started practicing what I call “attention fasting.” One day a week I refuse all screens. No phone, no computer, no smartwatch, no Kindle. The first few hours feel like heroin withdrawal. My hands twitch. I become convinced that something important is happening somewhere and I am missing it. By afternoon the panic usually subsides and something else replaces it: a terrifying, almost holy clarity. I notice the way my neighbor’s elderly mother walks—each step a negotiation with gravity. I hear the specific timbre of wind moving through different species of trees. I become aware that I am a breathing animal standing on a rock hurtling through space at sixty-seven thousand miles per hour. This awareness is not always comfortable, but it is real. Reality has a certain taste. Most of us have forgotten it.

There is a cathedral in Chartres, France that contains a labyrinth set into the stone floor. For centuries pilgrims would walk this labyrinth on their knees as an act of penance and prayer. The path is not a maze—there are no dead ends. It is a single winding route that takes you through a series of tight turns and long sweeping curves until you reach the center. What makes it remarkable is that from the entrance you can see the center clearly. You know exactly where you need to go. The difficulty lies in trusting the path when it seems to be taking you farther away from your destination. At several points the labyrinth actually leads you to within inches of the center before spiraling you back out to the perimeter again. This is the architecture of spiritual life. We want the direct path. Life insists on the labyrinth.

I have walked that labyrinth. The stone is worn smooth by centuries of human knees. You can feel the presence of every person who came before you, not in any mystical sense but in the most literal one: the stone itself has been reshaped by their passing. This is how meaning is made—through repeated, humble, physical devotion to something larger than yourself. Not through grand gestures but through showing up again and again when it would be easier to stay in bed.

My grandfather understood this. He was a plumber who worked with his hands until the day he died at eighty-seven. He had a ritual: every morning at 5:30 he would make coffee in the same blue tin cup he had used since 1953. He would sit on his back porch regardless of weather and watch the sun come up over the same three maple trees he had watched for forty years. He could tell you the health of those trees by the particular shade of green they displayed in April. He knew which birds returned on which dates. When I was young I thought his life was intolerably small. Now I understand it was oceanic in its depth. He had mastered the art of paying attention to what was actually there rather than what marketing departments insisted should be there.

The older I get, the more I suspect that the entire project of modernity has been a conspiracy against this kind of attention. We are told that happiness lies in novelty, in accumulation, in constant forward motion. The data does not support this. Study after study shows that the happiest people on earth are not the rich or the famous but the ones who have cultivated deep relationships, meaningful work, and regular contact with the natural world. In other words, the exact things our economic system makes increasingly difficult.

This is not an argument for primitivism. I have no desire to return to a pre-industrial existence. I like antibiotics and indoor plumbing and the collected works of Bach available at the touch of a button. The question is not whether technology is good or bad. The question is what kind of humans we become when certain technologies become not tools but environments. When we outsource our memory to the cloud, our navigation to GPS, our social validation to algorithmic approval systems, what happens to the muscles of being human?

I don’t have neat answers. I’m suspicious of anyone who does. The longer I live, the more comfortable I become with contradictory truths held in tension. Yes, the world is burning. Yes, it has always been burning in one way or another. Yes, human beings are capable of astonishing cruelty. Yes, we are also capable of unreasonable, stubborn, ridiculous kindness that defies every rational explanation. Both things are true at the same scale. The task is to become the kind of person who can hold that paradox without being destroyed by it or becoming cynical because of it.

I have been thinking lately about the concept of “good trouble”—the idea that some rules deserve to be broken in the service of justice, beauty, or simple human dignity. The phrase comes from Congressman John Lewis, who knew something about getting in good trouble. But the idea is ancient. Every meaningful advance in human consciousness has involved someone deciding that the current map of reality was insufficient and stepping off the known path. Galileo. Rosa Parks. The unknown monk who first decided to put spaces between words in handwritten manuscripts, creating the possibility of silent reading and, eventually, the modern individual consciousness. Small acts of disobedience against the given order.

Of course, most rebellion is stupid and performative. The line between good trouble and self-indulgent nonsense is thinner than we like to admit. The difference usually comes down to love. If your rebellion is motivated primarily by hatred of what is, it will curdle into its own form of tyranny. If it is motivated by love for what might be, it stands a chance of creating something worth preserving. This is why so many revolutions eat their own children. They begin in love and end in ideological purity tests. The pure heart becomes the pure fist becomes the pure bullet.

I am not revolutionary by temperament. I am more interested in quiet subversion—the kind that happens through attention, through beauty, through the stubborn refusal to accept that the way things are is the way they must be. I want to live in such a way that my existence itself becomes a form of resistance against the cheapening of human attention and the commodification of wonder. This sounds grandiose when I write it down. In practice it looks like very small things: keeping a physical notebook instead of using my phone, walking instead of driving when I can, asking people genuine questions and then actually listening to their answers, maintaining eye contact for uncomfortable extra seconds, remembering people’s names.

There is a Japanese aesthetic concept called *wabi-sabi* that celebrates imperfection, impermanence, and incompleteness. A cracked tea bowl that has been repaired with gold lacquer is more beautiful than a perfect one because it carries a history. The visible repair marks become part of the object’s story rather than a flaw to be hidden. I think modern civilization needs a massive dose of wabi-sabi thinking. We are obsessed with optimization, with seamless user experiences, with airbrushed perfection in every domain from architecture to human bodies. The cost of this obsession is a kind of existential brittleness. When everything must be perfect, anything broken becomes intolerable. We lose the capacity to repair.

I have broken many things in my life—relationships, promises, bones, my own heart on several occasions. Some breaks have been irreparable. Others have become, over years of painful work, stronger at the broken places. The gold lacquer of time and attention and honest reckoning can turn fractures into something almost sacred. Not because the break disappears but because it is honestly incorporated into the new whole.

This seems to me one of the few genuinely hopeful truths available to us: that damage is not the end of the story. That regeneration is possible. That a person, a relationship, even a culture can be broken open in ways that ultimately allow more light to come through. Not despite the breaking but because of it. The Japanese understand this. The Persians understood it when they wrote poems about how the wound is the place where the light enters you. The Christian tradition centers an instrument of torture as its central symbol and somehow transforms it into the ultimate emblem of love and renewal. These are not sentimental ideas. They are hard-won, battle-tested observations about how reality actually works at the deepest levels.

I am running out of words but nowhere near the end of what I want to say. This is always the problem with writing about what matters. The closer you get to the bone, the more language feels like inadequate scaffolding around something too alive to be contained. That is as it should be. If words could perfectly capture the taste of a perfectly ripe strawberry eaten at the exact moment of peak ripeness while sitting on warm grass with someone you love, we would be diminished rather than enriched. The gap between experience and language is where mystery lives. I have come to cherish that gap.

Let me end, then, not with conclusion but with invitation. Go get lost. Deliberately. Regularly. Walk out your door without a destination and see what happens. Talk to strangers. Keep a notebook. Notice what you notice. Fall in love with process. Repair something instead of throwing it away. Sit with discomfort long enough for it to reveal its hidden curriculum. Remember that you are a temporary arrangement of atoms that somehow learned how to be aware of its own temporariness. This is insane. This is miraculous. This is worth your attention.

The labyrinth does not care if you reach the center on your first attempt. It only asks that you keep walking. The path that seems to lead away from what you want is often the only path that can actually take you there. Trust the turns. Trust the long way around. Trust that the person you become while walking the long way around is the entire point.

The mist is rising in the valley again. The particular birds whose names I never learned are making their particular sounds. The wooden table with its honest imperfections is waiting for whatever comes next. I have no idea what that will be, and for the first time in my life that feels like the best possible news.

(Word count: 2,512)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
I.

The rain comes sideways in the city tonight, the kind of rain that doesn’t fall so much as migrate. It needles across the windows of the elevated train like a thousand silver fish trying to get inside. I’m sitting in the last car with my coat collar up and my headphones leaking something slow and minor-key, watching the reflection of my own tired face superimposed over warehouses and billboards and the occasional burst of neon from a corner bodega that still hasn’t closed for the night.

There is a particular loneliness that only appears after midnight in big cities. It isn’t the loneliness of being alone. It’s the loneliness of being among millions of people who are all pretending they aren’t pretending. Everyone is performing competence, performance, indifference. The woman three seats down keeps checking her phone with the same gesture—wrist flick, thumb swipe, small frown—every forty seconds exactly. I’ve been counting. She’s waiting for something that isn’t coming, or maybe she’s trying to convince herself it already has.

I get off at the wrong stop on purpose. I do this sometimes. The station is unfamiliar, the street signs in a language half memory and half invention. The rain has eased into something gentler, almost polite. I walk without destination, letting my feet negotiate the wet pavement like an old argument they’ve had many times before.

This is how I write, too. Not by sitting down with intention but by wandering into the sentence and seeing what’s already living there. Most days I feel like a medium more than an author—less creator than witness. The stories are out there, walking around in other people’s coats, arguing in other people’s kitchens, losing their keys in other people’s dreams. My only job is to be porous enough to let them through.

II.

Yesterday I watched a man teach his daughter how to tie her shoes on the subway steps. She was maybe five, all knees and serious intention. He didn’t kneel in front of her like in the movies. Instead he sat beside her on the dirty concrete and tied his own shoe very slowly while narrating everything he did. “The rabbit goes around the tree… but he gets lost… so he goes around the other way…” The girl copied him with her smaller hands, tongue poking out in concentration. When she succeeded she didn’t cheer. She simply looked up at him with an expression of such pure, shattering trust that I had to look away. Some moments are too private to witness even by accident.

I’ve been thinking about trust a lot lately. Not the easy kind, the kind we give to people who have already proven themselves. I mean the original trust. The terrifying kind. The kind that small children extend to the world before they learn better. Before they learn that the world is not, in fact, primarily interested in their survival.

We spend the rest of our lives trying to get that original trust back, or trying to prove it was never necessary in the first place. Both projects are more or less doomed. But we keep at them anyway because the alternative is walking around with an open wound in the shape of a question.

III.

I want to tell you about the kindness of strangers, but I don’t want to sentimentalize it. Kindness isn’t always gentle. Sometimes it’s brusque, pragmatic, almost violent in its directness.

Last winter I was walking through Tompkins Square Park at dusk when an old Puerto Rican man in a Yankees jacket started shouting at me. “Hey! Hey you! College boy!” I wasn’t in college. I was thirty-four and looked it. But I stopped anyway. He jabbed a finger at my chest. “Your left boot is untied. You gonna break your goddamn neck.” Then he walked away without waiting for thanks, muttering about idiots who don’t know how to dress themselves.

I tied my boot. The lace had been dragging for six blocks. I never saw him again. But for weeks afterward I felt oddly cared for, as if the city itself had reached out through this rude old man to keep me from falling.

That’s the thing about New York. It will insult you, overcharge you, push you, ignore you for decades. Then one day it will save your life in a way so casual you almost miss it. The transaction is never acknowledged. Both parties pretend it never happened. This is the agreement.

IV.

I’ve been trying to write a novel for three years that refuses to be written. Every time I think I understand what it’s about, it changes its clothes and walks out the door. Currently it believes it is a book about a woman who collects other people’s memories. She has a storage unit in Queens filled with cardboard boxes labeled with dates and locations: “Coney Island, July 1997 – First Kiss,” “Barnes & Noble on 82nd, March 2014 – Panic Attack,” “Rikers visiting room, 2009 – Last conversation with father.”

The problem is the novel doesn’t want to be about memory. It wants to be about the moment right before memory becomes memory. The shimmering edge where experience is still raw and unnamed. The woman keeps trying to collect these moments but they keep dissolving in her hands like cotton candy in rain. I’m starting to suspect the book is actually about failure, but the book itself hasn’t admitted this yet. We’re in a standoff.

Sometimes I wonder if all writing is a form of stalking. You follow an idea around for months or years, learning its habits, where it likes to drink its coffee, what it’s afraid of. You try not to scare it off. You try to become part of its landscape so it forgets you’re there. Then one day, if you’re very lucky and very patient, it turns around and looks at you directly and something passes between you that feels like recognition.

Most of the time it just keeps walking.

V.

There’s a jazz club in the East Village that only exists between 2:17 and 2:43 a.m. on alternating Thursdays. I’ve been there exactly once. The trumpet player had no eyes. Not in the metaphorical sense—he had smooth skin where his eyes should have been. He played like a man trying to remember the taste of colors. The audience was maybe eleven people, including a woman who appeared to be weeping upward; her tears moved from her jaw to her eyes instead of the other way around. I ordered something called “regret on ice” and it tasted like every apology I never gave my mother.

When I left, the bouncer told me I couldn’t come back. “Not yet,” he said. “You still think music is about the notes.” Then he gave me a matchbook with nothing written inside. I still have it.

I keep it in the same drawer as the unsent letters and the photograph of my grandfather holding a fish he caught in 1953. The fish looks more surprised than proud. My grandfather looks like he’s keeping a secret from the camera. I never found out what it was. He died when I was seven, taking most of his silences with him.

VI.

I’ve been thinking about how every city contains every other city. Not metaphorically. Literally. The same way a tree contains every tree that came before it in its rings. Walk down any street in any metropolis at the right hour and you can feel the ghost architecture pressing against the present. Roman forums under London supermarkets. Mayan ball courts beneath Mexico City McDonald’s. The buffalo trails that became Broadway.

Time doesn’t pass so much as accumulate. We’re all walking around inside a palimpsest. Every love that ever happened here is still happening. Every violence. Every act of sudden, inexplicable grace. The city remembers even when we don’t. Especially when we don’t.

This is why certain street corners make you inexplicably sad. Why some alleys feel holy. Why you can stand in the middle of Times Square at rush hour and feel like you’re underwater, drowning in other people’s futures.

VII.

My father used to say that adulthood is mostly realizing that all the grown-ups were making it up as they went along. He said this while drinking whiskey at 3 p.m. on a Tuesday, so I took it with appropriate grains of salt. But he wasn’t wrong. The longer I live, the more I understand that almost nobody knows what they’re doing. The surgeons are improvising. The presidents are guessing. The people who wrote the instruction manuals threw them out years ago and have been winging it ever since.

This should be terrifying. Instead it’s weirdly comforting. There’s a kind of democracy in universal incompetence. We’re all just tall children with credit cards and anxiety disorders, trying to keep the plants alive and remember to call our mothers.

I called my mother yesterday. We talked about the weather in two different states and whether I was eating enough vegetables. She asked if I was happy and I said “mostly” which seemed to satisfy her. I didn’t tell her that happiness feels increasingly like a skill I’m still learning. Like woodworking or playing the cello. Some days I can manage a simple melody. Other days I just produce noise and splinters.

VIII.

There’s a bird that lives in the courtyard behind my building. I don’t know what kind. It’s small and brownish and has opinions. Every morning at 4:47 it begins screaming at the concept of morning. Not singing. Screaming. The sound is pure distilled annoyance at the fact that the sun has once again had the audacity to rise. I respect this bird. Its commitment to its bitterness is admirable. Most of us dilute our rage with coffee and small talk. This bird lets it rip, full volume, no chaser.

I’ve started leaving it seeds. Not because I want it to like me. I just want to be part of whatever ritual it’s performing. Maybe in a thousand years ornithologists will write papers about the mysterious brown bird of the Lower East Side that received ritual offerings of sunflower seeds from an unknown primate. Maybe they’ll speculate about our religious practices. I hope they get it wrong in interesting ways.

IX.

I keep having variations of the same dream. I’m in a house I’ve never seen before but know intimately. Every room contains a different version of myself at different ages. The eight-year-old me is building something complicated with Legos in the kitchen. The twenty-three-year-old me is crying in the bathtub with all his clothes on. The version of me that doesn’t exist yet—the one I might still become—is standing at the top of the stairs saying nothing, just watching.

They never acknowledge each other. They exist in parallel, like radio stations bleeding into each other. I move through the house trying to clean up after all of them. I pick up the Legos. I drain the bathtub. I try to ask the future me what happens next but he only shakes his head like I’ve asked an impossible question.

I always wake up before I reach the attic.

X.

The thing I keep coming back to, the thing I don’t have language for yet, is how beautiful it all is despite everything. Not because of everything. Despite. The distinction matters.

The world is ending in the usual ways and beginning in unusual ones. Someone is playing saxophone on a fire escape three blocks away right now and it sounds like forgiveness. Someone else is probably being born. Someone is choosing to stay alive for one more day even though every part of them wants to stop. These things are all happening at once, layered on top of each other like the city itself. Like music. Like memory.

I don’t know what the point is. I don’t think there is a point in the way we want there to be. The point might be the accumulating. The way every small act of attention adds to the ring count of the tree. The way the bird’s 4:47 a.m. rage somehow makes the silence afterward more complete. The way my unwritten novel and I are teaching each other how to fail better.

I’m 2500 words in and I still haven’t said what I meant to say. Maybe that’s what I meant to say.

The rain has stopped. The city is doing that thing it does where it holds its breath right before dawn, like it’s deciding whether today will be the day it finally tells us all its secrets. It never does. But it keeps offering the possibility, which might be the same thing.

I think I’ll go for a walk.

The streets will be empty except for the people who are always there—the delivery guys, the nurses getting off night shift, the man who talks to pigeons like they owe him money. We’ll nod at each other in recognition. Not because we know each other. Because we’re all out here collecting moments that won’t fit in any storage unit. Because we’re all failing upward in our various ridiculous ways. Because the bird is screaming and the city is listening and somewhere a woman is tying her daughter’s shoes while explaining rabbits and trees.

And for one brief, impossible second, it all feels like it might be enough.

(Word count: 2498)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 2500 words.
samples.result:
**The Quiet Majesty of Trees**

I want to talk about trees. Not in the sentimental, hug-a-forest way that sometimes feels like performance, but in the raw, almost uncomfortable way they exist as the longest continuous experiment on this planet. They have been here longer than us, longer than mammals, longer than flowers. While we were still tiny furred things scampering between fern roots, trees had already perfected the art of turning sunlight into substance. They are not background. They are the condition of possibility for almost everything we call “nature.”

Consider a single coast redwood (*Sequoia sempervirens*). Some of them are over two thousand years old. That means an individual tree alive today may have first pushed its cotyledons into the air when the Roman Republic was still a rowdy idea and the Mayan civilization had not yet begun its classical period. The same tree has been breathing while empires rose, collapsed, while humans invented agriculture, metallurgy, the printing press, calculus, jazz, quantum mechanics, and TikTok. It has survived ice ages, droughts, fires, and the indifferent cruelty of tectonic shifts. Its stillness is not passivity; it is a different speed of being.

We tend to anthropomorphize trees because our minds are wired for narrative and agency. We say a tree “reaches” for the light, that it “fights” for water, that it “remembers” through its rings. But the truth is stranger than any metaphor. A tree doesn’t aspire. It responds. Every decision (where to send sugars, when to drop leaves, how thick to grow the cambium this year) is a distributed computation performed across an entire organism without a brain. The intelligence is in the network: mycorrhizal fungi threading between roots like living Ethernet cables, chemical signals drifting on the wind, electrical impulses traveling at a stately three millimeters per second. A forest is not a collection of individuals. It is a superorganism arguing with itself in slow motion.

I once spent a night alone in an old-growth forest in Oregon during a February storm. The rain came down in sheets so dense it felt like being immersed in falling mercury. Every few minutes a gust would hit the canopy and the sound was biblical; a roar like an ocean breaking overhead. I sat with my back against a Douglas fir whose diameter was wider than my outstretched arms. At some point I realized I was leaning against something that had been photosynthesizing since before Christopher Columbus was born. The bark was so deeply furrowed it felt like resting against an ancient hand. Water poured down the grooves in rivers. The tree didn’t care about me. That indifference was strangely comforting. In a world where everything is constantly optimized for human attention, the tree’s utter lack of interest in my existence felt like grace.

Trees manufacture the air we breathe. Twenty-eight percent of the oxygen in every breath you take was produced by marine phytoplankton, but the rest comes largely from terrestrial plants, with forests doing the heavy lifting. Yet we have spent the last two hundred years treating them as either inconvenient obstacles to agriculture or raw material for toothpicks and toilet paper. The statistics are grotesque. We lose about ten million hectares of forest per year; an area roughly the size of Iceland or the state of Virginia, every single year. Most of that is permanent conversion to pasture or palm oil plantations. The Amazon, that great green lung, is now so fragmented and so close to tipping points that large sections may flip from carbon sink to carbon source within our lifetimes. The trees are dying faster than we can comprehend what their death means.

But here is what fascinates me: trees are fighting back in ways we barely understand.

In 1997 a Canadian forest ecologist named Suzanne Simard discovered that paper birch and Douglas fir were exchanging carbon through fungal networks. The scientific community initially scoffed. The idea that trees could “talk” through underground networks sounded like New Age mysticism. Twenty-five years later we know it’s not mysticism. It’s biochemistry. Trees shuttle sugars, nitrogen, defense compounds, even electrical signals through the Wood Wide Web. A mother tree can recognize her own kin and preferentially send them resources. When a tree is attacked by insects, it can warn its neighbors, who then ramp up production of distasteful chemicals before the bugs even arrive. This is not consciousness as we understand it, but it is undeniably a form of memory, communication, and community that operates on timescales we can barely perceive.

I sometimes wonder what it would be like to be a tree. To experience time not as a frantic series of emergencies but as a long, slow exhale. To feel winter not as suffering but as necessary contraction. To taste the difference in rainwater that falls after a lightning storm versus after a long drought. To know your neighbors not by sight but by the unique chemical signature of their root exudates. To live so completely inside your own body that the boundary between self and world becomes porous. The fungal hyphae that weave through your roots are not parasites; they are extensions of your digestive and nervous systems. You are a node in a larger mind.

There is a 9,550-year-old spruce in Sweden whose root system has been cloning itself since the end of the last ice age. The individual trunks come and go, but the root mass persists. Is it one tree or many? At what point does an organism become a place? These questions stop being semantic and start being metaphysical when you sit with them long enough. The oldest living thing on Earth may not be an individual at all but a colony, a relationship, a conversation that has been going on since before humans had language to describe it.

We are currently in the middle of the Sixth Mass Extinction, and trees are both victims and potential saviors. Reforestation projects have become popular, which is good, but many of them are ecologically naïve. Planting ten million monoculture pine trees in straight rows is not rewilding; it’s an agricultural crop with extra steps. Real forest restoration means understanding succession, mycology, hydrology, and the specific cultural requirements of hundreds of species. It means accepting that a healthy forest looks messy. It has dead trees, fallen logs, patches of different ages, plants that look like weeds to the untrained eye. Beauty in ecology is rarely photogenic.

I have planted trees. Hundreds of them. Most were in the Piedmont of North Carolina on eroded clay soils left behind by cotton farming. The first few years were discouraging. Seedlings died. Deer ate the ones that survived. Drought baked the ground until it cracked like pottery. But then something shifted around year seven. The canopy began to close. Leaf litter accumulated. Soil started to smell like soil again instead of sterile red dust. Birds appeared that hadn’t been there before. The temperature under the young forest became noticeably cooler than the surrounding pasture. The trees had begun to terraform their own environment. They were not just growing; they were changing the rules of the game.

There is a concept in Japanese forestry called “shinrin-yoku” (forest bathing) that has been somewhat commodified in the West. But the underlying physiology is real. Phytoncides, the airborne chemicals trees release to defend themselves, have measurable effects on human immune function. Cortisol levels drop. NK cells (natural killer cells that fight cancer and viruses) increase. Heart rate variability improves. We evolved under canopies. Our nervous systems still recognize the fractal patterns of branches against sky as home. Spending time among trees is not spirituality. It is evolutionary medicine.

I keep thinking about the survivors. The ancient bristlecone pines in the White Mountains of California that grow so slowly their annual rings are nearly invisible to the naked eye. Some are over 5,000 years old. They live in conditions so harsh (dolomitic soil, freezing nights, desiccating winds) that they look like tortured sculptures rather than trees. Their wood is so dense it can sink in water. They survive by essentially shutting down large parts of themselves, letting sections die back while preserving a thin ribbon of living tissue. Their strategy is maximum endurance through minimalism. I find this philosophically moving. In an age that celebrates frantic growth at all costs, the bristlecone asks: what if the goal was simply to still be here five thousand years from now?

There is also the matter of what trees remember. Dendrochronology (the study of tree rings) has given us detailed climate records going back thousands of years. But some researchers now believe that trees may possess other, more mysterious forms of memory. Experiments with stressed plants show that offspring can inherit stress responses even when the stressor is no longer present. The mechanism appears to involve epigenetics (chemical tags on DNA that influence gene expression without changing the genetic code itself). A tree that experiences drought may “teach” its seedlings to be more drought-tolerant before the seeds even leave the cone. This is not Lamarckian inheritance exactly, but it is closer to it than strict Darwinian orthodoxy would like. Life is messier than our models.

I sometimes lie awake thinking about the carbon. Every ton of dry wood contains roughly half a ton of carbon pulled from the atmosphere. The world’s forests currently hold about 861 billion tons of carbon in their biomass. That is equivalent to roughly eighty years of current global fossil fuel emissions. If we lost all the world’s forests tomorrow, we would essentially be unlocking a carbon bomb equivalent to detonating every fossil fuel reserve we’ve already discovered. The reverse is also true. Reforesting strategic parts of the planet at scale (not just any trees anywhere, but the right trees in the right places with the right fungal partners) could buy us crucial decades in the climate crisis. Not as a substitute for slashing emissions, but as a necessary complement.

The mathematics are daunting but not impossible. A 2019 study in *Science* suggested that restoring 0.9 billion hectares of forest (an area roughly the size of the United States) could sequester 205 gigatons of carbon. That’s about two-thirds of the carbon humans have added to the atmosphere since the Industrial Revolution. The caveats are enormous. Not all land is suitable. Biodiversity must come first. Indigenous land rights cannot be violated. Monocultures don’t count. Still, the numbers suggest that trees are among the most powerful technologies we have for carbon drawdown, and they essentially install themselves if we simply get out of their way.

I have favorites. The bald cypress with its impossible knees rising from blackwater swamps like supplicants. The ginkgo, living fossil, whose fan-shaped leaves turn the most electric yellow in autumn before dropping all at once in a single golden day. The strangler fig that begins life as an epiphyte and slowly murders its host tree with patient, muscular roots. The baobab, upside-down tree of African savannas, whose massive trunks store thousands of liters of water. The trembling aspen clones in Utah that form single organisms spanning over a hundred acres. Each one feels like a different answer to the question “What is a tree?”

Lately I’ve been thinking about death. Not in the morbid sense, but in the ecological one. A large tree in a forest doesn’t die and disappear. It becomes a snag, then a nurse log. It may stand as a skeleton for decades, providing nesting cavities for birds and bats. When it finally falls, its decaying mass becomes a raised bed of perfect fertility in a nutrient-poor forest floor. Seedlings sprout along its length in a line that biologists call “regeneration on a corpse.” The fallen tree will nourish hundreds of new individuals over centuries. Its death is not an ending but a redistribution of wealth earned over five hundred years of photosynthesis. There is no waste.

Human cultures have always known this on some level. Sacred groves. Tree worship. The World Tree of Norse mythology. The Bodhi tree under which the Buddha attained enlightenment. Yggdrasil. The Egyptian sycamore that was home to Hathor. Indigenous Australian songlines that trace forest corridors. The Cherokee belief that trees are the oldest living beings and therefore the wisest. Almost every culture that lived intimately with forests developed some form of tree reverence. Only industrial civilization tried to reduce them to board feet and biomass.

We are paying for that reductionism now. Not just in lost biodiversity and destabilized climate, but in a deeper spiritual poverty. When you spend enough time among old trees, you begin to feel the weight of their regard. Not that they care about you specifically (they don’t), but that you are in the presence of something that has contemplated the sun for centuries. It rearranges your sense of scale. Your problems don’t get smaller exactly, but they become properly sized. You are a mayfly arguing with other mayflies on the bark of something ancient.

I want my ashes scattered in a forest, but not before I spend whatever years I have left trying to be useful to trees. There is work to be done. Mycorrhizal inoculation of nurseries. Protection of old-growth stands that still remain. Development of better remote sensing so we can detect illegal logging in real time.
