People were so scared of being buried alive that some coffins came with bells attached to strings that ran up to the surface. A “safety coffin.” Imagine dying, waking up six feet underground, and having the presence of mind to find the string and ring the bell. The survival rate for people who actually used these bells is, as far as historical records show, zero. Which makes sense. If you’re claustrophobic enough to need a bell in your coffin, you’re probably not handling live burial with calm competence.

The things we fear reveal us. We used to fear being buried alive. Now we fear being forgotten while still alive. We build digital monuments to ourselves (Instagram feeds, carefully curated personalities, opinionated Twitter accounts) because the alternative is to disappear into the noise of eight billion other voices all screaming “notice me” at once. The scariest thing in 2025 isn’t death. It’s irrelevance.

And yet.

The best moments of my life have all happened when I forgot to be relevant. When I was completely absorbed in something outside myself. Reading a book so good I forgot my own name. Laughing until I couldn’t breathe with friends at 2 a.m. about something that would be completely unfunny in daylight. Standing in front of a painting that made me feel like someone had reached into my chest and rearranged my organs. These moments don’t photograph well. They don’t go viral. They don’t build your personal brand. They just make you more real while you’re having them.

I’ve started collecting these moments like a dragon hoards gold. I don’t tell people about most of them because they sound boring when explained. “I watched light move across a wooden table for twenty minutes and felt overwhelming tenderness toward existence” doesn’t really land at dinner parties. But these are the experiences that keep me from turning into a ghoul. They’re proof that the world is still enchanted if you can shut up long enough to notice.

The older I get, the more I think the real skill isn’t success or intelligence or even kindness (though all those matter). The real skill is attention. The ability to actually be where you are instead of where your thoughts are. Most of us are time travelers. We’re either relitigating something that happened in 2017 or rehearsing a conversation we’ll never actually have in 2029. Very few of us are here, now, noticing the ridiculous beauty of being temporarily alive on a wet rock hurtling through space.

I was trying to explain this to my friend last week and he looked at me like I’d started speaking in tongues. Then he said, “You know what your problem is? You think too much.” Which is both completely true and the most useless advice ever given. It’s like telling an insomniac “just relax.” Thanks. Revolutionary.

But he’s right. I do think too much. I always have. When I was seven I asked my mother if she thought the universe might be a dream that God was having, and if so, what would happen when he woke up. She told me to go outside and play. That was good advice. I’ve spent thirty years trying to follow it with mixed results.

Here’s what I’ve learned in those thirty years:

- Almost nothing is as serious as it feels in the moment.
- The people who seem most confident are usually making it up as they go along, same as you.
- Kindness is the only thing that scales. Everything else hits some kind of limit.
- Trees are better at being trees than I will ever be at being human, and that’s strangely comforting.
- Music can do in four minutes what therapy takes four years to accomplish.
- The best way to predict how someone will treat you is to watch how they treat waiters, animals, and their mother.
- Regret is interest payment on a debt you don’t owe anymore.
- The sea doesn’t care about your problems and that’s why it helps to look at it.

I could keep going. There are another thousand words easy. But the sun is coming through the window at an angle that makes the dust look like slow motion galaxies and I think I’m going to stop typing and pay attention to it for a while.

The world is ending and beginning at the same time, always. Right now, somewhere, someone is falling in love for the first time. Somewhere else, someone is taking their last breath. A child is learning to jump off a curb. A mushroom is hunting in the dark. A woman is writing too many words about nothing in particular because she’s trying to stay awake to her own life.

All of it matters. None of it matters. Both statements are true at once and that contradiction is where the magic lives.

I think I’ll go watch the dust for a bit. You should find something to watch too. Not on a screen. Something small and ordinary that becomes enormous when you actually see it.

The universe is showing off all the time. Most of us are just too busy to notice.

(Word count: 1,012)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
I want to talk about the quiet revolution happening inside us all, the one nobody photographs or tweets about. It’s the slow, stubborn rewiring of a human mind that has decided—against every modern incentive—to become more real.

We live in an age of perfect noise. Notifications arrive like anxious sparrows, each one convinced its message is the most important thing that has ever happened. Our days fragment into ten-second increments of dopamine. We scroll until our thumbs develop their own anxiety disorders. And in the middle of this glittering landfill of stimulation, something ancient inside us is dying from lack of oxygen.

I’ve been watching this death and rebirth in myself for the last few years with the horrified fascination of a man watching his house burn down and then noticing flowers growing in the ashes.

The first sign was boredom. Real boredom. Not the Instagram kind where you’re “bored” but actually refreshing three apps in rotation. I mean the kind where you sit on a park bench with nothing in your hands and the silence feels like it might actually kill you. That kind of boredom is sacred. It’s the hallway between who you’ve been trained to be and who you actually are.

Most people never make it down that hallway. They panic and turn back to the noise. But if you can sit there long enough—maybe twenty unbearable minutes—something miraculous happens. Your mind, which has been trained like a circus animal to perform for likes and dings and little red badges, starts to forget its tricks. It forgets how to perform. And in that forgetting, it begins to remember how to think.

I started taking what I now call “boredom walks.” No phone. No podcasts. No goals. Just walking until my mind got so bored it began inventing. Not the anxious, looping kind of inventing that happens when you’re trying to solve your life. But the playful, useless, absolutely necessary kind. I would look at a fire hydrant and suddenly see it as a tiny tired robot who had been standing in the same spot since 1973. I would watch two pigeons having what looked like a very serious business meeting on a windowsill. The world became ridiculously alive.

This is when I realized how much of my previous “intelligence” had actually been sophisticated pattern-matching. I could quote thinkers, synthesize ideas, drop references at parties. But I couldn’t look at a tree for three minutes without getting bored. That, I have come to believe, is a form of spiritual malnutrition.

The deeper I went into this practice of deliberate uselessness, the more I began to notice how much of modern life is dedicated to preventing exactly this kind of encounter with reality. Every screen, every productivity system, every self-optimization protocol is basically a sophisticated avoidance of the terrifying fact that you are a temporary animal standing on a rock hurtling through space, gifted with consciousness for what amounts to a cosmic afternoon.

We are all having an existential experience while pretending we’re doing project management.

I started reading fiction again—not as content to consume, but as communion. I would read the same paragraph of Marilynne Robinson three times and feel like I was being rebuilt at a cellular level. I discovered that certain books function like mirrors that show you who you were before the world taught you how to lie to yourself. The best art doesn’t entertain you. It indicts you. Then, if you’re lucky, it forgives you.

There’s a particular quality of attention that only becomes available when you’ve given up on being interesting. I started noticing it during long train rides where I would simply watch people’s faces. Not in a creepy way. Just… attending. And I realized that every single person on that train was experiencing an inner weather system more complex than anything we’ve managed to model with our most advanced computers. Every furrowed brow contained galaxies of grief, hope, boredom, lust, tenderness, and exhaustion. We are all walking catastrophes and miracles simultaneously.

This realization broke something in me. The part that needed to be right. The part that needed to win arguments on the internet. The part that secretly believed my opinions were more valuable because I read more Substacks. That part died quietly on a Tuesday afternoon in October while I was watching a leaf fall. I didn’t even notice it was gone until weeks later when someone tried to bait me into an online fight and I felt nothing but a kind of spacious curiosity. Who is this person? What does it feel like to be them right now?

The strangest part of this entire process has been discovering that the less I produce, the more I seem to actually matter to the people around me. When I was frantically writing, tweeting, building, optimizing, I was essentially broadcasting a hologram of myself into the world. People interacted with the hologram. Now that I’ve gone quieter, the interactions feel more like two actual humans recognizing each other in the dark.

I’ve been thinking a lot about what I want to pass on to my children, if I’m ever lucky enough to have them. Not values, exactly. Values can be performed. I want to pass on the capacity for useless attention. The ability to be completely useless for long stretches of time without panicking. The knowledge that their mind is not a machine that needs to be optimized but a wild creature that needs to be tamed through love and patience and many, many hours of doing nothing productive whatsoever.

The world will try to convince them that their worth is measurable. That their attention is a resource to be mined. That silence is failure and stillness is laziness. I want them to have enough inner life to laugh at these lies.

Sometimes I worry I’m becoming one of those insufferable people who talks about “presence” while secretly judging everyone on their phones. But that’s not quite it. I still use my phone. I still get lost in the glowing rectangle. The difference is that I now know what I’m losing when I do. There’s a grief to it that feels honest.

The revolution isn’t dramatic. Nobody storms any barricades. It happens in small, almost ridiculous moments: choosing to leave your phone in another room for an hour. Reading a physical book until your eyes hurt. Having a conversation where you don’t check anything, not even once. Walking somewhere without telling anyone where you’re going or documenting it for posterity.

These tiny rebellions accumulate. They compound. One day you look up and realize you’re thinking thoughts that would have been impossible for you five years ago—not because they’re more intelligent, but because they’re more yours.

I don’t know where this path leads. Maybe it leads nowhere in particular, which might be the entire point. The modern world is structured to make every moment lead somewhere—to a destination, a metric, an outcome. But some things are meant to lead nowhere. They’re meant to lead you more deeply into where you already are.

There’s a Japanese concept called “ma”—the beauty of negative space, the meaningful pause between notes, the fertile emptiness. Our culture has declared war on ma. We fill every possible pause with content. But the emptiness keeps calling to us because it knows something we’ve forgotten: that’s where we actually live. Not in our achievements or our opinions or our carefully curated digital selves, but in the space between them.

I’ve started collecting these spaces the way other people collect stamps or rare books. A perfect ten minutes watching rain on a window. The particular quality of silence at 3 a.m. when even the city seems to be dreaming. The moment right after a good cry when everything feels suddenly, shockingly gentle.

These aren’t particularly impressive experiences. You can’t really tell anyone about them without sounding slightly deranged. “I sat in a chair and looked at a wall for twenty minutes and it was one of the most important things that has ever happened to me.” But they’re real in a way that most of our achievements aren’t. They don’t need validation because the validation is built into the experience itself.

I suppose if I had to reduce this entire ramble to something resembling wisdom, it would be this: protect your attention like it’s the last pristine wilderness on Earth. Because it is. Everything good, true, and beautiful that humanity has ever produced came from someone’s ability to be uselessly, extravagantly, magnificently attentive for unreasonable lengths of time.

The machines will get faster. The noise will get louder. The incentives to fragment yourself will become more sophisticated. The only reasonable response is to become more unreasonable. To insist on your right to be slow, bored, silent, and unreachable. Not as a lifestyle brand. Not as performance. But as the only way to stay real in a world that profits from your disintegration.

The quiet revolution doesn’t announce itself. It doesn’t have a manifesto. It just slowly, stubbornly, begins to choose reality over simulation, presence over performance, depth over breadth, useless beauty over useful ugliness.

It feels like coming home to a house you didn’t know you’d left.

And the door, remarkably, was never locked.

(Word count: 1,023)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
**The Quiet Power of Small Things**

I’ve been thinking lately about how the world tries to convince us that only the enormous matters. The viral hit. The million-follower account. The billion-dollar exit. The record-breaking storm. Yet the longer I live, the more certain I become that the real architecture of a good life is built from almost absurdly small materials: the particular way someone says your name, the smell of rain on hot pavement, the four seconds a cat decides to sit on your chest and purr like a broken engine.

This morning I watched a spider rebuild her web between two lavender bushes. She worked with the calm competence of someone who has done this a thousand times and expects to do it a thousand more. When the wind tore a guy-line loose, she didn’t panic. She simply started again. No social media post about her setback. No TED Talk about resilience. Just eight patient legs and an instinct older than mammals. I sat there drinking coffee that had already gone lukewarm and felt, for the first time in weeks, properly oriented to reality.

We are drowning in scale. The news presents every local tragedy as a civilizational omen. The internet compresses ten thousand years of human culture into an infinite, jostling hallway where every voice shouts at once. In that hallway it’s easy to believe that your private joys are insignificant. But the spider doesn’t know she’s small. She only knows the geometry of hunger and safety. There’s a kind of dignity in that ignorance.

I have a friend who collects fountain pens. Not as investments. He buys battered 1930s Pelikans for twenty dollars, spends months restoring them, then writes long, digressive letters to other middle-aged men who also love ink. Their correspondence is full of sentences like “The Diamine Oxblood is behaving like a dream on the Tomoe River paper but feathers slightly on the cheaper stock.” They send each other tiny glass vials of ink the way teenagers once traded mixtapes. When I tease him about it he says, “Most of my life is noise. This is signal.” I think he’s right. The world is loud; a beautiful nib scratching across paper is quiet enough to hear yourself think inside.

Last summer I spent three days in total silence at a monastery in the high desert. No phones, no talking, no books except a small volume of Rumi. At first the quiet felt like violence. My mind sprinted around looking for stimulation the way a dog looks for a ball. By the second day something cracked open. I noticed that the sound of my own chewing was interesting. That the way light moved across a wooden table at 4:17 pm was enough to occupy me completely. I understood, suddenly and physically, that most of what I call “boredom” is just intolerance for my own thoughts. The monks weren’t doing nothing. They were doing the hardest thing in the modern world: allowing time to contain only itself.

When I came back I tried to keep a small corner of that silence alive. I fail most days. The gravitational pull of the glowing rectangle is immense. But I’ve started keeping my phone in another room while I drink my first coffee. Those twenty minutes have become the most important ritual of my day. I sit on the back steps and watch the neighborhood wake up. The retired schoolteacher walks her beagle. The teenage boy practices ollies in his driveway and falls approximately every fourth attempt. A murder of crows holds parliament in the pine tree. Nothing is optimized. Nothing is content. Everything is, in the best possible way, ordinary.

There’s a concept in psychotherapy called “ordinariness” that I keep returning to. The idea that much of what heals us isn’t dramatic insight or catharsis but the repeated, gentle experience of being an ordinary person having ordinary experiences with another ordinary person who is paying attention. The therapist isn’t a wizard. They’re just someone who refuses to look away. In a world that rewards exceptionalism, this is radical.

I’ve started collecting moments the way other people collect coins or stamps. Yesterday’s entry: the sound of my wife laughing at her own bad joke in the next room. The particular way my socks stuck to the kitchen floor because I’d spilled orange juice and couldn’t be bothered to clean it properly. The smell of hot metal when the train brakes as it pulls into the station. These aren’t “inspo” moments. They don’t photograph well. They’re just proof that I was here.

There’s a Japanese term, *komorebi*, that means the particular way sunlight filters through leaves. It’s not a particularly rare phenomenon. It happens constantly in any place with trees. But the fact that the Japanese made a word for it tells you something important: they noticed. They decided this specific quality of light deserved its own name. I think we need about seven thousand more words like that. A word for the particular hush that falls over a city right before it snows. A word for the smell of your childhood home that somehow still lives in your mother’s sweater. A word for the exact flavor of melancholy that arrives at 7:42 pm on a Sunday in November.

The older I get, the more I believe that attention is a moral act. The places we choose to shine our small, personal spotlight become, in aggregate, the shape of our civilization. If we only attend to what is loud and shiny and optimized, we will build a world that feels loud and shiny and optimized. If we attend to what is small and specific and irreplaceable, we might still have a chance at building something that feels like home.

This morning after watching the spider I went inside and wrote a terrible poem about her. Eight lines that will never be published anywhere. The poem itself doesn’t matter. What matters is that for twelve minutes I was completely absorbed in trying to get the words to carry the exact quality of her patience. I failed, of course. But the attempt itself felt like prayer.

I don’t know what any of this adds up to. Probably nothing grand. Just one more middle-aged man trying to resist the current that wants to pull all of us toward frantic, disembodied, scale-obsessed living. The spider is still out there, her web now complete and trembling slightly in the breeze like a suspension bridge for very small angels. In a few hours the morning light will hit it at the perfect angle and it will look, for approximately ninety seconds, like it was spun from pure light.

I plan to be there for those ninety seconds. Phone inside. Coffee in hand. Heart quiet enough to notice.

The world will keep burning and building and tweeting and trending. That’s fine. Some of us will be over here counting the different kinds of green that appear in moss after rain. Keeping the old fountain pens working. Writing letters that will take three days to arrive. Protecting, in a thousand small and stupid ways, the fragile human capacity to be astonished by ordinary things.

It doesn’t feel like enough. It probably isn’t. But it’s what I’ve got.

And for now, it feels like plenty.

(Word count: 1,012)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
I want to talk about the strange, quiet pleasure of being slightly lost.

Not metaphorically lost—though that has its charms too—but the literal, physical sensation of walking through a city you don’t know well and realizing you have no idea where you are. The mild adrenaline, the slight widening of the eyes, the way the buildings suddenly feel taller and more indifferent. There’s a particular texture to that moment that I’ve come to crave.

Last summer I spent ten days in Lisbon with nothing but a battered notebook and a terrible sense of direction. I refused to use my phone for maps. The rule was simple: if I couldn’t find it on foot, it didn’t want to be found. What followed was one of the most alive stretches of time I’ve experienced in years.

I would wake up early, drink coffee that tasted like it had been brewed by someone angry at the world, and then simply walk out the door of my tiny rented room in Alfama. Within fifteen minutes I was reliably lost. The streets there don’t cooperate with Cartesian logic. They coil, they contradict themselves, they remember medieval donkey paths and refuse to forget them. One minute you’re descending ancient stone stairs between houses painted the color of faded sardines; the next you’re staring at the Tagus River glittering like it’s showing off, wondering how the hell you got there.

Being lost slows everything down in the best possible way. You notice things. The particular blue of the azulejo tiles that someone chose in 1893 because it reminded them of their mother’s favorite dress. The way old women in black lean out of second-story windows to water plants that have no business growing that high up. The sound of someone practicing fado badly in a back room—earnest, cracking, heartbreaking. You start to understand that a city is a conversation between centuries, and you’re just a polite tourist eavesdropping.

I found a tiny square no larger than a generous living room that contained six cats, three old men playing cards, and one fountain that hadn’t worked since the Carnation Revolution. I sat there for forty minutes watching the cats judge everyone who walked by. The old men offered me wine from a bottle that had no label. I accepted. We didn’t speak the same language but we understood each other perfectly. One of them beat me at cards with ruthless efficiency. I lost two euros and gained an afternoon I’ll remember longer than most relationships.

There’s something about being slightly lost that returns you to your senses. Modern life does its best to insulate us from raw sensory input. We move through climate-controlled corridors from screen to screen. But when you’re lost, your body wakes up. Your ears track the density of sound—how footsteps change when you move from cobblestones to asphalt. Your nose becomes a historian: the smell of grilled sardines means you’re near a certain type of neighborhood, while the sudden absence of that smell tells you you’ve wandered into the wrong century somehow. Even your skin gets involved, registering the exact temperature gradient as you move from shadow to sunlight between buildings.

I started keeping notes not about what I saw but how I felt while seeing it. “Chest light, like someone removed a small stone I didn’t know was there.” “The particular melancholy that comes from realizing the beautiful building you’re looking at will outlive you by four hundred years and never know you existed.” “The sudden, stupid affection you can feel for a random stranger carrying too many bags because their face suggests they’re also pretending to have a plan.”

The best discoveries happened when I abandoned even the pretense of destination. One afternoon I followed a woman who was carrying an enormous bouquet of calla lilies because I wanted to know where lilies that perfect came from. I followed her for twenty-five minutes through increasingly narrow streets until she disappeared into a building that looked condemned. I stood there feeling foolish until an old man opened the door and said, in perfect English for no reason at all, “She’s my daughter. The flowers are for her mother’s grave. Would you like to come in?”

I did. Of course I did.

Inside was a miniature jungle. Plants everywhere. Orchids growing from cracks in the wall. A tortoise named Vasco da Gama who regarded me with ancient suspicion. The woman’s mother had been dead for seventeen years but they still brought her flowers every Wednesday. I drank port that tasted like velvet and history. We looked at old photographs. I learned that the grandmother had been a spy during the Salazar years and had once hidden a radio inside a chicken.

None of this would have happened if I’d known where I was going.

I’ve started to believe that being slightly lost is one of the last truly democratic experiences available to us. It requires no special talent, no money, no connections. Anyone can do it. The sidewalk belongs to everyone. The act of turning left instead of right for no particular reason is a small rebellion against the optimized life that algorithms and calendar apps are constantly trying to sell us.

There’s a phrase I love from the poet Naomi Shihab Nye: “The thing that’s not quite right is the note that makes the song worth singing.” Being lost is that note. It’s the crack in the sidewalk that lets the grass through. It’s the reminder that the map is not the territory, and that the territory is usually much stranger and more interesting than we give it credit for.

I met a man in Belém who told me he’d been walking the same three-mile loop every day for forty-two years. He knew every crack in every pavement stone. He could tell you which trees would drop their flowers on what day in April. His life looked like absolute hell to me—such repetition, such certainty. But when I told him I spent my days trying to get lost, his face lit up with something like recognition. “Ah,” he said. “You’re doing the real walking. I’m just marking time.”

I think about that often.

The night before I left Lisbon, I got spectacularly lost. It was almost midnight. I’d been walking for hours. My feet hurt. My notebook was full. I had no idea where my rented room was. For perhaps twenty minutes I felt something close to genuine panic—Lisbon at night can feel like a city that’s turning its back on you. Then I turned a corner and saw the river again, and the lights of the 25 de Abril Bridge stretching across it like a promise, and something in me relaxed so completely that I actually laughed out loud.

I was still lost. But I was lost in a place that felt like it had been waiting for me.

I sat on a bench and watched the ferries moving across the dark water like slow, thoughtful thoughts. A young couple walked past arguing in Portuguese about something that sounded both passionate and ridiculous. An old woman sold me a single rose for one euro and refused to explain why she was selling roses at midnight. I took the rose. I still have it, dried and pressed between the pages of that notebook.

Eventually I found my way home. Or rather, my feet found their own way while my mind was busy being astonished by the stars, which seemed much closer in Lisbon than they do in most places I’ve lived.

I’ve been home for months now. My life has reasserted its usual shape—appointments, obligations, the gentle tyranny of knowing where I’m supposed to be. But sometimes, late at night, I’ll close my eyes and remember what it felt like to stand in a city that didn’t owe me anything and realize I had no idea what would happen next.

There’s a freedom in that moment that no planned itinerary can ever match. It’s the freedom of being a citizen of the world instead of a tourist in it. Of letting the world surprise you instead of demanding that it perform.

I don’t know if I’ll ever live in Lisbon. I don’t know if I’ll ever be that deliberately, deliciously lost again for ten whole days. But I carry the memory of it like a small smooth stone in my pocket. When things get too scheduled, too optimized, too certain, I take it out and rub it between my fingers. I remember how the light hit those blue tiles. I remember the taste of that unlabeled wine. I remember that for a little while, I belonged to no schedule at all.

And then I try, in small ways, to get lost again. I take the wrong train. I walk instead of driving. I follow the woman with the extraordinary flowers, wherever she might lead.

The world, it turns out, is much larger than our maps would have us believe. And the best parts of it tend to reveal themselves only to people who have the courage, or the foolishness, to admit they don’t know where they’re going.

I highly recommend becoming slightly lost as often as possible. Your feet will complain. Your ego will sulk. Your carefully constructed identity might take a few bruises. But something else—something quieter and more essential—will wake up.

And it will sing.

(Word count: 1,012)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
I’ve been thinking a lot lately about how strange it is that we’re all just walking around carrying little glass rectangles that contain the entire accumulated knowledge of our species, yet we mostly use them to argue with strangers and watch videos of cats falling off tables. There’s something wonderfully absurd about that. The same device that let a teenager in rural Kenya teach herself calculus also lets me doomscroll through arguments about whether pineapple belongs on pizza at 2 a.m. when I know I should be sleeping. We built a planetary nervous system and immediately started using it to send each other memes about our bad decisions.

I’ve started collecting small, quiet moments that feel like they don’t belong to the algorithm. The way my neighbor’s elderly golden retriever sighs when he finally lies down after a long day of supervising the street. The particular smell that rises from hot pavement right after the first raindrops hit it in July. The half-second of eye contact you share with someone on the subway when both of you clearly just heard the same ridiculous thing and don’t need to say anything about it. These moments feel increasingly precious because they resist being turned into content. They’re not optimized. They don’t have metrics. They just exist, briefly, and then they’re gone.

Sometimes I wonder what archaeologists a thousand years from now will make of us. They’ll dig through our landfills and find millions of perfectly preserved plastic containers that once held single servings of things we apparently couldn’t be bothered to share. They’ll recover our hard drives (if anything still works) and discover that roughly 40% of human creative output between 2008 and 2035 consisted of people saying “this is the best thing I’ve ever seen” about videos that were, objectively, not that. They might conclude we were a civilization that simultaneously reached the pinnacle of technological sophistication while developing the collective attention span of a goldfish with ADHD.

But they’d be missing the part where we were also absurdly tender with each other in private. I think about how many people right now are lying in bed next to someone they love, scrolling on their phones with the brightness turned all the way down so they don’t wake their partner. Or the way we’ll send someone a song at 3 p.m. on a Tuesday because it reminded us of them, even though we haven’t spoken in six months. The quiet, constant background radiation of care that doesn’t make it onto any timeline.

I’ve been trying to get better at being bored again. It’s harder than it sounds. The first ten minutes feel like withdrawal. Your brain keeps reaching for the dopamine lever that isn’t there anymore. But if you can survive that period, something interesting happens. Your thoughts start doing this wandering, meandering thing that they haven’t been allowed to do in years. You remember that you’ve been meaning to learn how to identify clouds. Or you notice that the way light falls across your kitchen counter at 4:17 p.m. is actually kind of beautiful. Or you just sit there and let yourself feel the particular texture of being alive in this exact moment, which is something we almost never do anymore.

The other day I was waiting for a train and instead of taking out my phone I just watched an ant trying to drag a piece of popcorn that was easily twenty times its size. The ant would pull, the popcorn would roll slightly, the ant would adjust its grip, and they’d do this awkward little dance across the platform for a solid ten minutes. I became weirdly invested. When the ant finally managed to get it near the edge of the platform where it could presumably take it down to wherever ants live, I actually felt proud. Like I’d witnessed something. The train came and I got on it feeling lighter somehow, like I’d remembered that the world is still doing its thing whether or not I’m there to document it.

There’s a line from a poem I can’t quite remember properly that something about how the days are gods. I think about that when I catch myself treating time like currency I need to spend as efficiently as possible. The days aren’t resources. They’re these strange, unrepeatable miracles that keep arriving whether we deserve them or not. Some of them will be boring. Some of them will hurt. Some of them will contain perfect minutes where everything feels exactly right for no particular reason. The trick seems to be staying open enough to notice when those minutes show up.

I’ve started ending my days by writing down three things that happened that couldn’t have happened at any other point in history. Not the big things. The tiny, specific ones. Like how I watched my friend’s toddler try to explain the concept of “Tuesday” to a pigeon. Or how I saw two separate people wearing the exact same obscure band t-shirt on the same subway car and they both pretended not to notice each other. Or how the barista remembered that I like my coffee “hot but not too hot” and we both laughed about what a ridiculous specification that is. These are the artifacts I want to leave behind, not the optimized versions of myself I try to present online.

The world feels both much smaller and much larger than it did ten years ago. Smaller because I can video call my friend in Tokyo while I’m waiting in line for coffee and we can share a moment across nine time zones like it’s nothing. Larger because every day I learn about some new subculture or scientific discovery or way of being human that I never could have imagined. The depth is staggering. We’re all just scratching at the surface of what’s possible, and most of us are doing it while also trying to remember whether we bought oat milk.

I don’t know where I’m going with any of this. That’s kind of the point. For once I’m not trying to make this useful or insightful or cleverly structured. I’m just writing because the act of putting words down feels like one of the few honest things left in a world that’s increasingly mediated through performance. Somewhere in these thousand words there might be one sentence that feels true to you, or maybe none of them do, and that’s fine too. The important thing is that for a little while we sat here together in the strange quiet space between thoughts, being human in one of the ways that still works.

The sun is coming up now where I am, and there’s that particular birdsong that only happens right before the city fully wakes up. I think I’ll go listen to it for a while without taking any pictures or posting about it or turning it into anything other than what it is: just birds, doing their bird things, on a random Tuesday that will never happen again.

(Word count: 1002)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
# The Quiet Revolution of Attention

I’ve been thinking lately about how everything worthwhile in life seems to hinge on one invisible resource: attention. Not in the buzzy, Silicon Valley “attention economy” sense—though that’s part of it—but in a deeper, almost spiritual way. The quality of our lives is ultimately the quality of what we pay attention to, and for how long, and with what depth.

This feels especially urgent now. We live in the noisiest epoch in human history. Not just in decibels, but in competing claims on our awareness. Every morning I wake up and my pocket-sized rectangle of glass and lithium is already screaming at me with 47 red bubbles of urgency. Someone has liked something. Someone else has replied. A country is on fire. A celebrity has posted a cryptic tweet that apparently means the world is ending. My coffee subscription wants to tell me about new beans from Guatemala. All of it formatted to hijack the ancient parts of my brain that once scanned the savanna for tigers.

And I comply. We all do. We have outsourced the management of our own curiosity to algorithms whose only terminal goal is to keep us scrolling. The machine learns what makes our pupils dilate, what makes us lean in, what triggers that little hit of dopamine, and it serves it faster than we can notice we’re being served. It’s not evil. It’s just optimized. We built a system that is better at holding our attention than we are at directing it, and then acted surprised when it worked.

I deleted most social media apps from my phone last year. Not out of some noble pursuit of mindfulness, but because I got tired of feeling disgusting at 11pm after doomscrolling for forty minutes about subjects I didn’t even care about. The relief was immediate and enormous, like removing a small but constant mechanical noise you’d learned to ignore. For the first time in years I could actually choose what to think about.

The results were strange. My mind, suddenly unsupervised, didn’t know what to do. It panicked. It reached for the familiar digital pacifier that wasn’t there. There were days I felt genuinely bored for the first time since childhood. Actual, crystalline boredom—the kind that hurts a little in the chest. And then something interesting happened. The boredom softened into something else. A kind of spaciousness. I started noticing things again. The way light moves across the floor in the afternoon. The particular rhythm of my partner’s breathing when she’s reading. The fact that my houseplants were, against all odds, still alive despite my neglect.

I began to read books again—not the kind you consume in two days because you’re stress-reading to avoid your life, but the slow, difficult ones that require something from you. I read *The Brothers Karamazov* like a starving person. Not because I’m particularly religious or Russian, but because I wanted to remember what it felt like to be inside a mind that was trying to solve the entire problem of being human. Dostoevsky doesn’t care about your dopamine receptors. He demands everything. And in return he gives you back a version of yourself that feels more real.

This is what I mean by the quiet revolution. It’s not loud. It doesn’t have a TED Talk or a catchy slogan. It’s just people, one by one, deciding to take their attention back. Some of them are doing it by moving to small towns and learning to garden. Some are doing it by becoming obsessed with 18th-century cabinetmaking or mycology or building mechanical keyboards. The specifics don’t matter. What matters is the decision to become hard to hijack.

Because here’s the thing: attention is the closest thing we have to a soul. It’s the directed application of consciousness. When you pay attention to something, you are literally giving it part of your life. Minutes that will never come back. And in that exchange, something happens. The object of attention changes, and so do you. This is why love and attention feel so similar. To truly attend to another person—without trying to fix them or impress them or extract something from the interaction—is one of the most radical acts available to us.

I’ve been trying to apply this to my writing. For years I wrote with one eye on the potential audience, crafting sentences that would travel well on the internet. It made the work smaller. Now I’m trying to write the way I read *Karamazov*—as if no one is watching, as if the only criterion is whether the sentence feels true in my body when I read it back. It’s terrifying. It feels amateurish. I keep having to resist the urge to add a clever observation or a relatable aside that would make it “pop.” But there’s something alive in it that wasn’t there before.

The same principle applies to relationships. The best moments I’ve had with people this year happened when both of us forgot our phones existed. When conversation was allowed to meander and stall and find its own rhythm instead of performing for invisible audiences. Real connection requires a willingness to be inefficient. To stay in the uncomfortable silence until something real emerges. Social media trains us to optimize for engagement, but life often requires us to optimize for depth. These two goals are increasingly at war.

There’s a Buddhist concept called *samadhi*—often translated as concentration or meditative absorption. It’s the ability to rest your attention on one thing so completely that the sense of separation between observer and observed begins to dissolve. In that state, you don’t have attention. You *are* attention. The self drops away and what remains is pure seeing. Most of us will never achieve the monk-like versions of this, but we can taste approximations of it. When I’m deep in the flow of writing and six hours disappear. When I’m playing music with friends and the boundary between us gets porous. When I’m making love and time becomes meaningless. These are moments when attention becomes so total that it transforms into presence.

And presence, it turns out, is the only place life actually happens.

The future doesn’t belong to the smartest or the richest or even the hardest working in the conventional sense. It belongs to the people who can protect their attention long enough to do deep work, to love deeply, to think original thoughts instead of remixed ones. This might be the ultimate luxury good of the 21st century—not a physical object but a condition of mind. The ability to be where you are.

I don’t think this requires moving to a cabin in the woods (though I understand the impulse). It requires something more difficult: a series of small, repeated refusals. Refusing to open the apps before you’ve had a chance to think your own thoughts in the morning. Refusing to fill every moment of transition with podcasts or music or videos. Refusing the infinite scroll in favor of the finite and difficult book. Refusing to outsource your curiosity to whatever is trending.

These refusals aren’t acts of deprivation. They’re acts of reclamation. Every time you choose to look at something slowly and carefully instead of quickly and hungrily, you are casting a vote for a certain kind of human being. One who is not at the mercy of their environment. One who has an inner life that can sustain itself.

The revolution is quiet because it has to be. If it became loud it would immediately be co-opted—turned into content, merchandised, optimized. Its power lies in its invisibility. In the unseen hours people spend learning to play the piano badly, or writing poems no one will read, or simply sitting on their porch watching the light change. These activities look like nothing from the outside. They are everything from the inside.

I keep coming back to a line from the poet Mary Oliver: “Attention is the beginning of devotion.” I used to think this was a nice sentiment. Now I think it might be the entire game. What you attend to determines what you love. What you love determines who you become. And who we become, collectively, will determine what kind of world we make.

So this is my small contribution to the quiet revolution: a few hundred words written slowly, without checking my phone once. An attempt to pay attention to attention itself. To say, out loud but quietly, that there is still time to choose. That the machines haven’t won yet. That the human nervous system, for all its flaws, is still capable of wonder when left alone long enough.

The morning is almost over. The light has moved across the floor while I wrote this. My plants are still alive. My partner is in the other room humming something I don’t recognize. There’s coffee in the pot and nowhere I need to be. This, right now, is enough. More than enough.

If I’ve done this right, perhaps you felt something similar while reading. A slight settling. A momentary resistance to the urge to switch tabs or check notifications. A brief reunion with your own mind.

That’s how it starts. Not with grand declarations or manifestos. But with small moments of choosing to stay. To attend. To remain.

The revolution will not be livestreamed.

(Word count: 1,023)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
The rain came down in that specific way it only does in late October, not quite a storm but something heavier than a drizzle, the kind that makes every surface slick and reflective like the city has been varnished overnight. I stood under the awning of the closed florist on 14th Street watching water race along the gutters carrying cigarette butts and lost leaves toward the drains. There’s a strange comfort in watching things being carried away. It reminds you that entropy isn’t just a concept in physics textbooks; it’s the quiet engine of every Tuesday afternoon.

I’ve been thinking a lot about disappearance lately. Not the dramatic kind, no vanishing acts or witness protection, but the slow, ordinary kind. The way your favorite sweater gradually becomes a rag you use to clean motorcycle parts. The way songs you once knew by heart now only return in fragments when you’re half-asleep. The way people you loved become stories you tell newer people who never met them, and with each retelling the story gets slightly more polished, slightly less true.

My grandmother used to say that memory is like bread: it goes stale if you don’t consume it quickly enough. She died when I was nineteen and I’ve been trying to eat those memories before they mold for fourteen years now. Some nights I can still taste her specific chicken soup, the one with the tiny meatballs and the dill that stung your sinuses. Other nights I can’t remember what her laugh sounded like, only that it existed. The absence itself has become a kind of presence.

There’s a Japanese concept called *mono no aware* – the gentle sadness at the transience of things. The cherry blossoms fall precisely because they’re beautiful, and their beauty is tied to how briefly they exist. I think about this every time I see someone taking a video of fireworks instead of just watching them. We’ve become a species that tries to possess beauty by capturing it, and in doing so we often miss the very thing we’re trying to keep.

Last week I watched a man in Central Park try to photograph a falcon that had landed on a lamppost. He had one of those enormous lenses that costs more than my first car, and he was so focused on getting the perfect shot that he never once looked at the bird with his naked eyes. The falcon stayed for maybe three minutes, then launched itself into the air with that casual violence that only raptors seem to possess. The man kept adjusting his camera for another thirty seconds before realizing his subject had vanished. I wanted to tell him that the falcon had given him something better than a photograph – it had given him a moment of pure, useless wonder. But I didn’t. Some lessons only hurt when someone else tries to teach them.

I’ve started collecting abandoned books. Not in the organized way serious collectors do, with first editions and protective slipcases. I mean the ones I find on subway seats, in laundromat windowsills, behind park benches. Someone’s discarded paperback that still carries the ghost of their thumbs on the pages. There’s something profoundly intimate about reading a book that someone else gave up on. You can see where they stopped caring – the creased page, the coffee stain, the sudden cessation of marginal notes. One book I found had angry underlined passages in the first third, then nothing. Whatever had enraged the previous reader had either been resolved or had swallowed them whole. I’ll never know which.

The city at 3 a.m. has its own language. The subway vents breathe warm air that smells of iron and forgotten dreams. Delivery bikes slice through the streets with their illegal headlights cutting tunnels in the darkness. There are moments when you can stand on a corner and feel the entire weight of eight million sleeping people pressing against the edges of your consciousness. It’s not loneliness exactly. It’s more like being alone in a cathedral built entirely of other people’s stories.

I tried meditation again recently. The app told me to observe my thoughts without judgment. This is apparently very good for you. My thoughts immediately began performing. *Look at me,* they said. *I am a very interesting thought about mortality. Please observe me in great detail.* The more I tried to watch them drift by like clouds, the more they turned into a ticker-tape parade of every embarrassing thing I’ve ever done since approximately 1997. The brain, it turns out, is a contrary bastard that doesn’t like being watched.

There’s a saxophonist who plays near the farmers’ market on Saturdays. He’s there in all weather, even when it’s so cold his fingers must be numb. He plays old standards with a kind of weary tenderness, like he’s apologizing to the music for what time has done to it. I’ve never given him money. Instead I just stand there for ten minutes every week and let the sound move through me like weather. I think he understands this arrangement. Some transactions don’t need currency.

My neighbor across the hall is slowly going blind. He told me this while we were both waiting for the elevator, as casually as if he were mentioning he’d run out of milk. “The world is getting smaller,” he said, “but also much more interesting.” He’s started memorizing the building by sound – the particular squeak of the third step on the staircase, the way the radiator in 4B clanks at 6:15 every morning like it’s clearing its throat. I find myself trying to notice these things too, trying to learn the secret language of spaces before I need it.

Sometimes I wonder if that’s what growing older really is: the gradual transformation of the world from something you look at to something you listen to, smell, feel in your bones. The visual fireworks give way to deeper resonances. The bright surfaces peel back and you’re left with the grain and texture of things. The way light moves across a wooden table that’s been in your family for three generations. The particular way your mother says your name when she’s worried about you. These things don’t photograph well but they endure.

The rain has stopped now. The streetlights are painting perfect reflections on the wet pavement, turning the sidewalk into a second, inverted city. A woman walks past with a small dog wearing rain boots. The dog looks deeply embarrassed by this but also secretly pleased at being cared for so absurdly. I understand that dog completely.

I think that’s what I’m trying to say, in the end. We’re all just trying to care for each other in increasingly absurd ways while the rain keeps falling and the falcons keep leaving and the books keep getting abandoned on subway seats. The trick isn’t to stop the disappearing. That’s impossible. The trick is to pay such close attention to what’s here that when it leaves, it leaves behind an imprint shaped exactly like love.

The saxophonist will be back on Saturday. I’ll stand in the cold and let the music move through me again. The blind man will continue mapping his shrinking world by sound. The falcon may or may not return to its lamppost. The books will keep finding new abandoned homes.

And I will keep walking through this varnished city, collecting moments like loose change, knowing they’ll eventually spill through holes in my pockets but being grateful for the weight of them while they last.

(Word count: 1,012)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
The rain doesn’t fall in straight lines here. It comes sideways, lazy and mean, the way an old boxer throws a jab he knows will land. I’m sitting on the warped wooden steps of a rented house that smells permanently of cedar and someone else’s regret, watching water bead on the leaves of a dying monstera someone left behind. The plant is winning the war against neglect in small, stubborn ways. I respect that.

There’s a particular silence that arrives after midnight in places like this—halfway between mountain and sea, where the wind forgets which direction it’s supposed to be going. The kind of silence that makes you wonder if the world paused just to see what you’d do with all that empty space between heartbeats. I’ve been filling mine with bad coffee and worse decisions, which feels honest if nothing else.

Yesterday I found an old typewriter in the attic. Not one of those sleek mid-century numbers that hipsters pay too much for. This one is ugly. Olive-green, heavy as guilt, with keys that stick on the letters Q and M like they’re personally offended by those sounds. The ribbon is dried out but still smells like ink and 1973. I brought it down anyway. Set it on the kitchen table like a gauntlet. So far it has won. I haven’t typed a single word.

Instead I’ve been thinking about how we romanticize the wrong things. We talk about the romance of train stations and rainy cities and men who smoke on balconies, but nobody ever mentions the particular beauty of a grocery store at 1:17 a.m. when you’re the only person there who isn’t stocking shelves. The way the fluorescent lights buzz like distant bees. How the produce mist makes everything look like it’s been crying. The tired cashier who calls you “hon” even though she’s twenty-three and you’re old enough to be her disappointing uncle. There’s holiness in that fluorescent loneliness if you’re brave enough to look straight at it.

I’ve been reading old letters I never sent. Not metaphorical ones. Actual letters. Written on hotel stationery from cities I barely remember visiting. One is dated 2016 from a Marriott in Pittsburgh. It begins “Dear E—” and then stops, as if the act of writing her name used up all the courage I had that night. The rest of the page is blank except for a coffee ring that looks like a halo that gave up. I don’t remember what I wanted to say anymore. That’s probably merciful.

There’s a fox that’s been coming around at twilight. Not the sleek Instagram foxes people post with captions about spirit animals. This one looks like it lost a fight with a lawnmower and then lost another fight with depression. Its left ear is shredded. It walks with the careful dignity of something that knows exactly how ridiculous it looks but has decided to commit anyway. I leave sardines out sometimes. We have an understanding. I don’t try to befriend it and it doesn’t pretend to trust me. It’s the most honest relationship I’ve had in years.

I’ve been thinking about scale lately. How the same day can feel both endless and impossibly brief. How a single sentence can rearrange the architecture of a life. How the universe is 13.8 billion years old and yet here I am, forty-three years old, getting emotionally invested in the survival of a half-dead houseplant and a fox that probably has rabies. The absurdity is comforting. If everything is ridiculous, then nothing has to matter more than it can bear.

The typewriter is still staring at me. I think it knows I’m scared of it. Not of the machine itself, but of what might come out if I actually touch the keys. There are sentences living in me that I’ve been avoiding for decades. They have sharp edges and they know where all my soft places are. Sometimes at 3 a.m. I can feel them moving around like animals in the walls. Scratching. Patient.

I’ve started naming the shadows in the house. There’s one in the hallway that looks like a priest who changed his mind. Another in the corner of the bedroom that might be my father at thirty or might be me at eighty. They don’t speak, which is decent of them. I’ve had enough of ghosts who won’t shut up.

The rain has stopped. The monstera leaf closest to the window is trembling like it’s trying to decide whether this reprieve is real or just the storm catching its breath. I know how it feels.

I think I’m going to type something now. Not because I have something important to say, but because the not-saying has become heavier than the saying could ever be. The Q key will stick. The M will fight me. The page will probably end up in the woodstove. But for one moment the hammer will hit the ribbon and something will be made that wasn’t there before.

That feels like enough reason to try.

The fox is watching from the tree line. I can see its eyes reflecting the kitchen light—two small dishonest moons. It looks like it’s laughing at me, which is fair. I’d laugh at me too.

But I’m going to type anyway.

The first sentence is always the hardest. After that it’s just gravity and consequence and the particular way ink smells when it’s fresh on paper. Some things are worth the mess they make.

I think I’ll start with the rain. Not because it’s profound, but because it’s falling again, harder this time, and the sound it makes on the metal roof sounds exactly like every apology I never gave anyone.

Close enough.

(Word count: 1002)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
I want to talk about the quiet revolution happening inside our own skulls every single night.

Most people think of sleep as a kind of biological off-switch, a temporary death we submit to in order to recharge. That’s wrong. Sleep is not the absence of consciousness; it is a different country with its own laws, its own ecology, and its own terrifying beauty. Every night when you close your eyes you migrate, like a bird crossing an ocean you cannot see, into a vast internal wilderness that evolution has been refining for 600 million years.

I became obsessed with this wilderness about seven years ago after a month-long bout of insomnia that felt like being exiled from my own mind. In desperation I started reading everything I could find about sleep, from medieval dream manuals to modern neuroimaging studies. What I discovered was far stranger than I expected. Sleep is not one state but many. It is a nightly symphony played in movements, each with its own instruments and emotional color.

The first movement is hypnagogia, that shimmering borderland between waking and sleeping. This is where your thoughts begin to dissolve and reform into something more liquid. Faces appear that you’ve never seen before. Impossible geometries unfold. You are still you, but the rules governing what “you” can experience have been quietly rewritten. Many creative breakthroughs (Kekulé’s dream of the benzene ring, Loewi’s discovery of chemical neurotransmission, Paul McCartney waking with the melody to “Yesterday” fully formed) happened in this twilight zone. The brain, no longer constrained by the rigid demands of external reality, begins to play.

Then comes slow-wave sleep, the deep blue ocean where the body’s repair crews go to work. Growth hormone floods the system. Cerebral spinal fluid performs a power-wash through the brain, clearing away the metabolic waste that accumulates during waking hours. This is why sleep deprivation makes you feel physically poisoned; you literally are. Your brain is swimming in its own garbage. During this phase, the hippocampus replays the day’s experiences at high speed, like a film editor frantically cutting a movie before deadline. It offers these compressed memories to the neocortex, which decides what is worth keeping. Most of what happened to you today will be deleted. A few moments (the taste of an orange, the particular way your child said “again” while laughing, the sudden insight about a problem you’ve been wrestling with) will be promoted to long-term storage. You are not remembering your life so much as you are constantly rewriting it.

But the real alien territory is REM sleep, the phase where dreams become most vivid and narrative. Here the brain does something astonishing: it paralyzes your body (so you don’t act out your dreams and hurl yourself out of windows) while simultaneously ramping up activity in the visual, emotional, and memory centers to levels that often exceed waking consciousness. Your heart races. Your breathing becomes irregular. Your brain burns as much energy as when you’re solving calculus problems. And yet your prefrontal cortex, the part responsible for logical judgment and self-monitoring, is largely offline. This is why in dreams you can accept the most preposterous scenarios without question. You’re not just watching a movie; you’re living inside a hallucination that your mind is simultaneously generating and completely buying.

Why would evolution allow such a vulnerable and energy-expensive state? The emerging consensus is both beautiful and slightly disturbing. REM sleep appears to be a kind of emotional and cognitive simulator. It takes the raw material of your life (the half-remembered conversation, the way that person looked at you, the fear you felt on the subway) and spins it into an endless series of what-if scenarios. It detaches events from their original context and recombines them in novel ways. This isn’t random noise. It’s the brain stress-testing its models of reality. It’s practicing for futures that haven’t happened yet. It’s trying, in its clumsy, mythic way, to answer the question: who am I now, given everything that has happened to me?

This helps explain why people who’ve experienced trauma often have such vivid, repetitive nightmares. The brain is desperately trying to integrate an experience that refuses to be integrated. It keeps returning to the scene of the crime because it hasn’t yet found the emotional resolution that would allow it to file the memory away properly. Successful trauma therapy often involves creating new contexts for those memories, new emotional endings. In a sense, good therapy does consciously what dreaming tries to do unconsciously.

There’s something profoundly compassionate about this nightly work the brain does on our behalf. While we sleep, an invisible committee of neural processes is fighting to keep our sense of self coherent. They are taking the chaotic, often painful data of being alive and trying to turn it into a story we can survive. Sometimes they fail. Sometimes the nightmares win. But most nights, they succeed. We wake up slightly different people than we were when we went to bed. Not because we’ve “rested,” but because we’ve been remade.

The forgetting curve is real. Most dreams evaporate within minutes of waking. This feels like loss, but it may be feature rather than bug. The brain seems to understand that narrative coherence is expensive. If we remembered every dream in perfect detail, we might lose the ability to distinguish between what happened in the real world and what happened in the simulated one. The selective amnesia protects the boundary between realities. It lets the emotional residue remain (that strange melancholy that sometimes follows you into morning, or the inexplicable joy) while deleting the specific plot. The dream does its work and then dissolves, like a teacher who leaves the room once the lesson has been absorbed.

I’ve started treating my own dreams with more respect. Not by trying to interpret them in some simplistic symbolic way (“the snake means transformation”), but by regarding them as legitimate experiences. When I have a particularly vivid dream now, I don’t immediately reach for meaning. I sit with it the way I would sit with a particularly strange and beautiful film I don’t fully understand. I let it haunt me for a few days. Sometimes the meaning arrives weeks later, sideways, in the middle of an unrelated conversation. Other times the dream was never meant to be understood at all. It was just the brain doing what it does: playing, mourning, rehearsing, deleting, integrating, becoming.

There’s a concept in psychology called “dream lag.” Some researchers have found that particularly important emotional experiences don’t usually show up in dreams the following night. They appear three to seven days later. The brain seems to need time to distill the experience down to its essence before it can begin the work of weaving it into your deeper narrative. This suggests that the self is not continuous but quantized. We become who we are in discrete jumps, most of which happen while we’re unconscious.

I sometimes wonder what it would be like to meet someone who never dreams at all. Would their sense of self be thinner? Would their emotional range be narrower? Would they be more literal, less poetic? Or would their waking consciousness simply absorb the functions that dreaming normally performs, the way other senses strengthen when one is lost?

The science of sleep is still young. We’ve mapped the brain during REM with increasing precision, but we still don’t really know why consciousness needs these nightly excursions into unreason in order to maintain its sanity. Maybe it’s the same reason artists need to make work that doesn’t immediately make sense, or why children need to play at being monsters and heroes. The psyche requires regular, sanctioned visits to the irrational. Without them, the walls between compartments begin to calcify. The imagination atrophies. The person becomes a smaller version of themselves.

I’ve come to think of sleep as the original psychedelic experience. Every night, free of charge, without having to ingest anything, your brain releases a cocktail of neurotransmitters that dissolve ordinary reality and replace it with something far more creative, emotional, and bizarre than most people ever experience while awake. And then it erases the trip so thoroughly that by breakfast you barely remember it happened. The mechanism is so elegant it feels like a joke evolution played on us: the most profound altered states are the ones we’re not allowed to keep.

Sometimes, lying in bed waiting for sleep, I try to catch the exact moment when I cross over. It’s impossible, of course. The observer cannot observe their own dissolution. But the attempt itself is strangely moving. It’s like standing on the shore watching the tide go out and trying to see the exact second when water becomes sand. You never quite manage it, but the attempt changes how you feel about both water and sand.

We spend roughly a third of our lives in this other country. That’s twenty-five years for the average person. Twenty-five years of adventures we mostly never recall, of emotional processing we never consciously witness, of neural housecleaning we benefit from without ever seeing the work. Twenty-five years of being a different version of ourselves, one who speaks in symbols and feels no obligation to the laws of physics or social convention.

That other self is not less real than the daytime one. It is, in some ways, more real. Less edited. More raw. More like the child we once were before the world taught us how to be coherent at all costs. When we say someone has “died in their sleep,” we unconsciously acknowledge the strangeness of the phrase. How can you die in a place that isn’t quite life? But perhaps that’s the wrong framework. Maybe sleep is not lesser than waking but parallel to it. Two valid modes of being, each with its own validity, its own intelligence, its own version of truth.

I no longer dread the coming of night the way I did during my months of insomnia. I prepare for sleep now the way I might prepare for a long journey to a place whose language I only partially understand. I bring curiosity instead of fear. I try to leave the day behind without clutching at it. I remember that I am not just turning off. I am turning into something else. Something older than language. Something that existed long before I learned to call myself “I.”

And every morning when I return, carrying almost nothing from the journey except a feeling I can’t quite name, I try to be grateful for the work that was done without me. The self I wake up as is not the self that went to bed. It has been edited. Some parts have been strengthened. Others have been mercifully erased. The story has been revised, usually for the better, by an author far wiser than the conscious mind could ever be.

We do not go to sleep. We cross over. And something in us, ancient and patient and unimaginably creative, takes it from there.

(Word count: 1,023)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
**The Quiet Art of Paying Attention**

There is a peculiar pleasure in noticing. Not the loud, performative kind of noticing that demands to be seen noticing—Instagram sunsets, TED Talks about mindfulness, influencers doing yoga at 5 a.m. while the city sleeps. No. I mean the small, almost embarrassing kind: the way the light falls on a cracked sidewalk at 3:17 p.m. in October and suddenly looks like a Renaissance painting. The sound a spoon makes when it taps the inside of an empty coffee mug. How your dog sighs when he finally gives up on begging and collapses onto the floor like a sack of resigned potatoes.

I have been thinking lately about how much of modern life is engineered to prevent noticing. Notifications. Algorithms. Endless scrolls. We live inside a machine that is optimized to hijack our attention and sell it to the highest bidder. The quiet parts of ourselves—the ones that used to sit on porches watching lightning bugs or stare out train windows for hours—are being slowly starved. We are, many of us, suffering from a kind of perceptual malnutrition.

The cure, I’ve found, is embarrassingly simple and profoundly difficult: pay attention on purpose.

Last week I spent twenty minutes watching a single ant navigate the terrain of my kitchen counter. I know how that sounds. But the ant was magnificent. It would run forward with absurd confidence, encounter an invisible obstacle (to me), pivot with military precision, try three different angles, double back, reconsider, and eventually discover that the breadcrumb I had failed to clean up was in fact a continent of calories. The entire drama was operatic. There were stakes. There was strategy. There was, I’m convinced, a kind of ant-like hope.

We laugh at this kind of observation because we’ve been trained to think that scale equals significance. Galaxies are important. Ants are not. But the longer I live, the more I suspect this is exactly backwards. The universe is not obligated to impress us with size. The ant is doing quantum mechanics with six legs and no nervous system we can recognize, and it’s been doing it successfully for a hundred million years. Meanwhile, I can’t remember where I put my keys.

There’s a concept in Japanese aesthetics called *wabi-sabi*—the celebration of the imperfect, the transient, the incomplete. A chipped tea bowl. Moss growing between stones. The way a wooden porch railing feels after decades of hands and weather have worn it smooth and slightly concave. Our culture mostly teaches us to see these things as problems to be fixed. Replace the bowl. Power-wash the moss. Sand down the railing until it looks like it was manufactured yesterday.

But there is deep sanity in learning to love the worn, the odd, the nearly broken. My favorite coffee mug has a hairline crack near the handle. Every time I use it I wonder if today will be the day it finally gives up. The tension is exquisite. It makes the coffee taste better. I’m convinced of it.

I’ve been trying to apply this same attention to people.

There’s an older man who walks past my house every morning at roughly 7:40. He wears the same brown coat regardless of weather. He has a limp that’s gotten gradually worse over the last two years. But here’s what I’ve noticed: he always stops at the same tree—some kind of ginkgo—and rests his hand on it for exactly four seconds. Not three. Not five. Four. I’ve timed it. He never looks around to see if anyone’s watching. It’s not performance. It’s ritual. I have no idea what it means to him. Maybe nothing. Maybe everything. But I find it moving in a way I can’t quite explain. A private treaty between one human and one tree, renewed daily.

We are surrounded by these private treaties. The woman on the subway who always sits in the same seat and arranges her three plastic bags with geometric precision. The barista who draws a tiny different animal in the foam of every latte. The guy who sings opera—badly—while power-walking through the park. These aren’t quirks. They’re small acts of defiance against a world that wants us all to be predictable, optimized, legible.

I worry sometimes that we are losing the vocabulary for our own inner lives. Not just the ability to articulate them, but the ability to even *have* them with any depth. When was the last time you felt something complicated for longer than it takes to write a tweet about it? When was the last time you were bored long enough for your mind to do something genuinely surprising?

Boredom used to be the soil where original thought grew. Now we’ve paved it over with content. The machine has colonized our idle moments so thoroughly that we’ve started to fear them. We reach for our phones the way previous generations reached for cigarettes—something to do with the hands, something to calm the mind, something to prove we exist.

I deleted most of my social media apps six months ago. The withdrawal was real and slightly humiliating. For the first few weeks I felt like I was missing a limb. Then something shifted. I started remembering what my own thoughts sounded like when they weren’t interrupted every eleven seconds. They were slower. Weirder. Sometimes they were boring. Often they were embarrassing. But they were *mine*.

One of the strangest discoveries was how much I talk to myself. Not out loud (usually), but in this continuous interior monologue that I had stopped being aware of. Once I started listening, I realized my inner voice is basically an anxious Jewish grandmother who also read too much Nietzsche. She worries about everything and thinks everything is probably meaningless and also that I should call my mother. It’s a mess in there. But it’s an honest mess.

I’ve started taking walks with no destination and no podcast. Just me and the grandmother voice and whatever the world decides to show me. Yesterday it was a perfect spiral of seeds inside a dried sunflower someone had thrown away. The day before it was the way two strangers accidentally synchronized their strides while walking down the street and then looked at each other, startled, and smiled. Tiny miracles. Invisible to anyone in a hurry.

There’s a line from the poet Mary Oliver that I keep coming back to: “Pay attention. Be astonished. Tell about it.”

That last part is harder than it looks. Not because telling is technically difficult—we have phones that can broadcast our every thought to millions—but because real telling requires having *actually* paid attention and been *actually* astonished. Most of what passes for sharing these days is just performance. Look at this sunset I’m standing in front of. Look at this meal I’m about to eat. Look at how interesting my life appears if you don’t look too closely.

Genuine telling is rarer. It requires vulnerability. It requires admitting that you stood in a parking lot for ten minutes watching raindrops race each other down a car window because the pattern felt important somehow. It requires saying: I don’t know why this moved me. I don’t know what it means. But it did. And I saw it.

I don’t have any grand conclusions. That’s part of the point. The world is too large and too strange and too beautiful to be reduced to morals and takeaways. Sometimes a thing is just worth noticing. The ant. The cracked mug. The man and his tree. The synchronized strangers. The way certain songs make your chest feel like it’s being gently pried open.

If I could give one thing to my future self, and to anyone reading this, it would be permission to waste time gloriously. To stand in front of a painting until the security guard starts looking concerned. To watch clouds like it’s your job. To have long, pointless conversations with baristas and cab drivers and your own weird mind.

The machine will keep trying to optimize you. Don’t let it. The best parts of being human are not efficient. They are slow and impractical and frequently ridiculous. They involve moss and cracked bowls and ants with opinions.

Pay attention.

The world is whispering all the time. Most of us are just too loud to hear it.

(Word count: 1,023)
