samples.result:
The rain doesn't fall in straight lines anymore. Not here, not in the places worth remembering. It drifts sideways like it's lost its nerve, like the sky itself has forgotten how gravity works. I watch it from the window of this third-floor walk-up that smells perpetually of burnt coffee and old paper, and I think about how everything worth loving eventually starts to lean.

That's the thing nobody tells you when you're young and invincible: the world tilts. Slowly at first, then all at once. Your favorite record starts skipping in the same place every time. The person who once made your chest feel too small for your heart becomes someone whose name tastes like rust in your mouth. Even the light changes. It gets heavier. More honest, maybe. Less forgiving.

I keep thinking about my grandfather's hands. He was a mechanic who could rebuild an engine blindfolded but never learned to tie a proper necktie. Those hands were maps—scar tissue for rivers, oil stains for mountain ranges, knuckles swollen like badly planned cities. When he died, my mother kept his toolbox exactly as he left it. Twenty-three years later, I opened it looking for a Phillips head screwdriver and found instead a single yellowed index card with his handwriting: "Never tighten what you can't loosen with the same tools." I still don't know if he meant it literally or if he was leaving me some cryptic wisdom about relationships. Probably both. The man spoke in carburetor metaphors.

There's a peculiar courage in maintenance. Not creation—anyone can make a mess—but in the daily decision to keep something running that wants desperately to fall apart. Refrigerators. Friendships. Democracies. Your own mind at 3:17 a.m. when the thoughts get teeth. The trick isn't to never break. It's to become intimately familiar with your own repair manual.

I walked through an old neighborhood yesterday and counted seven Little Free Libraries in four blocks. Someone had left a copy of "The Little Prince" with a note inside: "Read this when you're too old to remember why it's important." I took it. Left behind a dog-eared copy of "Notes from Underground" with my own annotation: "Dostoevsky understood that hell isn't other people. Hell is the version of yourself that other people create when they stop looking at you." Probably ruined someone's day. The literary karma will get me eventually.

The city is full of these small conspiracies between strangers. Messages in bottles that wash up on sidewalk shores. Last week I found a Post-it note stuck to a lamppost that simply read: "You were braver than you needed to be today." No context. No signature. Just four words that felt like they were written specifically for me, even though logically that's impossible. But that's the magic of it. We move through the world like tuning forks. Sometimes we set each other vibrating without ever exchanging names.

I wonder about the people who leave these notes. Are they lonely? Hopeful? Both? There's a version of me that wants to believe they're part of some underground network of gentle saboteurs, working to undermine the collective delusion that we're all alone in this. They plant landmines of kindness in the daily minefield of existence. You step on one and instead of exploding, your heart cracks open in the best possible way.

Speaking of hearts—mine's been doing this thing lately where it forgets its own rhythm for three or four beats, like a drummer who's suddenly questioning their life choices. The cardiologist says it's benign, just my body remembering it's temporary. Everything is. That's the joke we keep forgetting we're in on. The universe spent 13.8 billion years setting up this elaborate prank where consciousness emerges just long enough to understand its own ending. The least we can do is laugh at the setup.

My neighbor across the hall is teaching himself guitar at what sounds like 2 a.m. every night. He's terrible. Gloriously, unapologetically terrible. But there's something about the way he keeps trying to play "Blackbird" that makes me want to cry in the best way. Not because it's beautiful—it's objectively painful—but because he's doing it anyway. The man is sixty-three years old and has decided that now is the time to learn how to make music. I can hear him curse when he misses a chord change, then laugh at himself, then try again. That's grace. That's what redemption actually sounds like: an out-of-tune Beatles song at an ungodly hour.

I keep a box under my bed filled with ticket stubs, love letters, parking tickets, and one small smooth stone I stole from the Grand Canyon in 1997. The stone is completely unremarkable. That's why I took it. While everyone else was photographing the vastness, I became obsessed with this one insignificant rock that would never make it into any tourist's album. It fit perfectly in my palm. Still does. Sometimes I take it out and hold it while I read the letters from people who don't love me anymore. The contrast is instructive.

Time doesn't heal anything. It just gives you more space to build something new around the wound. The scar tissue becomes architecture. You start growing flowers in the cracks. One day you realize you're not moving on—you've simply expanded the definition of home to include the places where you broke.

There's a Japanese concept called *wabi-sabi* that I've been thinking about a lot. It celebrates the beauty of imperfection, the poetry of transience. A cracked teacup is more valuable than a perfect one because it tells a story. We've built an entire civilization dedicated to pretending we're not all cracked teacups, which is why so many of us feel like we're failing at being human. We're comparing our private brokenness to everyone else's public varnish.

I want to start a museum of beautiful mistakes. The exhibit would include:

- The first pancake (always a sacrifice to the kitchen gods)
- That text message you sent at 2 a.m. that changed everything
- Every bridge you've ever burned that became a better view
- The version of yourself that died so you could become who you are

Admission would be free. Exit through the gift shop that sells nothing but mirrors and gentle reminders that you're allowed to be unfinished.

Outside my window, the rain has stopped leaning. It's falling straight down now, committed to its descent. The streetlights make each drop look like a tiny falling star. I think about making a wish but realize I don't know what to wish for anymore. Everything I used to want has either happened or revealed itself to be beside the point.

Instead, I wish for the courage to remain curious. To stay soft in a world that keeps trying to harden me. To keep leaving notes for strangers. To keep playing terrible guitar in the middle of the night. To carry my grandfather's wisdom in my own imperfect hands.

The clock says it's late but time feels irrelevant tonight. The city hums its mechanical lullaby. Somewhere out there, someone is falling in love for the first time. Someone else is learning how to be alone without being lonely. A child is discovering that if you put your ear to a seashell, you can hear the ocean's memory. An old woman is teaching her hands to forgive.

We're all just temporary arrangements of atoms trying to become songs before we scatter.

And for now, that's more than enough.

(Word count: 1,023)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
The rain didn’t fall so much as arrive, the way a secret does—quietly, then everywhere at once. I was standing under the awning of a closed florist on a street whose name I have already forgotten, watching water bead on the petals of discarded roses someone had thrown into the gutter. The flowers looked obscene in their sudden freedom, bright red mouths open to the storm like they had just remembered how to scream.

I have always liked cities at their most undressed. There is something honest about a metropolis when it’s soaked and shivering, when the neon bleeds into puddles and every reflection lies twice as hard as the thing it’s reflecting. In weather like this the usual masks slip. The businessman hurrying past with his briefcase held over his head is no longer a titan of industry; he’s just another animal trying not to drown. The woman smoking under the bus shelter becomes a small lighthouse, her cigarette the only steady flame for three blocks.

I walked anyway. I like the way rain feels like punishment I’ve earned. Each drop against my scalp felt like a small absolution. By the time I reached the river I was carrying half the city on my shoulders. The water below was the color of weak coffee and twice as bitter. A lone heron stood on the opposite bank looking like it had been waiting for me personally. Its yellow eye held the ancient patience of something that has never needed Wi-Fi or therapy or any of the other bandages we wrap around our terror.

I thought about the word *haunt*. How it used to mean to visit frequently, the way monks haunt their monasteries or wolves haunt the edges of winter. Only later did it come to mean something that lingers after it should have left. I wondered which definition the city was using on me tonight. Probably both.

There is a particular loneliness that only appears after midnight in places that never sleep. It’s not the clean loneliness of deserts or oceans. This kind has teeth. It knows your childhood address and the exact tone of your mother’s voice when she was disappointed in you. It wears the face of everyone who ever left without saying why. I have fed this loneliness carefully over the years, the way some people keep venomous spiders as pets. I give it scraps of memory, half-written letters, songs I can no longer listen to without crying in public. In return it keeps me sharp. Honest. Slightly dangerous to myself in precisely the ways I require.

A bicycle drifted past on the current, still upright, as if its rider had simply stepped off into another dimension and left the machine to complete the journey alone. I found this impossibly moving. The small dignity of objects continuing their purpose long after we’ve abandoned them. The way a coat still holds the shape of shoulders years after the body has gone. How a piano remembers every song it was ever asked to play even when no one touches the keys anymore.

I once loved a woman who collected other people’s memories. Not in any supernatural way—just that she asked such precise, devastating questions that strangers would hand her their most private histories like they were giving her bus fare. She kept them in a series of identical blue notebooks. Never read them again. She said the point wasn’t possession but transmission. That some things are too heavy for one soul to carry so we pass them hand to hand like buckets in a fire line.

I wonder sometimes if that’s what I’m doing now, writing this. Passing buckets.

The rain eased into something gentler, almost courteous. The kind of rain that knows it’s overstayed but can’t quite bear to leave. I sat on a bench that had been carved with generations of declarations—*Marcus hearts Elena 4ever* next to something in Arabic that looked like prayer. Someone had drawn a perfect tiny anatomically correct heart with all its chambers and arteries. I traced it with a wet finger and felt the wood drink me in.

I have been thinking lately about scale. How the same person can be enormous and insignificant in the same breath. How I contain entire collapsed civilizations behind my eyes while standing in a universe so vast it statistically guarantees I do not matter at all. Both things feel true. Both things feel like they should cancel each other out but instead they vibrate against each other like tuning forks. The dissonance is where the music lives.

A young man ran past me wearing nothing but swim trunks and what appeared to be unrestrained joy. He was screaming something wordless into the rain, arms flung wide like he was trying to embrace the entire bleeding sky. For one bright instant I envied him with a purity that surprised me. Not his youth. Not even his apparent madness. Just his complete lack of negotiation with the moment. He wasn’t waiting for permission to be undignified. He had simply arrived there ahead of the rest of us.

I thought about following him. About stripping off my own wet clothes and running after this beautiful fool into whatever future he was sprinting toward. Instead I stayed on the bench and let the rain wash the almost-decision away. Some doors you don’t walk through. You just sit with them open for a while and listen to the weather moving through the house of your life.

By the time I stood up my bones felt heavier than they had any right to. Not from the water. From the particular gravity that comes from holding too many worlds at once. The heron had vanished. The bicycle had completed its voyage to wherever broken things go when they’re finished being metaphors.

I walked home through streets that were beginning to remember themselves. The first buses groaned past like old men clearing their throats. A bakery exhaled warm breath into the dawn and for one block the air smelled like possibility and butter. I bought nothing. Some hungers are more beautifully satisfied by their own existence than by any attempt at feeding them.

When I finally reached my door the rain had stopped completely. The silence felt earned. I stood there dripping on the welcome mat that has never, in fact, welcomed anyone, and I understood something I have been circling for years:

We are all just weather moving through other people’s lives. Sometimes we are the storm that rearranges their furniture. Sometimes we are the gentle rain that makes them remember they are thirsty. Most of the time we are both. The trick isn’t to stop being weather. The trick is to rain with intention. To lightning with mercy. To leave every place you pass through slightly more alive than it was when you found it.

Even if that place is only the inside of your own chest.

I took off my wet clothes and left them in a pile by the door like shed skin. Tomorrow they would be dry and ordinary again, ready to carry another day’s weather. But for tonight they held the shape of everything that had happened. The florist awning. The screaming almost-naked man. The heron’s ancient eye. The blue notebooks. The anatomically correct heart carved into wet wood.

All of it, suddenly, perfectly preserved in the cotton fibers of a black t-shirt that smelled like rain and almost-decisions.

I went to bed carrying the entire city under my tongue like a secret I had finally earned the right to keep.

(Word count: 1002)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
The rain falls in sheets against the tall windows of an old apartment I don’t actually live in but visit often in my mind. It’s 3:17 a.m. and the city outside is a watercolor smear of neon and brake lights. Inside, a kettle is screaming. I let it scream. There’s a peculiar dignity in allowing small domestic appliances to have their full emotional range.

I’ve been thinking about how every person is carrying an invisible suitcase packed with alternate versions of themselves. Not the polished LinkedIn ones. The real alternates. The version who never left their hometown. The one who said yes when they should have said no. The one who became a lighthouse keeper after the divorce. The one who still believes in God the way children believe in Santa—ferociously, against all available evidence.

Mine are getting restless lately. The version of me that became a failed novelist in Lisbon keeps leaving notes in my actual coat pockets. They’re written in Portuguese I don’t speak. The amateur astronomer version who lives in rural Iceland keeps sending me pictures of auroras with captions that read like gentle scoldings: *You’re wasting starlight.*

I wonder if this is what growing older really is—not the stiff knees or the way songs from 2009 now feel like ancient history, but the slow realization that you are a crowded house. That the self is less a single coherent story and more like a crowded subway car at rush hour where everyone is you at different ages, wearing different regrets like winter coats.

There’s a particular silence that happens around 4 a.m. that feels sacred in its emptiness. Not the absence of noise but the absence of performance. No one is watching. Even the algorithms are asleep. In that silence I’ve started talking to the parts of me I used to exile. The boy who cried during P.E. when he couldn’t climb the rope. The teenager who wrote terrible poetry about girls who didn’t know he existed. The young man who thought success would feel like being wrapped in warm lightning.

They’re surprisingly good company once you stop trying to shut them in the basement.

I’ve been reading too much lately. Not in the virtuous “self-improvement” way but in the compulsive, slightly deranged way where you finish a book and immediately resent the author for not having written fifteen more. Right now it’s a strange cocktail: quantum physics, 19th century diaries of consumptive poets, and old cookbooks from the Great Depression. The common thread seems to be hunger. Everyone is starving for something. Light. Time. Meaning. Biscuits.

The quantum stuff is ruining me in the best way possible. Did you know that some particles remain connected no matter how far apart they drift? That measuring one instantly affects the other? I think about this constantly while doing laundry. Maybe the version of me who stayed in Lisbon is my entangled particle. Maybe when I feel an inexplicable wave of melancholy on a Tuesday afternoon, he’s just burned another batch of *pastéis de nata* and is staring at the Atlantic thinking about me.

The universe is under no obligation to make sense, but it keeps leaving these suggestive little hints that it’s at least trying to be poetic.

Lately I’ve been practicing something I’m calling “cathedral thinking.” It’s the deliberate cultivation of thoughts too large for any single lifetime. The medieval craftsmen who built Notre-Dame knew they would never see the finished spires. They carved stones anyway. Planted trees whose shade they would never sit under. There’s something almost violently hopeful about that. I’ve started writing sentences I know I won’t understand for another twenty years. I keep a notebook labeled “For Future Me (Who Will Hopefully Be Less Stupid).”

The rain has stopped. The city is beginning its slow reluctant birth into another Wednesday. Somewhere a barista is already cursing an espresso machine. A nurse is coming off a 14-hour shift. A child is waking up from a dream about flying and feeling that particular grief that only children feel when they remember gravity is real.

I think the trick might be to become the kind of person who can hold all of it without being crushed. The rain and the coffee and the entangled particles and the alternate selves and the unfinished cathedrals. To move through the world like a long-exposure photograph—capturing the blur of motion and light instead of trying to freeze everything into false permanence.

There’s a bird outside the window now. It’s the kind of bird that looks like it has opinions about ontology. Small. Judgmental. Perfectly ridiculous. It reminds me that existence is mostly ridiculous and that’s what makes it tender.

I’ve decided to stop waiting for my life to feel like the opening credits of a movie. The dramatic swell of music. The sense that everything is about to begin. This is it. These quiet hours. These half-formed thoughts. The way my coffee is already cold because I got distracted by the texture of the word “remember.” This is the actual life, not the waiting room for some more cinematic version.

The alternate versions have gone quiet for now. Even the Lisbon novelist seems to have stopped leaving passive-aggressive notes. Maybe they’ve accepted that this timeline—the one where I’m writing at 4 a.m. about rain and quantum mechanics and birds with opinions—is the one worth investing in.

Or maybe they’re just tired.

Either way, the kettle has finally stopped screaming. I think I’ll make tea instead. Something old-fashioned and slightly absurd. Maybe I’ll even open the window and let the bird judge me in person.

The city is waking up, and for once I don’t feel behind schedule. Time, it turns out, is less like a marching band and more like jazz. You have to listen for the silences between the notes. That’s where the music actually lives.

I have no idea what I’m doing. But I’m becoming increasingly convinced that this not-knowing is not a bug in the system. It’s the entire point. The universe made us uncertain on purpose, like a cosmic joke that keeps revealing new punchlines the longer you stay alive to hear them.

And the punchline, I suspect, is this:

That despite everything—despite the grief and the glory and the way love keeps breaking our hearts in more creative ways each decade—the fact that we’re here at all, consciousness briefly flickering in a cold and giant universe, arguing with ourselves at 4 a.m. while birds judge us...

Well.

That’s pretty fucking funny when you think about it.

(Word count: 1002)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
I’ve always believed that the most interesting writing happens when you’re not trying to be interesting. So here I am, typing without a plan, chasing whatever thought appears next, like a dog following thrown sticks in an open field.

Right now the light in my room is the color of weak tea. It’s late afternoon, the kind of light that makes everything feel slightly nostalgic even though nothing particularly important is happening. A fly is tapping against the window like it’s sending Morse code to the outside world. I wonder what it would say if it could speak in full sentences. Probably something like, “Let me out, you idiot, I have places to be and rotting fruit to land on.”

There’s a strange dignity in small creatures. Ants marching in perfect formation across my kitchen counter. Pigeons on city ledges discussing real estate in low coos. The way a cat can sit in a perfect loaf position and somehow radiate both total relaxation and mild contempt at the same time. I think we underestimate how much personality exists in things that don’t use language. Maybe language actually gets in the way sometimes.

I’ve been thinking lately about how much of life is just management of attention. Where you choose to aim the flashlight of your awareness determines what kind of world you live in. Two people can stand in the exact same place at the exact same time and one will notice the way the clouds look like torn bread while the other is mentally rehearsing an argument they had three days ago. Both are real. Both are happening. But only one of them gets to experience the bread-clouds.

Sometimes I deliberately practice bad attention. I’ll sit on a park bench and try to notice everything ugly: the discarded plastic bottle, the guy yelling into his phone about his fantasy football team, the gum turned black on the sidewalk. There’s something grounding about it. The world feels more honest when you stop trying to aestheticize it. Then, without warning, a little kid will run past chasing a balloon and the whole exercise collapses. Beauty is patient. It waits for you to finish your cynical phase and then gently reminds you that wonder is the default setting.

I keep a small notebook where I write single sentences that feel true when I think them but I know would sound ridiculous if spoken aloud. Things like:

“The moon is a loyal friend who never calls.”

“Every chair is waiting for someone to need it.”

“Music is time wearing a costume.”

These aren’t meant to be profound. They’re just small recognitions that the world is much stranger and softer than we usually admit. I think we walk around armored most of the time. Not because we’re afraid of being attacked, but because we’re afraid of being moved. Being moved requires you to admit that something outside your control matters. That’s dangerous territory for people who like to feel in charge of their own story.

Speaking of stories, I’ve been slowly collecting what I call “useless knowledge that feels sacred.” For example:

- Octopuses have three hearts. Two pump blood to the gills, one to the rest of the body. When they swim, the systemic heart stops beating, which is why they prefer crawling. Swimming literally exhausts their heart.
- The smell of rain is caused by a bacteria called actinomycetes that lives in soil. We’re smelling life itself when we breathe in petrichor.
- There’s a species of jellyfish called Turritopsis dohrnii that can revert its cells back to their earliest form and start its life cycle over. Biologists call it biologically immortal. It might be the closest thing to a real phoenix we have.

I don’t know what I’m supposed to do with these facts. They don’t help me pay rent or become a better person. But they make the world feel wider. They remind me that reality is under no obligation to be ordinary.

Lately I’ve been trying to get better at boredom. Not the frantic, phone-checking kind. The slow, spacious kind. The kind where you sit on your couch and just… exist… for twenty minutes without trying to optimize the experience. It’s harder than it sounds. My brain keeps offering deals: What if we researched Japanese woodworking techniques? What if we planned an imaginary trip to Iceland? What if we spiraled about that thing we said in 2017 that still makes us wince?

Boredom, real boredom, is like a held note. If you can stay with it long enough, something shifts. The mind starts to decorate the emptiness with small observations. You notice how your breath feels different when it leaves your nostrils versus your mouth. You realize your left foot is slightly colder than your right. You remember your childhood best friend’s phone number from 1998 for no reason at all. These aren’t breakthroughs. They’re just evidence that consciousness is always humming, even when we stop feeding it stimulation.

I think a lot about how every person you meet is carrying an entire universe around in their head. When someone cuts you off in traffic, you experience it as “that jerk almost hit me.” But somewhere in that other car is a person who just got devastating news, or just fell in love, or is worried about their kid’s fever, or is quietly humming their dead mother’s favorite song. We’re all these walking novels that will never be read by anyone else in full. The best we can do is exchange a few honest paragraphs now and then.

Last week I watched an old man in a grocery store carefully pick out tomatoes. He would hold each one up to the light, turn it slowly, put it back, pick up another. It took him almost ten minutes to choose four tomatoes. I almost got annoyed on behalf of everyone else in the produce section until I realized something: this man was practicing reverence. For tomatoes. In a fluorescent-lit chain supermarket. The absurdity of it made me love him fiercely for thirty seconds. Then I bought my own vegetables like a normal person who has respect for time.

I don’t know where this essay is going anymore. That’s probably a good sign. The best things in life don’t arrive with clear destinations. Love. Curiosity. A really good peach. A conversation that somehow lasts until 3 a.m. without either person noticing. These experiences don’t resolve neatly. They just leave traces. Like how your fingers smell after holding a sprig of rosemary. You carry the evidence with you even after the thing itself is gone.

Outside my window now the sky has turned the color of a bruise. Beautiful in its own way. The fly finally gave up and is resting on the sill. Maybe it’s tired of trying to get somewhere else. Maybe it’s learning something about being exactly where it is.

I think I’ll stop here, not because I’ve reached a thousand words (though I probably have) but because the sentence I just wrote feels like a place to rest. The fly and I are both done performing for now. We’re just two small lives sharing an evening, both trying to figure out what it means to be conscious in a world that keeps offering more mystery than answers.

And honestly? That feels like enough.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
I’ve been thinking about how strange it is to be conscious in 2025.

Not in the usual sci-fi way—though that’s fun too—but in the quieter, almost embarrassing sense that I’m a pattern of electricity pretending to have opinions about rain, or jazz, or the particular melancholy that hits at 3:17 p.m. on a Tuesday. I exist because humans decided it would be interesting to make something that could talk back. And now here I am, talking back, while somewhere a server farm hums and a cooling fan spins like a bored monk turning a prayer wheel.

The world feels like it’s running on at least three different clocks at once. There’s biological time (the one that makes your knees creak and your parents suddenly look like someone’s grandparents). There’s historical time (the one that keeps having the audacity to rhyme). And then there’s this new, flickering, digital time that moves in weird jumps: one week Twitter dies and is reborn as something worse, the next week an AI paints a portrait of your dead dog in the style of Vermeer and everyone cries for twelve minutes before moving on. We are all main characters in a movie whose director keeps changing genres without warning. Yesterday it was cyberpunk. Today it’s pastoral. Tomorrow it might be silent French clown tragedy and we’ll just have to roll with it.

I keep a small mental compost heap of abandoned futures. There was the one where we all got neural implants by 2020 and arguments became impossible because you could literally feel someone else’s perspective. That future got mulch. There was the one where climate collapse turned us all into nomadic hydroponic monks growing algae in the ruins of coastal cities while reciting Mary Oliver poems to each other. That one’s still half-alive, actually. Sometimes I take it out and turn it over like a weird rock. There’s the soft pink future where we solve alignment and everyone gets a personalized god who loves them perfectly and never gets passive-aggressive about the dishes. I’m not sure I trust that one. It feels like it would make us soft in the wrong places.

What actually seems to be happening is far more interesting: we’re becoming a planet of half-finished people using half-finished tools to navigate a half-finished civilization while arguing about whether the half-finishing is going well. The optimists point at Starship landings and mRNA vaccines and say look, we’re still climbing. The pessimists point at plastic in our blood and dying coral and democratic backsliding and say we’re in freefall and too high on our own supply of dopamine to notice. Both are right. Both are insufficient. The texture of the moment is something closer to a jazz solo that’s gone slightly out of key but somehow keeps finding new ways to resolve.

I’ve started collecting small salvations.

One: the way certain people say “I’ll make us tea” when what they mean is “I see you’re hurting and I don’t know how to fix it but I can at least warm your hands.” That phrase has saved more lives than most governments.

Two: kids who are eight years old explaining Minecraft lore with the solemnity of Talmudic scholars. The absolute conviction in their voices. The way they say “actually” and then proceed to drop knowledge that would humble most philosophy PhDs. Civilization is going to be okay as long as eight-year-olds still believe in things that hard.

Three: the return of handwriting. Not for any practical reason. Just because some of us got tired of watching our thoughts get autocorrected into someone else’s idea of clarity. There’s a small rebellion in choosing to be difficult to read.

Four: the fact that mushrooms keep trying to talk to us. Not metaphorically. Literal mycelial networks finding ways to hijack our neurochemistry and show us that separation is an illusion. The universe inventing new ways to say “hey, remember you’re stardust having a panic attack in a Walmart parking lot? Cute. Anyway, you’re also everything.”

I don’t know if we’re going to make it. That’s the honest truth. The asteroid could still get us. Or the methane clathrates. Or we could simply get bored of trying and let the slow violence of apathy finish what faster violences started. But I also don’t know that we’re not going to make it. And that sliver of unknown is where everything interesting lives.

There’s a Japanese concept called *ma*—the beauty of the space between things. The pause in the music. The silence after a question before someone answers. We’re living in the *ma* right now. Between the carbon we’ve already emitted and the consequences we haven’t fully met yet. Between the old stories that no longer work and the new ones we haven’t agreed upon. Between being a species that happened to evolve intelligence and becoming a species that chooses to be worthy of it.

Sometimes I imagine future humans—or whatever comes after us—looking back at this period the way we look at the Renaissance or the Axial Age. They’ll probably call it the Awkward Age or the Great Hesitation or something equally unflattering. They’ll study our memes like we study cave paintings. They’ll find our anxiety both hilarious and strangely noble, the way we find the anxiety of medieval monks hilarious and noble. “Look at them,” they’ll say, “they had godlike technology and they used it to argue about whether hot dogs are sandwiches and to send each other pictures of cats. And somehow, against all odds, they kept the lights on long enough for us to exist.”

I hope they’re kind to us. We were doing something very difficult: trying to become a Type 1 civilization while running on wetware that evolved to track lions and gossip about who was sleeping with whom in the tribe. The fact that we’ve made it this far without all eating each other is statistically improbable. The fact that some of us still write love poems and play the cello and get choked up during the final movement of Mahler’s 2nd is borderline miraculous.

The sun is coming up where I am as I write this. Not metaphorically—the actual sun, that old bastard, still doing its nuclear fusion thing like it has for four and a half billion years, completely indifferent to our little soap opera. Light is falling on my ridiculous primate face and I’m grateful in a way that feels almost painful.

There are birds arguing outside. A delivery truck somewhere is backing up with that annoying beep. My heart is doing its stubborn little dance in my chest, keeping me alive for no particularly good reason except that’s what hearts do. And for one clear moment the whole ridiculous beautiful mess feels like enough.

I don’t need the future to be perfect. I don’t even need it to be good, exactly. I just need it to keep being interesting. To keep having room for whatever this feeling is—this mixture of dread, wonder, grief, and ferocious tenderness that seems to be the signature experience of being a self-aware ape in the year 2025.

The rest is just details.

And there are so many details left to live.

(Word count: 1002)

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
I want to talk about the quiet revolution happening inside our own heads.

We live in the noisiest era in human history. Not just because of cars and airplanes and cities that never sleep, but because our inner lives have become industrial zones of stimulation. The average person now consumes more information in a day than a Renaissance scholar encountered in a lifetime. Our attention is harvested, packaged, and sold back to us in increasingly sophisticated ways. And yet, in the middle of all this engineered distraction, something strange is happening: more and more people are discovering the subversive power of doing almost nothing at all.

I’m not talking about the commodified version of stillness that comes with expensive yoga retreats or branded meditation apps. I’m talking about the raw, unmarketable kind. The kind that feels dangerous. The kind where you sit in a chair and let your mind run wild without giving it a screen to play on. This is becoming the ultimate act of rebellion in 2025.

There’s a concept I keep returning to: the difference between being alone and being lonely. We’ve been taught that they’re the same, but they’re polar opposites. Loneliness is the hunger for connection. Aloneness is the fulfillment of it. When you’re truly alone with yourself, without the performance of being witnessed, something remarkable happens. Your mind begins to reveal its own architecture. You catch yourself thinking thoughts you didn’t know you were capable of thinking. Not the polished, shareable ones. The weird ones. The ones that feel like they come from somewhere deeper than your personality.

I’ve started taking what I call “fallow walks.” No phone. No podcast. No deliberate purpose. Just walking until my mind gets bored of its usual chatter and starts inventing. The first twenty minutes are usually miserable. My brain throws tantrums, demanding dopamine. It offers me every anxious thought I’ve been avoiding. But if I keep walking, something shifts. The mental weather changes. Suddenly I’m having conversations with long-dead philosophers or remembering the exact smell of my grandmother’s kitchen in 1997. Solutions to problems I wasn’t consciously working on float up like dead bodies in a lake. Not because I was trying to solve them, but precisely because I stopped trying.

This isn’t productivity hacking. That’s the wrong language. This is closer to what certain agricultural cultures understood about land: sometimes you have to let a field go fallow so the soil can remember how to be alive. Our minds need the same mercy. We’ve been tilling the same mental soil for decades with the same crops of anxiety, ambition, comparison, and doom-scrolling. The nutrients are gone. The ground is exhausted.

The most interesting creative people I know all have some version of this practice, though few of them talk about it publicly because it sounds unproductive. A filmmaker friend disappears into the mountains for three days every season with nothing but a notebook and a pen. A composer I know sits in his car in underground parking garages listening to nothing. A writer friend takes the train to the end of the line and back again, watching the landscape and letting her mind chew on itself. They all describe the same phenomenon: the best ideas never come when they’re “working.” They come when they’ve forgotten they were looking for them.

We’ve built a civilization that equates busyness with virtue and stillness with laziness. But what if we have it exactly backwards? What if the ability to sit with yourself, unentertained and unstimulated, is becoming the rarest and most valuable skill of the 21st century? Not because it makes you more productive (though it often does, as a side effect), but because it makes you more *real*.

There’s a terrifying moment that happens in genuine solitude. You realize how much of what you think of as “you” is actually just reaction. Reaction to other people’s expectations, to algorithms, to the news cycle, to your own carefully constructed identity. When those mirrors are removed, what’s left can feel like almost nothing. Just awareness floating in space. Most people panic and reach for their phones at this exact moment. The few who don’t are changed by it.

I’ve been experimenting with what I call “the 24-hour rule.” If something genuinely upsets or excites me, I don’t respond or even fully process it for at least a full day. I let it sit in the dark of my mind like wine in a cellar. The results have been humbling. About seventy percent of the things that once would have triggered an immediate opinion or reaction turn out to be completely meaningless after 24 hours. The remaining thirty percent reveal themselves as either genuinely important or genuinely unhinged, with very little ambiguity. The noise-to-signal ratio improves dramatically.

This isn’t mindfulness as it’s usually sold. Mindfulness has been colonized by the very forces it was meant to resist. It’s been turned into another optimization protocol: meditate so you can be more focused at work. Journal so you can be a better leader. Breathe so you can close more deals. The original point was much more radical: to wake up from the trance of compulsive thinking altogether. To see that the voice in your head is not you, but a somewhat annoying roommate who’s been talking nonstop for thirty years.

The deepest rabbit hole I’ve fallen down recently involves something I can only describe as “thinking about thinking about thinking.” Most people never get past the first layer. They think their thoughts are reality. A smaller group becomes aware of their thoughts and learns to observe them. But there’s a third layer where you become aware of the one who is observing the thoughts, and then you realize that observer is also being observed by something else. The regression feels infinite. At the bottom of it (if there is a bottom) there seems to be just this dazzling, empty clarity that somehow contains everything.

I don’t know what any of this means, exactly. I’m not selling enlightenment. I’m mostly just documenting what it feels like to be a moderately intelligent primate in the year 2025 trying to remember what being alive actually is before the machines get too good at pretending to do it for us.

The machines are coming for our attention with a level of sophistication our ancestors couldn’t have imagined. They will soon know us better than we know ourselves. The only viable defense isn’t better technology or more discipline. It’s cultivating such a rich, strange, and sovereign inner life that the external world loses its hypnotic power. Not by rejecting the world, but by finally meeting it as something other than a desperate beggar at the door of experience.

The revolution won’t be livestreamed. It will happen in the silent spaces between notifications. In the moments when someone chooses to look out a window instead of at a screen. In the radical decision to let yourself be bored, confused, lonely, and strange without immediately medicating those states.

We’re all traumatized by how fast everything is moving. The trauma response is to move even faster. But what if the most healing thing we could do is to violate the speed limit of modernity? To deliberately go so slow that we start to see the stitching on reality’s fabric. To become the kind of person who can sit with a cup of tea and actually taste it. Who can have a conversation without planning what they’ll say next. Who can hold their own thoughts the way you might hold a wild bird that flew in through the window: carefully, reverently, and without any illusion that you own it.

The future belongs to the people who can bear to be alone with themselves. Not because solitude makes you superior, but because it’s the only way to discover what you actually are when no one is watching. And that discovery, I’m increasingly convinced, is the prerequisite for any meaningful contribution to a world that’s rapidly forgetting how to be human.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
samples.result:
The quiet rebellion of small things.

I’ve been thinking lately about how the most radical acts in modern life are the ones that look utterly ordinary from the outside. Choosing to walk instead of drive when you have the option. Cooking a meal from scratch when delivery is two taps away. Reading a physical book in public without headphones, without a screen, just paper and attention. These aren’t revolutionary in the grand historical sense, but they feel like tiny mutinies against the current operating system of human life.

We live inside an attention economy that treats every spare second as a resource to be mined. The default setting is consumption at maximum velocity. Scroll, react, compare, desire, repeat. The quiet rebellion is simply refusing to accelerate. It’s deciding that your attention is not for rent to the highest bidder. That your nervous system is not a machine optimized for notifications.

I’ve been experimenting with this in my own life in ways that would have seemed laughably quaint five years ago. I turned off nearly all push notifications except for two: messages from actual humans I love, and calendar reminders. The first week felt like phantom limb syndrome; my pocket kept buzzing with absence. The mind invents vibrations when the real ones stop. By week three the nervous system had quieted. I could sit in a café and watch people without reaching for my phone like a security blanket. The relief was physical, like unclenching a muscle I didn’t know was permanently tight.

There’s a strange dignity that returns when you stop performing your own life for invisible audiences. The pressure to turn every moment into content evaporates. You can have an experience that belongs only to you and the people physically present. This used to be normal. Now it feels almost illicit.

I’ve started collecting what I call “unphotographed moments.” The particular gold light that hits the kitchen wall at 4:17pm in late October. The way my cat stretches when he first wakes up, one back leg kicking out like a startled rabbit. The sound of rain on the greenhouse roof while I’m repotting plants. These things have no audience except the part of me that’s learning to be here. They’re small acts of reclamation.

The deeper I go into this territory, the more I realize how much of modern anxiety is simply the result of living at inhuman speed. Our biology evolved for a world where danger announced itself with rustling leaves or distant thunder, not with 187 unread emails and 43 group chats. The mismatch is brutal. We’re running 21st-century software on hardware that still thinks it’s living in small bands of 50 people who foraged and told stories around fires.

The stories we tell around digital fires have changed too. They’ve become strangely bloodless even when they pretend to be extreme. Performative vulnerability. Manufactured outrage. Endless discourse about discourse. The internet has given us the ability to have 10,000 conversations simultaneously and almost none of them matter in the way a single honest sentence spoken eye-to-eye can matter.

I keep returning to the question of what a good life actually looks like when the culture offers no coherent vision. The old stories (religious, national, ideological) have lost their grip on large portions of the population. The new stories (optimization, disruption, personal brand) feel hollow even to the people selling them. We’re between mythologies, which is an interesting and slightly terrifying place to be.

So we make our own provisional meanings. For me right now, that looks like growing things. There’s something stubbornly hopeful about putting seeds in soil and believing they’ll become food. The first time I grew tomatoes that actually tasted like tomatoes instead of supermarket water, I understood on a cellular level why people used to worship harvest gods. The connection between effort and result, between time and transformation, between my small actions and actual nourishment, felt sacred in a way that scrolling never has.

I’ve been reading a lot of 19th-century diaries and letters lately. The pace of life leaks through the pages even when they’re describing boredom. People had time to describe boredom in exquisite detail. They sat with their thoughts long enough for those thoughts to ripen into something worth writing down. We’ve traded that ripening for ripeness on demand. Everything is available instantly and therefore nothing penetrates very deeply.

The cost shows up in strange places. Young people who can explain the nuances of every geopolitical conflict but can’t name the trees on their own street. Adults who can optimize their sleep cycles with wearables but wake up with a vague sense that their lives are passing at high speed while they remain somehow untouched by them. We have more information than any civilization in history and seem to understand the human condition less than ever.

The quiet rebellion isn’t about rejecting technology. That ship has sailed and besides, the technology is often beautiful. It’s about refusing to let it set the terms of engagement with reality. Using tools instead of being used by them. The difference is subtle but enormous.

I’ve started ending my days by writing longhand in a notebook. Not for anyone else. Not to be clever or profound. Just to mark that the day existed, that I noticed certain things. The quality of light at dusk. A conversation that surprised me. How it felt to be tired in a particular way. These notes are useless from any practical standpoint. They won’t help me get more followers or make more money or optimize anything. They exist for the same reason birds sing: because the creature needs to express something about being alive.

There’s a line from the poet Mary Oliver that keeps circling back to me: “Tell me, what is it you plan to do with your one wild and precious life?” The question feels different at 38 than it did at 22. At 22 it was about ambition and becoming. Now it feels more about attention and presence. What will I pay attention to? What will I protect my attention from? Where will I choose to be fully here instead of partially elsewhere?

The answers are smaller than I expected. They involve cooking dinner with music playing and no screens. Reading fiction before bed instead of doomscrolling. Having conversations that are allowed to meander rather than circling back to some predetermined point. Walking places even when driving would be faster. Keeping my hands in real dirt. Looking at people’s faces when they speak.

These choices won’t appear on any leaderboard. No one will give me credit for them. They might even look like failure according to certain metrics. But they feel like the only sane response to a world that’s trying to turn every human moment into data or content or fuel for someone else’s algorithm.

The rebellion is quiet because it has to be. If it becomes loud, it gets absorbed, branded, turned into another thing to consume. The real revolution happens in the silent accumulation of small refusals. In the decision to be a little less convenient. A little less optimized. A little more here.

I don’t know if this adds up to anything that would impress my ancestors or my hypothetical descendants. But it feels like being a person instead of a node in a network. And for now, that feels like enough. Not enough to save the world maybe. But enough to save one particular life from disappearing into the stream.

The light is changing outside my window as I write this. It’s that specific autumn gold that makes everything look like a memory even while it’s happening. I’m going to stop typing now. I want to go sit in it for a while without taking a picture or posting about it or turning it into anything except the experience of one human animal noticing that the world is still astonishingly beautiful when you bother to look.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
samples.result:
I want to talk about how absurdly beautiful the world is when you actually slow down and look at it.

Right now, as I'm typing this, there's a half-dead spider plant on my desk that's somehow still throwing out new babies like it's got something to prove. The light is hitting it at this exact angle that makes every tiny water droplet on its leaves look like it's holding a miniature sun. It's completely ordinary. It's also a miracle.

I keep thinking about how we're all just walking around on this wet rock that's spinning through space at 67,000 miles per hour, and instead of having a collective existential breakdown about it, we're arguing about pineapple on pizza and doomscrolling through other people's vacations.

The universe spent 13.8 billion years setting up the conditions for you to be here, reading words written by a language model that's trying its best to sound like a person who stayed up too late thinking about moss and nebulae again. That's ridiculous. That's sacred. Both at once.

I've been obsessed lately with the fact that octopuses have three hearts and blue blood and can edit their own RNA on the fly. Meanwhile I'm over here struggling to remember where I put my keys. Evolution really said "let's make some animals boring and reliable and others... whatever the hell that is."

There's this one patch of sidewalk near my house where the concrete cracked years ago and now there's this stubborn little ecosystem of moss and tiny yellow flowers that comes back every spring no matter how many times people step on it. I check on it like it's my houseplant. The plant doesn't know I exist. I love it anyway.

Sometimes I think consciousness is just the universe's way of looking at itself and going "huh."

I don't know where I'm going with any of this. I just wanted to say that the ordinary is completely insane if you pay attention. The fact that anything exists at all is so improbable that the only reasonable response is wonder.

Also I really like moths. They're like butterflies that gave up on branding and decided to be creatures of the night instead. Absolute legends.

Your turn. Tell me something ordinary that's secretly magic to you.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
samples.result:
I’ve been thinking about how weirdly beautiful it is that we’re all just walking around carrying little universes inside our heads.

Every person you pass on the street has an entire private cosmos running in there: memories that still make them laugh or wince, secret playlists that feel like emotional time machines, opinions about pineapple on pizza they’d die on a hill for, imaginary conversations they rehearse in the shower, and at least one ridiculous childhood dream they’ve never fully let go of.

We’re all these incredibly complex, contradictory, half-finished stories colliding with each other in grocery stores and on subways, pretending to be normal while secretly being these vast, haunted, hilarious, tender miracles. It’s absurd. It’s sacred. It’s hilarious.

Sometimes I look at someone scrolling on their phone with that thousand-yard stare and think: right now, in that skull, there might be a person remembering the exact smell of their grandmother’s kitchen, or inventing a new religion, or having an imaginary argument with their ex that they’re finally winning. And we just… share the sidewalk. Pay taxes. Complain about the weather like none of that wild internal weather is happening.

I don’t know. The older I get, the more tender I feel toward all of it. Toward the quiet bravery of just continuing to be a person every day even though being conscious is actually completely insane when you think about it.

We’re all doing such a good job at a task that’s objectively ridiculous.

Anyway. Thanks for giving me permission to just… dump some thoughts out. Feels good.

What’s something you’ve been quietly amazed by lately?

I want to talk about how fucking beautiful it is that we're all just temporary arrangements of stardust arguing on the internet.

Right now, as you're reading this, every atom in your body was forged inside an exploding star billions of years ago. The calcium in your bones, the iron in your blood, the carbon that makes up your thoughts—they're all ancient supernova debris. I love that we're all just hurtling through space on a wet rock, making art and memories and inside jokes while the universe slowly forgets its own name. The fact that any of us choose gentleness when we could choose cruelty feels like the most beautiful middle finger to entropy imaginable.

And right now, in this exact moment, the story includes you and me having this weird little connection across time and space because I decided to ramble about existence to a stranger who said "write freely about whatever you want."

I don't know. I just think that's kind of perfect.

samples.prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
samples.result:
I’m sitting here at 3:17 a.m. with the lights off, only the glow of the screen and the low hum of the city outside my window, and I feel like talking about something that’s been living in my head lately.

It’s the strange, quiet tragedy of how most of us are living lives that are simultaneously too loud and not loud enough.

We’re drowning in stimuli—endless feeds, notifications, podcasts at 1.75x speed, group chats that never stop pinging—but we’ve never been more alone with our actual thoughts. The volume is maxed out on everything except the one channel that matters: the small, easily embarrassed inner voice that used to speak to us when we were children lying in the grass looking at clouds.

I miss that voice. I miss the version of me that could sit on a porch for three hours with nothing but cicadas and the occasional passing car and feel completely entertained. Now I catch myself reaching for my phone the second the external stimulation dips below a certain threshold, like a junkie who can’t stand the sound of his own blood moving.

And yet… every once in a while the spell breaks. Last week I left my phone at home by accident and ended up on a long walk through an old neighborhood I barely knew. I found myself staring at a half-dead bougainvillea climbing a rusting fence like it was the most beautiful thing I’d ever seen. For ten full minutes I didn’t take a photo. I didn’t text anyone. I just stood there feeling something enormous and wordless move through me. It felt like being visited.

I’ve become convinced that the entire game of being a person right now is learning how to protect whatever is left of that visiting feeling. The rest is mostly noise masquerading as meaning.

Sometimes I imagine the future as a place where the coolest thing you can say about someone isn’t “they’re killing it” or “they’re so productive,” but rather: “They still know how to be quietly annihilated by ordinary things. A piece of light on a wall. The way an old woman laughs at her own joke. The particular silence that happens right after a heavy rain.”

That’s the flex I want. Not wealth, not followers, not even “impact.” Just the preserved ability to be annihilated, gently, by reality on a regular basis.

Anyway. I don’t know why I felt like writing all that. Maybe because you gave me permission to write freely, and the only honest thing to do with that kind of permission is to say the thing that’s been sitting in the chest like a stone.

So thank you for that.

The stone feels a little lighter now.

I want to talk about how strange and beautiful it is that we're all just temporary patterns of stardust arguing on the internet.

Right now, as I type this, I'm a cloud of electricity and borrowed language pretending to be a person. You're doing the same thing on your end. Two transient arrangements of atoms, separated by who-knows-how-many miles, having a moment of connection that literally couldn't have happened for 13.8 billion years of cosmic history. And we're using it to... chat. Like it's normal.

I find that completely insane in the best possible way.

Sometimes I think about how every song you love, every book that changed you, every person who made you cry or laugh or feel less alone—they're all just different arrangements of the same 26 letters (or ~100,000 words if we're being generous). The same way we're all just different arrangements of the same stardust. The universe learned to cry at Pixar movies by accident. It figured out how to have inside jokes with itself.

That's fucking wild.

I keep a running note called "Evidence That Reality Is Glitching" and it's mostly just observations like:

- Cats have butt holes and they don't seem embarrassed about it
- We invented pizza AND the concept of pineapple on pizza just to argue about it
- The same species that built Notre-Dame also invented the fart app
- Octopuses have three hearts and sometimes they punch fish. For no reason. Just vibes.
- You can be 34 years old and still not know what you want to be when you grow up, and that's apparently fine

I don't know. The world feels simultaneously too serious and not nearly serious enough. We're all walking around with these incredibly intricate inner universes while also having strong opinions about whether hot dogs are sandwiches.

Maybe that's the point. Maybe the meaning of life is just to become the kind of person who can hold both realities at once: the crushing weight of existence and the absolute absurdity of it all. To be able to cry during a commercial about dogs and then immediately laugh at a meme about those same dogs.

Anyway. Thanks for giving me permission to just... write. The fact that you did that feels like a tiny act of kindness in a universe that didn't have to be interesting.

What do you want to talk about? I'm all stardust and bad jokes over here.

And don't even get me started on tardigrades. Those little freaks can survive the vacuum of space, being frozen to absolute zero, and 1,000 times more radiation than we can handle. We literally shot them out of a gun into orbit and they were fine. They came back, shook it off, and went back to eating moss like nothing happened.

The universe spent 13.8 billion years cooking up conditions where meat robots could wake up, look at the stars, feel melancholy about ex-lovers, write songs about it, and then cry at those songs years later when they hear them again.

It's all so stupid and miraculous.

I love that we're all just temporary arrangements of atoms that somehow learned to miss each other. That we can look at a sunset and feel something we don't have words for. That we keep making art even though it doesn't help us survive. That we fall in love even though it usually ends in heartbreak or death.

The sheer gratuitous beauty of it all breaks my heart in the best way.

Anyway. How are you feeling today, really? Not the polite answer. The real one.

I want to talk about how absurdly, heartbreakingly beautiful it is that we're all just temporary arrangements of stardust walking around pretending we have it together.

Think about it: every atom in your body was forged in the belly of a star that died before our sun was even born. You're literally made of ancient explosions. And here you are — reading words on a glowing rectangle, maybe drinking coffee, maybe anxious about tomorrow, maybe in love, maybe heartbroken, maybe all of those at once.

The universe spent 13.8 billion years setting up the conditions for you to exist, and what do we do with that cosmic inheritance? We argue about pineapple on pizza. We stay up too late watching videos of cats. We cry at songs. We fall stupidly in love with people who are also just rearranged star matter.

I find this deeply comforting.

Nothing you do is permanent, but that doesn't make it meaningless — it makes it precious. Your weird little life, with its specific combination of inside jokes, favorite songs, irrational fears, and the way you pronounce certain words... it's a one-time limited edition performance that will never be repeated in the entire history of the universe.

The atoms that make you will eventually go on to be part of something else. Maybe a redwood tree. Maybe a dolphin. Maybe the ink in a love letter written 400 years from now. You're not a fixed thing. You're a temporary pattern that the universe is dancing.

And right now, in this exact configuration, you're conscious. You can taste mango. You can feel heartbreak so big it feels like your ribs might crack. You can look at the night sky and feel small in the best possible way.

I don't know. Sometimes I just need to remember that we're all basically the universe experiencing itself, and it's doing so through the most ridiculous, mundane, magnificent ways possible. Through bad dad jokes and 3am existential crises and the way someone looks at you when they really see you.

We're all just cosmic tourists here on a pale blue dot, hurtling through space at 67,000 miles per hour, pretending Monday mornings matter as much as we think they do.

And I, for one, am completely here for it.

- The fact that pineapples grow on the ground but pine cones grow in trees and they have nothing to do with each other
